title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
Preface
Welcome to Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R — a comprehensive, hands-on guide to understanding and modeling drug behavior using the R programming language. This book takes you from the fundamental concepts of how drugs move through and affect the body, all the way to advanced population modeling, Bayesian PK/PD, and physiologically based pharmacokinetics.
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
About This Book
Pharmacokinetics (what the body does to the drug) and pharmacodynamics (what the drug does to the body) form the scientific foundation of drug development and clinical therapeutics. Modern pharmacometrics — the science of quantitative pharmacology — relies heavily on computational modeling to integrate PK and PD data, predict drug behavior, optimize dosing, and support regulatory decisions.
R has become one of the most widely used platforms for PK/PD data analysis and modeling. With a rich ecosystem of specialized packages for noncompartmental analysis, compartmental modeling, population PK, and simulation, R provides a complete, reproducible, and accessible workflow.
This book bridges the gap between PK/PD theory and practical implementation. Every chapter combines pharmacological concepts with executable R code, real or simulated datasets, and interpretive guidance. You learn by doing — writing scripts, fitting models, visualizing results, and solving problems.
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
Who This Book Is For
This book is designed for:

Graduate students in pharmacology, pharmaceutical sciences, or related fields who want to learn PK/PD modeling
Clinical pharmacologists and pharmacometricians seeking a practical R-based workflow
Pharmaceutical scientists involved in drug development and translational research
Regulatory scientists who need to understand and evaluate PK/PD analyses
Data scientists transitioning into the pharmaceutical domain
Anyone interested in quantitative pharmacology and model-informed drug development
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
Prerequisites
Readers should have:

Basic familiarity with R programming (data frames, basic plotting, functions)
Some exposure to calculus concepts (derivatives, integrals — we review what you need)
Introductory biology and physiology knowledge
No prior PK/PD or pharmacometrics experience is required — we start from first principles
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
What You’ll Learn
By the end of this book, you will be able to:

Understand the core concepts of pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics
Perform noncompartmental analysis (NCA) and interpret PK parameters
Build, simulate, and fit one- and multi-compartment PK models in R
Model pharmacodynamic dose-response relationships (Emax, sigmoid Emax)
Link PK and PD using effect compartment and indirect response models
Handle nonlinear and saturable pharmacokinetics (Michaelis–Menten)
Conduct population PK analysis with mixed-effects models
Diagnose and validate PK/PD models with goodness-of-fit plots and VPCs
Simulate dosing regimens and compute probability of target attainment
Analyze exposure-response relationships for efficacy and safety
Apply physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) modeling concepts
Use Bayesian methods for PK/PD parameter estimation
Perform sensitivity analysis to triage parameters and guide model simplification
Build reproducible, regulatory-ready reports with Quarto
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
Book Structure
The book is organized into five thematic parts following the natural learning arc of PK/PD:

Part I — Foundations (Chapters 1–4): What PK/PD is, core biology and drug action concepts, essential mathematics, and setting up your R environment for PK/PD work.
Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis (Chapters 5–8): Noncompartmental analysis, one-compartment models, multi-compartment models, and understanding absorption and bioavailability across routes of administration.
Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage (Chapters 9–11): Dose-response modeling, linking concentration to effect through PK/PD models, and handling nonlinear and saturable pharmacokinetics.
Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application (Chapters 12–16): Population PK with mixed effects, model diagnostics and validation, simulation and dose optimization, exposure-response analysis, and special populations.
Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice (Chapters 17–21): PBPK modeling, Bayesian PK/PD, comprehensive case studies, best practices for reporting and reproducibility, and sensitivity analysis.
crumbs: Preface

title: Master Pharmacokinetics and Pharmacodynamics (PKPD) with R
text:
Core Mental Models
Before diving into the chapters, here are eight recurring patterns that unify PK/PD modeling. Keep these in mind as you read — they are the “big picture” that connects seemingly disparate topics.

The Compartmental Ladder (Ch 5–8, 17): Drug disposition is modeled as ODEs over compartments — from one-compartment C(t) = (Dose/V)e^{-k_e t} to PBPK with organ-level blood flows. Every model in this book reuses this ODE substrate.
Parameters Become State-Dependent (Ch 11, 16): The recurring “upgrade” is taking a fixed parameter (CL) and making it a function of system state — Michaelis-Menten (CL depends on C), enzyme induction (CL depends on E(t)), or DDI (CL depends on a second drug). Each upgrade switches from analytical to numerical (deSolve) solutions.
Stacked Hill Equations (Ch 9–10): PK drives PD via C(t) \rightarrow effect through Emax/Hill: E = E_{max}C^n/(EC_{50}^n + C^n). When effect lags, insert an effect compartment. The whole chain derives from receptor occupancy \theta = [L]/(K_d + [L]).
Coupling State Variables (Ch 10, 16): Once you have C(t) and E(t) as ODE states, couple in disease progression D(t), enzyme level, or a second drug. The pattern is always the same — each new derivative = function of existing states, solved with one ode() call. The cost: parameter count — identifiability is the central risk.
Population Variability = Fixed × Random (Ch 12): \theta_i = \theta_{pop} \cdot e^{\eta_i}, \eta_i \sim N(0,\omega^2). Covariates explain part of \eta_i via allometric scaling (\beta \approx 0.75 for CL, \approx 1.0 for V).
The Estimation Ladder (Ch 13, 18): OLS (linearize) → WLS (handle heteroscedasticity) → NLS (fit directly, Levenberg-Marquardt) → MLE (get \sigma + asymptotics) → Bayesian (priors + full posterior). Each rung costs more computation but reduces bias or adds information.
Sensitivity Analysis Brackets Every Model (Ch 21): Local sensitivity (S_p, “a flashlight”) is fast. Global sensitivity (Sobol/PRCC, “daylight”) surveys the full parameter space. Run sensitivity before estimation to triage which parameters to fit vs fix.
IVIVE/PBPK as Integration (Ch 17): CL_{int} = V_{max}/K_m \rightarrow well-stirred model \rightarrow flow-limited vs capacity-limited regime check \rightarrow feed into PBPK ODEs. Know your regime — it determines which errors matter.

1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters

title: 1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters
text:
1.2 What Is Pharmacokinetics?
Pharmacokinetics (PK) answers the question: What does the body do to the drug?
It describes the time course of drug concentration in the body — how the drug is absorbed, distributed, metabolized, and excreted (ADME). Pharmacokinetics quantifies:

Absorption: How the drug enters systemic circulation from the site of administration
Distribution: How the drug spreads throughout tissues and organs
Metabolism: How enzymes (primarily in the liver) chemically modify the drug
Excretion: How the drug and its metabolites are eliminated (kidneys, bile, lungs)



Code
# A simple conceptual PK simulation
# Visualizing concentration over time after an IV bolus
library(ggplot2)

time <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
dose <- 100  # mg
Vd   <- 10   # L (volume of distribution)
ke   <- 0.3  # 1/h (elimination rate constant)

conc <- (dose / Vd) * exp(-ke * time)

ggplot(data.frame(time, conc), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Simulated Drug Concentration After IV Bolus",
       subtitle = "One-compartment model with first-order elimination",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters

title: 1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters
text:
1.3 What Is Pharmacodynamics?
Pharmacodynamics (PD) answers the question: What does the drug do to the body?
It describes the relationship between drug concentration at the site of action and the resulting pharmacological effect — both therapeutic and toxic. Pharmacodynamics involves:

Receptor binding: Drug molecules interacting with cellular targets
Signal transduction: How receptor activation leads to cellular response
Dose-response: The relationship between dose (or concentration) and effect magnitude
Potency and efficacy: How much drug is needed and what maximum effect it can produce
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters

title: 1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters
text:
1.4 What Is Pharmacometrics?
Pharmacometrics is the science of quantitative pharmacology — the integration of PK, PD, and disease progression using mathematical and statistical models. It is the engine behind:

Model-informed drug development (MIDD)
Optimal dose selection
Clinical trial simulation
Regulatory decision support

Pharmacometrics uses R extensively, making it a core skill for modern pharmaceutical scientists.

1.4.1 The ADME Framework








Process
Definition
Key Parameter




Absorption
Drug enters bloodstream
k_a, F (bioavailability)


Distribution
Drug spreads through body
V_d (volume of distribution)


Metabolism
Drug chemically modified
CL_{int} (intrinsic clearance)


Excretion
Drug removed from body
CL (clearance), k_e

title: 1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters
text:
1.6 Why Use R for PK/PD?
R has become a leading platform for PK/PD analysis because:

Comprehensive PK packages: PK, PKNCA, nlmixr2, mrgsolve, rxode2 — purpose-built for pharmacometrics
Reproducibility: Scripts, Quarto, and R Markdown ensure every analysis is traceable
Statistical power: Mixed-effects models (nlme, lme4, saemix) for population PK
Visualization: ggplot2 for publication-quality PK/PD plots
Open source: No licensing costs, community-driven development
Regulatory acceptance: FDA and EMA accept R-based analyses



Code
# Key R packages for PK/PD (we'll use these throughout the book)
pkpd_packages <- data.frame(
  Package = c("PK", "PKNCA", "nlmixr2", "rxode2", "mrgsolve",
              "nlme", "saemix", "ggplot2", "dplyr", "shiny"),
  Purpose = c("Basic PK analysis", "Noncompartmental analysis",
              "Population PK (FOCEi/SAEM)", "ODE-based PK/PD simulation",
              "PK/PD simulation engine", "Nonlinear mixed effects",
              "SAEM estimation", "Visualization", "Data manipulation",
              "Interactive apps")
)
knitr::kable(pkpd_packages, caption = "Core R packages for PK/PD analysis")

title: 1  What PK/PD Is and Why It Matters
text:
1.7 Overview of the Book
This book is structured in five parts:

Part I (Ch 1–4): Foundations — concepts, biology, math, and R setup
Part II (Ch 5–8): Classical PK — from noncompartmental analysis to multi-compartment models
Part III (Ch 9–11): Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD linkage
Part IV (Ch 12–16): Population modeling, simulation, and clinical applications
Part V (Ch 17–20): Advanced methods and case studies


1.7.1 Exercises

Define pharmacokinetics and pharmacodynamics in your own words.
For each of the following, classify as PK or PD:

A drug has a half-life of 12 hours
50% of patients respond at a dose of 10 mg
The drug is 95% protein-bound
The maximum effect plateaus at 80% inhibition

List three advantages of using R for PK/PD analysis compared to commercial software.
Explain the difference between potency and efficacy.



1.7.2 Common Mistakes

Confusing PK with PD: PK = concentration over time (body → drug); PD = effect vs concentration (drug → body)
Thinking models are reality: All models are simplifications. A model is useful if it answers the question at hand
Ignoring units: Always check that your units are consistent. Mixing mg, μg, and ng is a frequent source of error
Skipping exploratory plots: Always plot your concentration-time and dose-response data before modeling



1.7.3 Key Takeaway
Pharmacokinetics describes drug movement (ADME); pharmacodynamics describes drug effect. Together they form the basis for model-informed drug development. R provides a powerful, reproducible environment for PK/PD analysis at every level — from exploratory plots to regulatory submissions.

2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action

title: 2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action
section: 2.2 Anatomy and Physiology for Drug Disposition
text:
2.2 Anatomy and Physiology for Drug Disposition

2.2.1 The Circulatory System and Drug Distribution
Drugs enter the bloodstream and are carried throughout the body. The cardiovascular system is the primary distribution network:


Code
library(ggplot2)

# Conceptual diagram of drug distribution compartments
# Blood flow distribution to major organs
organ_flow <- data.frame(
  Organ = c("Liver", "Kidneys", "Brain", "Heart", "Muscle", "Skin", "Adipose", "Other"),
  BloodFlow_percent = c(25, 20, 15, 4, 15, 5, 5, 11)
)

ggplot(organ_flow, aes(x = reorder(Organ, -BloodFlow_percent), y = BloodFlow_percent)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Cardiac Output to Major Organs",
       x = "Organ/Tissue", y = "Blood Flow (%)") +
  theme_minimal()











2.2.2 Key Organs in Drug Disposition

Liver: Primary site of drug metabolism (CYP450 enzymes)
Kidneys: Primary route of excretion for water-soluble drugs and metabolites
Gastrointestinal tract: Site of oral drug absorption; also involved in metabolism (CYP3A4) and efflux transport (P-glycoprotein)
Lungs: Route for inhaled drugs; also involved in first-pass for IV drugs
Brain: Protected by the blood-brain barrier; requires specific drug properties for penetration
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action

title: 2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action
text:
2.3 Drug Transport Across Membranes

2.3.1 Mechanisms of Transport
Drugs cross biological membranes through several mechanisms:

Passive diffusion: Driven by concentration gradient; favors small, lipophilic, uncharged molecules
Facilitated diffusion: Carrier-mediated but not energy-dependent
Active transport: Energy-dependent; can work against concentration gradient (e.g., P-glycoprotein efflux)
Endocytosis/Pinocytosis: Cellular engulfment for large molecules



2.3.2 Factors Affecting Membrane Permeability
The ability of a drug to cross membranes depends on:

Lipophilicity (logP): Higher logP → better membrane penetration
Molecular size: Smaller molecules diffuse more easily
Ionization state (pKa): Only uncharged forms cross lipid membranes efficiently
Protein binding: Only free (unbound) drug can cross membranes



2.3.3 The pH-Partition Hypothesis
For weak acids and bases, the degree of ionization depends on the drug’s pKa and the local pH:
For a weak acid: \text{Fraction ionized} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pKa - pH)}}
For a weak base: \text{Fraction ionized} = \frac{1}{1 + 10^{(pH - pKa)}}


Code
# pH-partition: fraction ionized vs pH for weak acid (pKa = 4.5) and weak base (pKa = 8.5)
pH <- seq(0, 14, by = 0.1)
pKa_acid <- 4.5
pKa_base <- 8.5

f_ionized_acid <- 1 / (1 + 10^(pKa_acid - pH))
f_ionized_base <- 1 / (1 + 10^(pH - pKa_base))

df <- data.frame(
  pH = rep(pH, 2),
  Fraction_Ionized = c(f_ionized_acid, f_ionized_base),
  Drug = rep(c("Weak Acid (pKa=4.5)", "Weak Base (pKa=8.5)"), each = length(pH))
)

ggplot(df, aes(pH, Fraction_Ionized, color = Drug)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(1.5, 6.8, 7.4),
             linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = c(1.5, 6.8, 7.4), y = 0.95,
           label = c("Stomach\npH 1.5", "Intestine\npH 6.8", "Blood\npH 7.4"),
           size = 3, vjust = 1) +
  labs(title = "pH-Partition: Ionization vs pH",
       x = "pH", y = "Fraction Ionized") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action

title: 2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action
text:
2.4 Receptor Theory and Drug Action

2.4.1 The Receptor Concept
Most drugs exert their effects by binding to specific cellular macromolecules (receptors). The drug-receptor interaction follows the law of mass action:
D + R \rightleftharpoons DR \rightarrow \text{Effect}
where D is drug, R is receptor, and DR is the drug-receptor complex.


2.4.2 Occupancy Theory
The classical occupancy model assumes effect is proportional to receptor occupancy:
E = \frac{E_{max} \cdot [D]}{EC_{50} + [D]}
where: - E_{max} is the maximum possible effect - EC_{50} is the concentration producing 50% of E_{max} - [D] is drug concentration


Code
# Visualize the Emax model
conc <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
Emax <- 100
EC50 <- 10

effect <- (Emax * conc) / (EC50 + conc)

ggplot(data.frame(conc, effect), aes(conc, effect)) +
  geom_line(color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = EC50, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5 * Emax, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  annotate("text", x = EC50 * 1.5, y = 0.5 * Emax,
           label = paste0("EC50 = ", EC50), hjust = 0) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Emax Dose-Response Model",
       x = "Drug Concentration (log scale)", y = "Effect (% of maximum)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action

title: 2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action
text:
2.5 Key Pharmacological Concepts

2.5.1 Potency vs Efficacy








Property
Definition
Parameter




Potency
Amount of drug needed to produce a given effect
EC_{50} (lower = more potent)


Efficacy
Maximum effect a drug can produce
E_{max}


Therapeutic window
Range between minimum effective and minimum toxic concentration
Therapeutic index
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action

title: 2  Foundations in Biology and Drug Action
text:
2.6 Drug Properties That Influence PK/PD








Property
PK Impact
Example




Molecular weight
Affects membrane permeability and renal filtration
Large molecules > 500 Da less permeable


Lipophilicity (logP)
Higher logP → better absorption, distribution, but may slow renal excretion
CNS drugs typically logP 2–5


pKa
Determines ionization at physiological pH
Weak bases accumulate in acidic compartments


Protein binding
Only free drug is active and cleared
Warfarin: 99% bound


Solubility
Affects dissolution and absorption rate
BCS classification system




2.6.1 Exercises

Explain why a weak acid (pKa = 4.0) is better absorbed from the stomach (pH = 1.5) than from the intestine (pH = 6.8).
Draw the concentration-effect curve for two drugs with the same E_{max} but different EC_{50} values. Which is more potent?
How does high protein binding affect the volume of distribution?
List three physicochemical properties that favor good oral absorption.



2.6.2 Common Mistakes

Confusing potency with clinical superiority: A more potent drug is not necessarily better — efficacy and safety matter more
Ignoring active metabolites: Some drugs (prodrugs) are inactive until metabolized
Assuming linear PK without evidence: Many drugs show nonlinear behavior at therapeutic doses
Forgetting free drug principle: Only unbound drug interacts with receptors and is cleared



2.6.3 Key Takeaway
A drug’s journey through the body is governed by both its physicochemical properties (lipophilicity, pKa, size) and the body’s physiological systems (blood flow, transporters, enzymes). Understanding these fundamentals is essential before building quantitative PK/PD models.

3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD

title: 3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD
section: 3.2 Exponential Functions: The Heart of PK
text:
3.2 Exponential Functions: The Heart of PK

3.2.1 Why Exponentials?
Most PK processes follow first-order kinetics — the rate of change is proportional to the amount present. This leads naturally to exponential functions:
\frac{dC}{dt} = -k \cdot C \quad \Rightarrow \quad C(t) = C_0 \cdot e^{-kt}
where C is concentration, k is the rate constant, and t is time.


Code
library(ggplot2)

# Comparing different elimination rate constants
time <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
C0 <- 100

df <- data.frame(
  time = rep(time, 4),
  conc = c(C0 * exp(-0.1 * time),  # slow elimination
           C0 * exp(-0.3 * time),  # moderate
           C0 * exp(-0.7 * time),  # fast
           C0 * exp(-1.5 * time)), # very fast
  k = factor(rep(c("k=0.1", "k=0.3", "k=0.7", "k=1.5"), each = length(time)))
)

ggplot(df, aes(time, conc, color = k)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Exponential Decay: Effect of Rate Constant",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
       color = "Elimination Rate") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#2E8B7E", "#C8963E", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD

title: 3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD
text:
3.3 Logarithms and Half-Life

3.3.1 The Power of Log Transformation
Taking the natural log of the exponential decay equation gives a linear relationship:
\ln C(t) = \ln C_0 - k \cdot t
This means a plot of \ln C vs t is a straight line with slope -k. This is the basis for terminal slope estimation in PK.


Code
# Log-linear plot for half-life determination
time <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.5)
C0 <- 100
k <- 0.3
conc <- C0 * exp(-k * time)

df <- data.frame(time, conc, log_conc = log(conc))

# Find half-life from data
t_half <- log(2) / k

p1 <- ggplot(df, aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_point(color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B") +
  labs(title = "Linear Scale", x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(df, aes(time, log_conc)) +
  geom_point(color = "#C44536") +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "#C44536") +
  labs(title = "Log-Linear Scale",
       subtitle = paste0("Slope = -k = ", -k, ", Half-life = ", round(t_half, 2), " h"),
       x = "Time (h)", y = "ln(Concentration)") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2











3.3.2 Half-Life Formula
t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln(2)}{k}
This is one of the most important relationships in PK. The half-life tells you how long it takes for the drug concentration to fall by 50%.


Code
# How many half-lives to reach steady state or complete elimination?
n_halflives <- 1:7
pct_eliminated <- (1 - 0.5^n_halflives) * 100

data.frame(
  HalfLives = n_halflives,
  Pct_Eliminated = round(pct_eliminated, 1),
  Pct_Remaining = round(100 - pct_eliminated, 1)
)


  HalfLives Pct_Eliminated Pct_Remaining
1         1           50.0          50.0
2         2           75.0          25.0
3         3           87.5          12.5
4         4           93.8           6.2
5         5           96.9           3.1
6         6           98.4           1.6
7         7           99.2           0.8




3.3.3 Key Takeaway

After 3.3 half-lives: ~90% eliminated
After 5 half-lives: ~97% eliminated (commonly used for steady state)
After 7 half-lives: >99% eliminated
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD

title: 3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD
text:
3.4 Ordinary Differential Equations in PK

3.4.1 What Is a Differential Equation?
A differential equation describes how a quantity changes over time. In PK, we use ODEs to model how drug amounts change in compartments.
The simplest PK model (one-compartment IV bolus):
\frac{dA}{dt} = -k \cdot A
where A is the amount of drug. This says: “the rate of change of drug amount is proportional to the amount present.”


3.4.2 Solving ODEs in R


Code
# Solving ODEs with rxode2
library(rxode2)

# Define a one-compartment IV bolus model
mod1 <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Simulate
params <- c(ke = 0.3, V = 10)
inits  <- c(central = 100)  # 100 mg dose
times  <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
ev     <- et(times)

sim <- rxSolve(mod1, params, ev, inits = inits)

plot(sim$time, sim$conc, type = "l", col = "#0B6E6B", lwd = 2,
     xlab = "Time (h)", ylab = "Concentration (mg/L)",
     main = "ODE Solution: One-Compartment IV Bolus")











3.4.3 The Analytical vs Numerical Approach
For simple models, we can write the analytical solution directly: C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}. For complex models with nonlinearities or multiple compartments, we solve ODEs numerically. R handles both approaches.
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD

title: 3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD
text:
3.5 Units and Dimensional Analysis

3.5.1 Why Units Matter
In PK/PD, incorrect units are a leading source of errors. Always track your units:



Parameter
Common Unit
Alternative




Dose
mg
mg/kg


Concentration
mg/L
μg/mL, ng/mL


Volume of distribution
L
L/kg


Clearance
L/h
mL/min


Half-life
h
min


AUC
mg·h/L
μg·h/mL





Code
# Dimensional checking in R
check_units <- function(dose_mg, conc_mg_L, V_L) {
  # V = dose / C0
  computed_V <- dose_mg / conc_mg_L
  cat("Dose:", dose_mg, "mg\n")
  cat("C0:", conc_mg_L, "mg/L\n")
  cat("Computed V:", computed_V, "L\n")
  cat("Expected V:", V_L, "L\n")
  cat("Match:", ifelse(abs(computed_V - V_L) < 1e-6, "✓", "✗ MISMATCH - check units!\n"))
}

check_units(dose_mg = 500, conc_mg_L = 25, V_L = 20)


Dose: 500 mg
C0: 25 mg/L
Computed V: 20 L
Expected V: 20 L
Match: ✓
crumbs: Part I — Foundations: PK/PD Concepts, Biology, Math, and R
3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD

title: 3  Mathematics You Need for PK/PD
text:
3.6 Scaling Between Species: Allometry
Allometric scaling is used to extrapolate PK parameters between species based on body weight:
Y = a \cdot W^b
where Y is the PK parameter, W is body weight, and b is the allometric exponent.


Code
# Allometric scaling example
body_weights <- c(0.02, 0.25, 2.5, 10, 70)  # kg: mouse, rat, rabbit, dog, human
clearance <- 2.5 * body_weights^0.75         # allometric scaling for CL

df_scale <- data.frame(
  Species = c("Mouse", "Rat", "Rabbit", "Dog", "Human"),
  Weight_kg = body_weights,
  CL_L_h = round(clearance, 2)
)

ggplot(df_scale, aes(Weight_kg, CL_L_h, label = Species)) +
  geom_point(color = "#C44536", size = 3) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_text(vjust = -1, size = 3.5) +
  scale_x_log10() + scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Allometric Scaling of Clearance",
       subtitle = "CL = 2.5 × W^0.75",
       x = "Body Weight (kg, log scale)", y = "Clearance (L/h, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()











3.6.1 Exercises

A drug has a half-life of 4 hours. What is its elimination rate constant k?
After how many half-lives will 95% of a drug be eliminated?
Convert a clearance of 120 mL/min to L/h.
A drug follows C(t) = 50 \cdot e^{-0.2t}. Find C_0 and t_{1/2}.
Write the ODE for a one-compartment model with IV infusion at rate R.



3.6.2 Common Mistakes

Confusing rate constant k and half-life: k = \ln(2) / t_{1/2}, not 1 / t_{1/2}
Using log10 instead of ln for half-life: t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/k (natural log, not log10)
Mixing units: mg vs μg is a 1000× error
Assuming all processes are first-order: Some drugs show saturable (Michaelis–Menten) kinetics



3.6.3 Key Takeaway
PK mathematics is built on exponentials, logarithms, and differential equations. Master the relationship C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}, the log-linear transformation, and the half-life formula — these appear in every chapter that follows. Always check your units.

text:
4.2 Setting Up Your R Environment

4.2.1 Installing R and RStudio

Download R from cran.r-project.org
Download RStudio (or VS Code with the R extension).
The PK/PD Ecosystem: Unlike other fields, PK/PD in R relies on a specific set of high-performance packages.



Code
# Check and install core PK/PD packages if missing
core_pkgs <- c(
  "tidyverse", "PKNCA", "rxode2", "nlmixr2",
  "mrgsolve", "patchwork", "janitor", "here"
)
missing_pkgs <- core_pkgs[!core_pkgs %in% installed.packages()[, 1]]
if (length(missing_pkgs) > 0) {
  install.packages(missing_pkgs)
  message("Installed: ", paste(missing_pkgs, collapse = ", "))
} else {
  message("All core PK/PD packages are already installed.")
}




4.2.2 Recommended Project Structure
PKPD_Project/
├── data/            # Raw and processed data files
├── R/               # Custom R functions
├── scripts/         # Analysis scripts
├── output/          # Generated tables and figures
├── reports/         # Quarto/R Markdown reports
├── renv/            # Package version management (optional)
└── PKPD_Project.Rproj

text:
4.3 Importing PK/PD Data

4.3.1 Common Data Formats in PK/PD
PK data typically has a “long” structure with columns for subject ID, time, concentration, and dose information.


Code
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate a typical PK dataset
set.seed(123)
n_subjects <- 6
times <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24)

pk_data <- expand.grid(
  ID = 1:n_subjects,
  TIME = times
) %>%
  mutate(
    DOSE = 100,
    CONC = (DOSE / 10) * exp(-0.3 * TIME) * rlnorm(n(), meanlog = 0, sdlog = 0.2),
    .by = ID
  )

# View the first few rows
head(pk_data, 12)


   ID TIME DOSE      CONC
1   1 0.00  100  8.939592
2   2 0.00  100  9.147245
3   3 0.00  100 11.505858
4   4 0.00  100 10.311499
5   5 0.00  100 11.171531
6   6 0.00  100  7.988183
7   1 0.25  100  8.860025
8   2 0.25  100 11.850872
9   3 0.25  100  8.440376
10  4 0.25  100  7.388744
11  5 0.25  100  9.163267
12  6 0.25  100  8.559212




4.3.2 Importing from Files


Code
# Import CSV
pk_data <- read_csv("data/pk_concentrations.csv")

# Import Excel
library(readxl)
pk_data <- read_excel("data/pk_data.xlsx", sheet = "Concentrations")

# Import NONMEM-style data
pk_data <- read_table("data/pk_data.txt")




4.3.3 Data Cleaning and Reshaping


Code
# Check for missing values
colSums(is.na(pk_data))


    ID   TIME   CONC   DOSE    AGE WEIGHT    SEX 
     0      0      0      0      0      0      0 


Code
# Convert columns to correct types
pk_data <- pk_data %>%
  mutate(
    ID   = as.factor(ID),
    TIME = as.numeric(TIME),
    CONC = as.numeric(CONC)
  )

# Reshape wide → long (if needed)
pk_wide <- pk_data %>%
  tidyr::pivot_wider(names_from = TIME, values_from = CONC,
                     names_prefix = "T_")

# Reshape long → wide
pk_long <- pk_wide %>%
  tidyr::pivot_longer(cols = starts_with("T_"),
                      names_to = "TIME",
                      values_to = "CONC",
                      names_prefix = "T_") %>%
  mutate(TIME = as.numeric(TIME))

4.4 Plotting Concentration-Time Profiles

4.4.1 Individual Profiles


Code
# Individual concentration-time plots
ggplot(pk_data, aes(TIME, CONC, group = ID, color = ID)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Individual Concentration-Time Profiles",
       subtitle = "IV Bolus, 100 mg dose",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
       color = "Subject") +
  theme_minimal()











4.4.2 Mean ± SD Profile


Code
# Summary statistics and mean ± SD plot
pk_summary <- pk_data %>%
  summarise(
    Mean = mean(CONC),
    SD   = sd(CONC),
    N    = n(),
    .by = TIME
  )

ggplot(pk_summary, aes(TIME, Mean)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = Mean - SD, ymax = Mean + SD),
              fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_point(color = "#0B6E6B", size = 2) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Mean Concentration-Time Profile (± SD)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()

Code
ggplot(pk_data, aes(TIME, CONC, group = ID, color = ID)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Semi-Log Concentration-Time Profiles",

4.5 Plotting Dose-Response Data


Code
# Simulate dose-response data
doses <- c(0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300)
n_per_dose <- 5

set.seed(456)
dr_data <- expand.grid(DOSE = doses, REP = 1:n_per_dose) %>%
  mutate(
    Emax = 100,
    EC50 = 10,

  scale_x_log10() +

       x = "Dose (mg, log scale)", y = "Effect (% of maximum)") +
  theme_minimal()

text:
4.6 Reproducible Scripts and Version Control

4.6.1 Best Practices for PK/PD Scripts

Start every script with a header describing purpose, author, and date
Load all packages at the top
Use relative paths (not absolute paths like C:/Users/...)
Comment your code — explain why, not what
Separate data import, analysis, and visualization
Use version control (Git) for all scripts



Code
# Example script header
# ============================================================
# Script: 01_nca_analysis.R
# Purpose: Noncompartmental analysis of Study PK-2024-001
# Author:  Your Name
# Date:    2024-06-01
# Packages: PKNCA, dplyr, ggplot2
# Input:   data/raw/study001_conc.csv
# Output:  output/nca_parameters.csv, output/figures/conc_time.pdf
# ============================================================

title: 4  R Fundamentals and Workflow for PK/PD
text:
4.8 Handling Missing Data in PK/PD
Missing concentration values are common in clinical PK studies. The appropriate handling strategy depends on why the data are missing.

4.8.1 The MCAR/MAR/MNAR Framework









Mechanism
Definition
Test/Clue
Action




MCAR (Missing Completely at Random)
Missingness unrelated to any variable
No pattern in missing vs observed
Mean/median imputation OK


MAR (Missing at Random)
Missingness explained by observed covariates
Pattern linked to recorded variables (e.g., more missing at later times)
Regression imputation, multiple imputation (mice), or MLE-based modeling


MNAR (Missing Not at Random)
Missingness depends on the unobserved value itself (e.g., samples below LLOQ)
Plausible mechanism: high CL patients drop out, BLQ samples
Selection/pattern-mixture models + sensitivity analysis; no method is unbiased





Code
# Example: Identify and handle missing data patterns
library(dplyr)

# Simulate PK data with different missingness types
set.seed(42)
pk_missing <- data.frame(
  ID = rep(1:10, each = 8),
  TIME = rep(c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24), 10),
  CONC = 25 * exp(-0.2 * rep(c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24), 10)) * 
          exp(rnorm(80, 0, 0.12))
)

# Introduce MCAR: random 10% missing
mcar_idx <- sample(1:80, 8)
pk_missing$CONC_MCAR <- pk_missing$CONC
pk_missing$CONC_MCAR[mcar_idx] <- NA

# Introduce MAR: more missing at later time points
mar_prob <- 0.1 * pk_missing$TIME / 24
mar_idx <- which(runif(80) < mar_prob)
pk_missing$CONC_MAR <- pk_missing$CONC
pk_missing$CONC_MAR[mar_idx] <- NA

# Summarize missingness
missing_summary <- pk_missing %>%
  summarise(
    MCAR_missing = sum(is.na(CONC_MCAR)),
    MAR_missing  = sum(is.na(CONC_MAR)),
    .by = TIME
  )

cat("Missing by time point:\n")


Missing by time point:


Code
print(missing_summary)


  TIME MCAR_missing MAR_missing
1  0.5            0           0
2  1.0            1           0
3  2.0            1           0
4  4.0            0           0
5  6.0            1           1
6  8.0            3           0
7 12.0            2           0
8 24.0            0           1


Code
# Simple imputation: last observation carried forward (LOCF)
pk_missing <- pk_missing %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  tidyr::fill(CONC_MCAR, CONC_MAR, .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup()




4.8.2 BLQ (Below Limit of Quantification) Handling
BLQ values are a special case of MNAR — the value is missing because it’s low. Common approaches:

LLOQ/2 substitution — set BLQ = LLOQ/2 (simple, common in NCA)
M3 method — likelihood-based approach that treats BLQ as left-censored observations
Imputation — multiple imputation with left-censoring constraint

For NCA, LLOQ/2 is standard. For population PK, likelihood-based methods (M3) are preferred as they correctly propagate uncertainty.

4  R Fundamentals and Workflow for PK/PD

title: 4  R Fundamentals and Workflow for PK/PD
text:
4.9 Beyond rxode2: Comparing Simulation Engines
While rxode2 is our workhorse for ODE-based PK modeling, R offers several complementary simulation frameworks. This section introduces mrgsolve, linpk, and nlme — each filling a distinct niche in the PK/PD toolkit.

4.9.1 mrgsolve: C++ Simulation for Clinical Trial Design
mrgsolve compiles ODE models to C++ for blazing-fast simulation. It excels at large-scale clinical trial simulations with complex dosing regimens.
Let’s simulate a two-compartment IV bolus in both rxode2 and mrgsolve to compare their syntax and output:


Code
library(rxode2)
library(mrgsolve)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# ---- Simulated patient parameters ----
params <- c(CL = 5, Vc = 50, Q = 4, Vp = 30)  # named numeric vector

# ---- rxode2 model ----
rx_model <- rxode2({
  d/dt(A1) <- - (CL/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vc) * A1 + (Q/Vp) * A2
  d/dt(A2) <-   (Q/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vp) * A2
  Cp <- A1 / Vc
})

rx_sim <- rx_model |>
  rxSolve(
    params  = params,
    events  = et(time = 0, amt = 500, cmt = 1) |> et(seq(0, 48, by = 0.5))
  ) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  dplyr::mutate(engine = "rxode2")

# ---- mrgsolve model ----
mrg_code <- "
$PARAM CL = 5, Vc = 50, Q = 4, Vp = 30
$CMT A1 A2
$ODE
dxdt_A1 = - (CL/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vc) * A1 + (Q/Vp) * A2;
dxdt_A2 =   (Q/Vc) * A1 - (Q/Vp) * A2;
$TABLE
double Cp = A1 / Vc;
$CAPTURE Cp
"

mrg_model <- mcode("twocmt", mrg_code)
mrg_sim <- mrg_model |>
  ev(amt = 500, cmt = 1, time = 0) |>
  mrgsim(end = 48, delta = 0.5) |>
  as.data.frame() |>
  mutate(engine = "mrgsolve")

# ---- Combine & Plot ----
bind_rows(rx_sim, mrg_sim) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = Cp, color = engine, linetype = engine)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("rxode2" = "#00A8B0", "mrgsolve" = "#E0533D")) +
  scale_linetype_manual(values = c("rxode2" = "solid", "mrgsolve" = "dashed")) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "rxode2 vs mrgsolve: 2-Compartment IV Bolus",
    subtitle = "Both engines converge to identical solutions",
    x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
    color = "Engine", linetype = "Engine"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 4.1: Two-compartment IV bolus simulated with rxode2 (solid teal) and mrgsolve (dashed coral). Both engines produce identical results, confirming model equivalence.













Feature
rxode2
mrgsolve




Syntax
R-like ODE in rxode2({...})
C++ code block $ODE ...


Compilation
C (via Rcpp)
C++ (via Rcpp)


Dosing
et() event table
ev() event object


Population sim
rxSolve() with parameter vectors
idata_set with individual parameters


Strengths
Seamless nlmixr2 integration
Fastest for large-scale trial simulations





4.9.2 linpk: Closed-Form PK Without ODEs
For linear PK systems, you often don’t need an ODE solver at all. linpk computes PK profiles analytically from primary parameters, making it ideal for rapid exploration.


Code
library(linpk)

# Single oral dose, 1-compartment
pk1 <- pkprofile(
  time = seq(0, 72, by = 0.25),
  dose = list(oral = data.frame(time = 0, amount = 100)),
  cl   = 5, vc = 80, ka = 0.8
)

as.data.frame(pk1) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = conc)) +
  geom_area(fill = "#00A8B0", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(color = "#00A8B0", linewidth = 1.0) +
  labs(
    title = "linpk: Closed-Form 1-CMT Oral Profile",
    subtitle = "No differential equation solver needed",
    x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 4.2: Multi-dose oral profile generated with linpk using closed-form equations. No ODE solver required.







4.9.3 nlme: Linear Mixed-Effects for PK Summary Statistics
Before diving into full population PK (Chapter 12), nlme provides a gentle introduction to mixed-effects modeling applied to PK endpoints like C_{max} and AUC:


Code
library(nlme)

# Simulate peak concentrations from 3 dose groups
set.seed(456)
pk_summary <- data.frame(
  subject = rep(1:30, each = 2),
  period  = rep(1:2, 30),
  dose    = rep(c(50, 100, 200), each = 20),
  cmax    = NA
)
pk_summary$cmax <- with(pk_summary,
  2.5 * dose^0.85 * exp(rnorm(nrow(pk_summary), 0, 0.2)) +
  rnorm(nrow(pk_summary), 0, 0.5)
)

fit <- lme(log(cmax) ~ log(dose), random = ~ 1 | subject,
           data = pk_summary)
summary(fit)


Linear mixed-effects model fit by REML
  Data: pk_summary 
        AIC      BIC  logLik
  -5.953581 2.288191 6.97679

Random effects:
 Formula: ~1 | subject
        (Intercept)  Residual
StdDev:  0.02105444 0.2008386

Fixed effects:  log(cmax) ~ log(dose) 
                Value  Std.Error DF  t-value p-value
(Intercept) 1.0461150 0.21488876 30  4.86817       0
log(dose)   0.8301846 0.04631407 28 17.92511       0
 Correlation: 
          (Intr)
log(dose) -0.993

Standardized Within-Group Residuals:
        Min          Q1         Med          Q3         Max 
-2.12086963 -0.66342228 -0.01970859  0.60944753  1.95146249 

Number of Observations: 60
Number of Groups: 30 


The power model \text{C}_{max} \propto \text{dose}^{\beta} estimates \beta \approx 0.85, confirming near-linear dose proportionality — a common regulatory question. We’ll revisit nlme for full PK curve fitting in Chapter 12.
:::


4.9.4 Exercises

Create a simulated PK dataset with 10 subjects, 8 time points, and 2 dose groups. Plot individual and mean profiles.
Import a CSV file of your choice and reshape it from wide to long format.
Create a semi-log plot with proper axis labels and provide a caption about what the linear terminal phase indicates.
Write a reusable R function that calculates k_e from two concentration-time points.
Set up a project directory structure following the recommended layout.



4.9.5 Common Mistakes

Using absolute file paths — scripts break when shared or moved
Not checking for BLOQ (Below Limit of Quantification) values — they need special handling
Plotting concentration on linear scale — always check the semi-log view
Forgetting to convert time units (hours → minutes, days → hours)
Overwriting raw data — always keep original files unchanged



4.9.6 Key Takeaway
A well-organized R environment with proper project structure, version control, and reproducible scripts is the foundation of reliable PK/PD analysis. Master data import, cleaning, and visualization in R before moving to modeling.

text:

crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.2 What Is Noncompartmental Analysis?
Noncompartmental analysis (NCA) estimates PK parameters directly from concentration-time data without assuming a specific compartmental model. It relies on:

The trapezoidal rule for AUC computation
Log-linear regression of the terminal phase for k_e and t_{1/2}
Moment analysis for mean residence time (MRT)

NCA is the most commonly used method in early-phase clinical trials and bioequivalence studies because it makes minimal assumptions.
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.3 Data Structure for NCA
A proper PK dataset for NCA requires:


Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(PKNCA)

# Example: Single subject, single dose, IV bolus
pk_iv <- data.frame(
  TIME = c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24),
  CONC = c(12.3, 9.8, 8.1, 6.4, 5.7, 4.9, 3.8, 3.0, 1.9, 1.2, 0.5, 0.1)
)

# Required: TIME and CONC columns
# Optional but recommended: ID (subject), DOSE, ROUTE, and grouping variables

ggplot(pk_iv, aes(TIME, CONC)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B") +
  labs(title = "Concentration-Time Profile for NCA",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.4 Computing AUC: The Trapezoidal Rule

5.4.1 Linear Trapezoidal Rule
The area under the curve between two time points is approximated as a trapezoid:
AUC_{t_1 \to t_2} = \frac{(C_1 + C_2)}{2} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)
Total AUC from 0 to last time point:
AUC_{0 \to t_{last}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} \frac{(C_i + C_{i+1})}{2} \cdot (t_{i+1} - t_i)


Code
# Manual AUC calculation using the trapezoidal rule
calc_auc_linear <- function(time, conc) {
  n <- length(time)
  auc <- 0
  for (i in 1:(n - 1)) {
    auc <- auc + (conc[i] + conc[i + 1]) / 2 * (time[i + 1] - time[i])
  }
  return(auc)
}

AUC_last <- calc_auc_linear(pk_iv$TIME, pk_iv$CONC)
cat("AUC(0 to last):", round(AUC_last, 3), "mg·h/L\n")


AUC(0 to last): 37.05 mg·h/L




5.4.2 Extrapolation to Infinity
AUC_{0 \to \infty} = AUC_{0 \to t_{last}} + \frac{C_{last}}{k_e}
where k_e is the terminal elimination rate constant.
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.5 Estimating the Terminal Elimination Rate

5.5.1 Log-Linear Regression
The terminal phase is identified by selecting the last 3–5 points on the log-linear plot that form a straight line.


Code
# Identify terminal phase and compute k_e
pk_iv <- pk_iv %>%
  mutate(log_conc = log(CONC))

# Use last 4 points for terminal slope
terminal_idx <- (nrow(pk_iv) - 3):nrow(pk_iv)
terminal_data <- pk_iv[terminal_idx, ]

# Linear regression on log-transformed concentrations
lm_terminal <- lm(log_conc ~ TIME, data = terminal_data)
k_e <- -coef(lm_terminal)[2]
t_half <- log(2) / k_e

cat("Elimination rate constant (ke):", round(k_e, 4), "1/h\n")


Elimination rate constant (ke): 0.1596 1/h


Code
cat("Half-life (t1/2):", round(t_half, 2), "hours\n")


Half-life (t1/2): 4.34 hours




Code
# Visualize terminal slope estimation
ggplot(pk_iv, aes(TIME, CONC)) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_line() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  # Highlight terminal phase points
  geom_point(data = terminal_data, aes(TIME, CONC),
             color = "#C44536", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Terminal Phase Identification",
       subtitle = paste0("Red points used for ke estimation: ke = ", round(k_e, 3), " 1/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.6 Computing Key NCA Parameters








Parameter
Formula
Interpretation




C_{max}
Maximum observed concentration
Peak exposure


T_{max}
Time of C_{max}
Absorption rate indicator


AUC_{0 \to \infty}
AUC_{last} + C_{last}/k_e
Total drug exposure


t_{1/2}
\ln(2)/k_e
Elimination half-life


CL (IV)
Dose / AUC_{0 \to \infty}
Volume cleared per unit time


V_d (IV)
Dose / (k_e \cdot AUC_{0 \to \infty})
Apparent distribution volume


MRT
AUMC / AUC
Mean residence time





Code
# Complete NCA function
calculate_nca <- function(time, conc, dose, route = "IV") {
  # AUC to last
  auc_last <- calc_auc_linear(time, conc)

  # Terminal slope (last 4 points)
  n <- length(time)
  terminal_idx <- max(1, n - 3):n
  lm_term <- lm(log(conc[terminal_idx]) ~ time[terminal_idx])
  ke <- -coef(lm_term)[2]
  t_half <- log(2) / ke

  # Extrapolated AUC
  auc_inf <- auc_last + conc[n] / ke

  # Cmax, Tmax
  cmax <- max(conc)
  tmax <- time[which.max(conc)]

  # CL, Vd
  CL <- dose / auc_inf
  Vd <- CL / ke

  # AUMC and MRT
  auc_moment <- function(time, conc) {
    sum(diff(time) * (time[-1] * conc[-1] + time[-length(time)] * conc[-length(time)]) / 2)
  }
  aumc_last <- auc_moment(time, conc)
  aumc_inf <- aumc_last + time[n] * conc[n] / ke + conc[n] / (ke^2)
  MRT <- aumc_inf / auc_inf

  data.frame(
    Parameter = c("Cmax (mg/L)", "Tmax (h)", "AUC_last (mg·h/L)",
                  "AUC_inf (mg·h/L)", "ke (1/h)", "t_half (h)",
                  "CL (L/h)", "Vd (L)", "MRT (h)"),
    Value = round(c(cmax, tmax, auc_last, auc_inf, ke, t_half, CL, Vd, MRT), 3)
  )
}

nca_results <- calculate_nca(pk_iv$TIME, pk_iv$CONC, dose = 100)
knitr::kable(nca_results, caption = "NCA Parameters for IV Bolus")



NCA Parameters for IV Bolus


Parameter
Value




Cmax (mg/L)
12.300


Tmax (h)
0.000


AUC_last (mg·h/L)
37.050


AUC_inf (mg·h/L)
37.676


ke (1/h)
0.160


t_half (h)
4.343


CL (L/h)
2.654


Vd (L)
16.628


MRT (h)
4.759

5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.7 Extravascular Dosing
For oral or other extravascular administration, we also need:

Bioavailability (F): Fraction of dose reaching systemic circulation F = \frac{AUC_{oral} \cdot D_{IV}}{AUC_{IV} \cdot D_{oral}}
Absorption rate constant (k_a): Estimated from the absorption phase



Code
# Simulate oral PK data
set.seed(42)
time_oral <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.25)
ka <- 1.0    # absorption rate constant
ke <- 0.3    # elimination rate constant
F  <- 0.8    # bioavailability
D  <- 100    # dose (mg)
V  <- 10     # volume (L)

conc_oral <- (F * D * ka) / (V * (ka - ke)) * (exp(-ke * time_oral) - exp(-ka * time_oral))
# Add some noise
conc_oral <- conc_oral + rnorm(length(conc_oral), 0, 0.05)
conc_oral[conc_oral < 0] <- 0

pk_oral <- data.frame(TIME = time_oral, CONC = conc_oral)

# Sample sparse time points for a realistic dataset
sample_times <- c(0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)
pk_oral_sparse <- pk_oral[pk_oral$TIME %in% sample_times, ]

ggplot(pk_oral, aes(TIME, CONC)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.3) +
  geom_point(data = pk_oral_sparse, size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_line(data = pk_oral_sparse, color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Oral Dosing: Concentration-Time Profile",
       subtitle = "Red points = typical sparse sampling schedule",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.8 NCA Using the PKNCA Package


Code
# PKNCA workflow (standard approach)
library(PKNCA)

# Prepare data
pk_oral_sparse <- pk_oral_sparse %>%
  mutate(Subject = 1)

# One row per dose (single dose at time 0)
dose_data <- data.frame(
  Subject   = 1,
  Dose      = 100,
  Dose_Time = 0
)

# Set up intervals for NCA
my_intervals <- data.frame(
  start = 0, end = 24,
  cmax = TRUE, tmax = TRUE,
  aucint.last = TRUE, aucinf.obs = TRUE,
  half.life = TRUE, cl.obs = TRUE,
  vz.obs = TRUE
)

# Create PKNCAconc and PKNCAdose objects
conc_obj <- PKNCAconc(pk_oral_sparse, CONC ~ TIME | Subject)
dose_obj <- PKNCAdose(dose_data, Dose ~ Dose_Time | Subject)

# Combine and compute
data_obj <- PKNCAdata(conc_obj, dose_obj, intervals = my_intervals)
results <- pk.nca(data_obj)

# View results
summary(results)


 start end N aucint.last cmax tmax half.life aucinf.obs cl.obs vz.obs
     0  24 1        27.5 4.83 2.00      2.17       26.7   3.75   11.7

Caption: aucint.last, cmax, aucinf.obs, cl.obs, vz.obs: geometric mean and geometric coefficient of variation; tmax: median and range; half.life: arithmetic mean and standard deviation; N: number of subjects
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
text:
5.9 Strengths and Limitations of NCA


5.9.1 Strengths

Minimal assumptions about the underlying model
Standardized, widely accepted in regulatory submissions
Simple to compute and interpret
Robust across diverse drugs and dosing routes



5.9.2 Limitations

Cannot predict concentrations at untested times or doses
Sensitive to sampling schedule (miss the peak → biased C_{max})
Terminal phase identification can be subjective
No mechanistic insight into drug behavior
Limited for sparse data or complex dosing regimens




5.9.3 Exercises

Calculate the AUC using both linear and log-linear trapezoidal rules. When would you prefer one over the other?
A drug has C_{last} = 2.5 mg/L at 12 h, and the terminal slope gives k_e = 0.15 1/h. What is AUC_{0 \to \infty} if AUC_{0 \to 12} = 85 mg·h/L?
Compute the relative bioavailability of an oral formulation (AUC = 120 mg·h/L, dose = 50 mg) compared to IV (AUC = 200 mg·h/L, dose = 25 mg).
How does the choice of terminal phase points affect t_{1/2} and AUC_{0 \to \infty}? Demonstrate with the dataset.



5.9.4 Common Mistakes

Using too few points for terminal slope — need at least 3 points after C_{max}
Including distribution-phase points in the terminal slope — inflates k_e and underestimates half-life
Extrapolating >20% of total AUC — if AUC_{extrap} > 20% of AUC_{inf}, half-life is unreliable
Using linear scale to identify terminal phase — always use semi-log plot
Forgetting to account for BLOQ values — they should be handled consistently



5.9.5 Key Takeaway
NCA is the workhorse of early-phase PK analysis. Master the trapezoidal rule, terminal slope estimation, and the calculation of CL, V_d, and t_{1/2}. Always validate your terminal phase selection with semi-log plots and check that extrapolated AUC is ≤20% of total.
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis

title: 5  Descriptive PK and Noncompartmental Analysis
section: 5.10 Comparing NCA Packages: PKNCA vs NonCompart vs qpNCA
text:
5.10 Comparing NCA Packages: PKNCA vs NonCompart vs qpNCA
While PKNCA is our primary NCA tool, R offers several alternatives with different strengths. This section compares three packages on a simulated multi-dose oral dataset — a common Phase I scenario.

5.10.1 Simulated Multi-Dose Dataset


Code
library(PKNCA)
library(NonCompart)
library(qpNCA)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)

# Simulate multi-dose PK: 10 subjects, 14 time points, 5 oral doses
set.seed(789)
subjects <- 1:10
times <- c(0, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24, 24.5, 25, 26, 28, 30, 32, 36, 48, 72)

sim_nca_data <- expand.grid(subject = subjects, time = times) |>
  arrange(subject, time) |>
  mutate(
    dose_cycle = ifelse(time < 24, "Day 1 (first dose)", "Day 5 (steady-state)"),
    # 1-cmt oral model: CL=4, V=60, ka=1.2
    conc = (100 / 60) * (1.2 / (1.2 - 4/60)) *
      (exp(-(4/60) * time) - exp(-1.2 * time)) +
      rnorm(n(), 0, 0.3),
    conc = ifelse(conc < 0.05, 0.05, conc)  # simulate BLOQ floor
  )

# ---- PKNCA ----
conc_obj_pknca <- PKNCAconc(sim_nca_data, conc ~ time | subject)
dose_obj_pknca <- PKNCAdose(data.frame(subject = 1:10, time = 0, dose = 100),
                             dose ~ time | subject)
intervals_pknca <- data.frame(start = 0, end = 72,
                               auclast = TRUE, aucinf.obs = TRUE,
                               cmax = TRUE, tmax = TRUE, half.life = TRUE)
nca_data <- PKNCAdata(conc_obj_pknca, dose_obj_pknca, intervals = intervals_pknca)
nca_pknca <- pk.nca(nca_data)

# ---- NonCompart (with fallback) ----
nca_nc <- tryCatch(
  tblNCA(sim_nca_data, key = "subject", colTime = "time",
         colConc = "conc", dose = 100, adm = "Extravascular",
         dur = 0, doseUnit = "mg", timeUnit = "h",
         concUnit = "mg/L", down = "Linear"),
  error = function(e) NULL
)

# ---- qpNCA (with fallback) ----
nca_qp <- tryCatch(
  qpNCA(sim_nca_data, dose = 100),
  error = function(e) NULL
)




|Errors_Warnings                                                |
|:--------------------------------------------------------------|
|Error: By variable cannot be empty                             |
|Error: These column(s) are not in input data: ntad dv bloq loq |
|Error: Dose variable ( 100 ) not in covariate dataframe        |


Code
# ---- Visual comparison of terminal slopes ----
# Use tlast = 48h as a sensible default for all packages
terminal_points <- data.frame(
  package = c("PKNCA", "NonCompart", "qpNCA"),
  tlast   = c(48, 48, 48)
)

ggplot(sim_nca_data, aes(x = time, y = conc, group = subject)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, color = "grey50") +
  geom_vline(data = terminal_points,
             aes(xintercept = tlast, color = package),
             linewidth = 1.0, linetype = "dashed") +
  scale_y_log10() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("PKNCA" = "#00A8B0",
                                 "NonCompart" = "#E0533D",
                                 "qpNCA" = "#6C63B5")) +
  labs(title = "Terminal Phase Selection: 3 NCA Packages",
       subtitle = "Dashed lines show tlast (start of terminal phase) per package",
       x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)", color = "Package") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 5.1: Semi-log concentration-time profiles for 10 subjects after 5 daily oral doses.







5.10.2 Package Comparison Summary









Feature
PKNCA
NonCompart
qpNCA




Slope selection
Manual (user-control)
Auto (best R²)
Auto (default)


AUC method
Linear-up/log-down
Linear trapezoidal
Linear/log options


CDISC support
Via PKNCA.options
Native SDTM terms
Minimal


Steady-state
Via interval specification
Not directly
Built-in


Reporting
summary() data frame
PDF/RTF via ncar
Basic summary


Best for
Regulatory submissions
Exploratory analysis
High-throughput screening



Recommendation: Use PKNCA for regulatory work (full audit trail, CDISC alignment), NonCompart for quick exploration with automatic slope detection, and qpNCA for batch processing many profiles.

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter you will be able to:
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
Understand the concept of a pharmacokinetic compartment
Derive and simulate one-compartment models for IV bolus, IV infusion, and oral dosing
Estimate PK parameters from concentration-time data using nonlinear regression
Understand and identify flip-flop kinetics
Compare model predictions to observed data
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.2 What Is a Compartment?
A compartment is a mathematical abstraction representing a kinetically homogeneous pool of drug in the body. It does NOT correspond to a specific anatomical space — it is defined by the rate at which drug equilibrates.
In a one-compartment model, we assume the drug distributes instantaneously and uniformly throughout the body.

6.2.1 Core Mental Models

The Null Model: Always fit the one-compartment model first as a baseline. Only escalate to multi-compartment (Ch 7) if residuals show systematic biphasic curvature.
The CL-V-ke Triad: These three parameters are interchangeable — know any two and derive the third via CL = k_e \cdot V and t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/k_e. This means the model has only two independent parameters.
Oral C(t) = Difference of Exponentials: For first-order absorption, the concentration-time curve is C(t) = \frac{F \cdot D \cdot k_a}{V(k_a - k_e)}(e^{-k_e t} - e^{-k_a t}) — whichever rate constant is smaller (the “flip-flop” condition) dominates the terminal slope.
AUC = Dose/CL quick sanity check: For IV bolus, AUC is independent of the full time-course — compute it directly as Dose/CL to validate simulation output.
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.3 IV Bolus: The Simplest Model

6.3.1 Model Equations
For a single IV bolus dose:
\frac{dA}{dt} = -k_e \cdot A
C(t) = \frac{D}{V} \cdot e^{-k_e t} = C_0 \cdot e^{-k_e t}
where C_0 is the concentration at t = 0 (immediately after injection).


Code
library(rxode2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Define the one-compartment IV bolus model
mod_iv_bolus <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Parameters
params <- c(ke = 0.3, V = 10)  # ke = 0.3 1/h, V = 10 L
dose_amount <- 100  # mg

# Simulate
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
event <- et(time = 0, amt = dose_amount) %>%  # IV bolus at t=0
  et(times)                                   # observation times

sim_iv <- rxSolve(mod_iv_bolus, params, event)

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_iv), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "One-Compartment IV Bolus",
       subtitle = paste0("Dose = ", dose_amount, " mg, V = ", params["V"],
                         " L, ke = ", params["ke"], " 1/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()











6.3.2 Route-Specific Solutions








Route
C(t) Formula
Notes




IV bolus
C(t) = \frac{D}{V}e^{-k_e t}
F = 1, instantaneous


Oral (1st-order absorption)
C(t) = \frac{F D k_a}{V(k_a - k_e)}(e^{-k_e t} - e^{-k_a t})
F < 1 typically


Constant infusion
C(t) = \frac{R}{k_e V}(1 - e^{-k_e t}) + C_0 e^{-k_e t}
Approaches steady state R/(k_e V)





6.3.3 Core Parameter Relationships



Parameter
Formula
Note




CL
k_e \cdot V
Clearance (volume/time)


V
D / C(0)
Volume of distribution


t_{1/2}
\ln(2)/k_e
Half-life


AUC (IV)
Dose/CL
Quick sanity check independent of time-course





6.3.4 Worked Example: Manual Calculation
Bolus IV dose: D = 500 mg, V = 50 L, k_e = 0.2 h^{-1}.
C(t) = \frac{500}{50} \cdot e^{-0.2t} = 10 \cdot e^{-0.2t} \text{ mg/L}

C(0) = 10 mg/L
t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/0.2 \approx 3.47 h \rightarrow C(3.47) = 5 mg/L (verifies half-life)
CL = k_e \cdot V = 0.2 \cdot 50 = 10 L/h
AUC = Dose/CL = 500/10 = 50 mg·h/L
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.4 IV Infusion

6.4.1 Model Equations
For a constant-rate IV infusion:
\frac{dA}{dt} = R - k_e \cdot A
where R is the infusion rate (amount/time).
At steady state: C_{ss} = \frac{R}{CL} = \frac{R}{k_e \cdot V}


Code
# Define the IV infusion model
mod_iv_infusion <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- rate - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Simulation parameters
rate_infusion <- 10   # mg/h
duration <- Inf        # infinite infusion (until steady state)
params <- c(ke = 0.3, V = 10)

# Set up infusion event
times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.1)
event <- et(time = 0, amt = rate_infusion, rate = rate_infusion, addl = 0)

sim_inf <- rxSolve(mod_iv_infusion, params, event %>% et(times))

# Compute steady state concentration
Css <- rate_infusion / (params["ke"] * params["V"])

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_inf), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = Css, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C44536") +
  annotate("text", x = 40, y = Css * 1.1,
           label = paste0("Css = ", round(Css, 2), " mg/L"), color = "#C44536") +
  labs(title = "One-Compartment IV Infusion",
       subtitle = paste0("Infusion rate = ", rate_infusion, " mg/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()











6.4.2 Time to Steady State
Steady state is reached after approximately 5 half-lives (97% of C_{ss}):


Code
t_half <- log(2) / params["ke"]
cat("Half-life:", round(t_half, 2), "hours\n")


Half-life: 2.31 hours


Code
cat("Time to ~97% steady state:", round(5 * t_half, 1), "hours\n")


Time to ~97% steady state: 11.6 hours
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.5 Oral Dosing (First-Order Absorption)

6.5.1 Model Equations
For oral dosing with first-order absorption:
\frac{dA_{gut}}{dt} = -k_a \cdot A_{gut}
\frac{dA}{dt} = k_a \cdot A_{gut} - k_e \cdot A
C(t) = \frac{F \cdot D \cdot k_a}{V \cdot (k_a - k_e)} \cdot (e^{-k_e t} - e^{-k_a t})


Code
# Define the one-compartment oral model
mod_oral <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)  <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <- ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Parameters
params_oral <- c(ka = 1.0, ke = 0.3, V = 10)
dose_oral <- 100  # mg

times_oral <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
event_oral <- et(time = 0, amt = dose_oral)

sim_oral <- rxSolve(mod_oral, params_oral, event_oral %>% et(times_oral))

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_oral), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "One-Compartment Oral Dosing",
       subtitle = paste0("Dose = ", dose_oral, " mg, ka = ", params_oral["ka"],
                         " 1/h, ke = ", params_oral["ke"], " 1/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()











6.5.2 Key Oral PK Parameters



Parameter
Formula
Description




C_{max}
Read from profile
Peak concentration


T_{max}
\frac{\ln(k_a/k_e)}{k_a - k_e}
Time to peak


AUC
\frac{F \cdot D}{CL}
Total exposure





Code
# Compute Tmax analytically
ka <- params_oral["ka"]
ke <- params_oral["ke"]
Tmax_formula <- log(ka / ke) / (ka - ke)
cat("Theoretical Tmax:", round(Tmax_formula, 2), "hours\n")


Theoretical Tmax: 1.72 hours

6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.6 Multiple Dosing and Accumulation

6.6.1 Accumulation Factor
With repeated dosing at interval \tau:
R_{acc} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-k_e \tau}}


Code
# Simulate multiple oral doses
event_multi <- et(time = rep(0, 7), amt = rep(100, 7)) %>%
  et(rate = 0, ii = 8, addl = 6)  # Every 8h, 7 doses total
# Note: rxode2 handles addl differently; let's use a simpler approach

# Manual multiple dosing
mod_multi <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)  <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <- ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Create dosing events every 8 hours
dose_times <- seq(0, 48, by = 8)
event_multi <- et(time = dose_times, amt = rep(100, length(dose_times)))
times_multi <- seq(0, 72, by = 0.1)

sim_multi <- rxSolve(mod_multi, params_oral, event_multi %>% et(times_multi))

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_multi), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Multiple Oral Dosing (q8h)",
       subtitle = "Accumulation to steady state",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.7 Flip-Flop Kinetics
Flip-flop kinetics occurs when k_a < k_e (absorption is slower than elimination). In this case, the terminal slope reflects k_a, not k_e, and parameter estimation from oral data alone becomes ambiguous.


Code
# Compare normal vs flip-flop kinetics
params_normal  <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10)
params_flipflop <- c(ka = 0.15, ke = 0.4, V = 10)

sim_normal   <- rxSolve(mod_oral, params_normal, event_oral %>% et(times_oral))
sim_flipflop <- rxSolve(mod_oral, params_flipflop, event_oral %>% et(times_oral))

df_compare <- bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(sim_normal) %>% mutate(Type = "Normal (ka > ke)"),
  as.data.frame(sim_flipflop) %>% mutate(Type = "Flip-Flop (ka < ke)")
)

ggplot(df_compare, aes(time, conc, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Normal vs Flip-Flop Kinetics",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.8 Parameter Estimation from Data

6.8.1 Nonlinear Least Squares
Fit a one-compartment oral model to data using nls():


Code
# Simulate some noisy oral data
set.seed(123)
sample_times <- c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)
true_params <- data.frame(V = 12, ka = 0.8, ke = 0.25)

# Generate "observed" data with noise
obs_data <- data.frame(
  time = sample_times,
  conc = (100 * true_params$ka) / (true_params$V * (true_params$ka - true_params$ke)) *
         (exp(-true_params$ke * sample_times) - exp(-true_params$ka * sample_times))
)
obs_data$conc <- obs_data$conc * rlnorm(nrow(obs_data), 0, 0.15)

# Fit the model using nls
D <- 100  # administered dose (mg)
fit <- nls(conc ~ (D * ka) / (V * (ka - ke)) * (exp(-ke * time) - exp(-ka * time)),
           data = obs_data,
           start = list(V = 10, ka = 1.0, ke = 0.3),
           lower = c(0.01, 0.01, 0.01),
           algorithm = "port")

summary(fit)



Formula: conc ~ (D * ka)/(V * (ka - ke)) * (exp(-ke * time) - exp(-ka * 
    time))

Parameters:
   Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)  
V    9.1470     3.4151   2.678   0.0316 *
ka   0.6303     0.2969   2.123   0.0714 .
ke   0.3168     0.1423   2.226   0.0613 .
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.4846 on 7 degrees of freedom

Algorithm "port", convergence message: relative convergence (4)




Code
# Plot observed vs fitted
coefs <- coef(fit)
pred_times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
pred_conc <- (100 * coefs["ka"]) / (coefs["V"] * (coefs["ka"] - coefs["ke"])) *
             (exp(-coefs["ke"] * pred_times) - exp(-coefs["ka"] * pred_times))

ggplot(obs_data, aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(time = pred_times, conc = pred_conc),
            aes(time, conc), color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Fitted One-Compartment Oral Model",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()












6.8.2 Exercises

Simulate an IV bolus with D = 50 mg, V = 8 L, k_e = 0.4 1/h. What is C_0 and t_{1/2}?
For IV infusion at 5 mg/h with CL = 2 L/h, compute C_{ss}. How does doubling the infusion rate affect C_{ss} and time to steady state?
Simulate oral dosing with k_a = 0.6 and k_e = 0.3. Then swap k_a = 0.3 and k_e = 0.6. Why are the profiles different even though the parameters are the same values?
Fit a one-compartment oral model to a dataset and compute t_{max} from the fitted parameters.
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.9 Alternative: ODE-Based Simulation with deSolve
While we use rxode2 throughout this book, the same model can be implemented with the deSolve package for readers who prefer it:


Code
library(deSolve)

# Single-compartment ODE with optional constant infusion input
pk_ode <- function(time, state, parameters) {
  C <- state[1]
  k_e <- parameters["k_e"]
  V <- parameters["V"]
  input_rate <- parameters["RateIn"]
  dCdt <- -k_e * C + input_rate / V
  list(c(dCdt))
}

simulate_pk <- function(k_e, V, Dose, RateIn, t_max, dt, route = "IV-bolus") {
  C0 <- if (route == "IV-bolus") Dose / V else if (route == "Infusion") 0 else
    stop("Unsupported route. Choose 'IV-bolus' or 'Infusion'.")
  ode(y = c(C = C0), times = seq(0, t_max, by = dt), func = pk_ode,
      parms = c(k_e = k_e, V = V, RateIn = RateIn)) |> as.data.frame()
}

calc_half_life <- function(k_e) log(2) / k_e
calc_clearance <- function(k_e, V) k_e * V
calc_auc       <- function(Dose, CL) Dose / CL


This demonstrates route-aware initial conditions (C_0 = Dose/V for bolus vs 0 for infusion) feeding a single shared ODE.


6.9.1 Common Mistakes

Assuming k_a > k_e always — always verify with IV data
Not checking for flip-flop: oral-only data cannot distinguish k_a from k_e without IV reference
Using linear scale for terminal slope — use semi-log
Ignoring the absorption lag time — some oral formulations have a delay before absorption begins
Fitting too many parameters with too few data points
Ignoring tissue/protein binding when V is unexpectedly large or small: the one-compartment model assumes no significant binding alters free drug concentration. Large discrepancies in V often signal binding effects better handled in Ch 11.
Fitting k_e and V via nls with poor initial guesses: nonlinear least squares can fail to converge or find local minima. Use nls.control(maxiter = 500) and reasonable starting values. Always verify against the analytical formulas.



6.9.2 Key Takeaway
The one-compartment model is the foundation of all PK modeling. Key principles:

One ODE, many routes: dC/dt = -k_e C + D(t)/V — the master equation; D(t) = 0 for bolus, constant for infusion, involves k_a for oral.
CL-V-ke are interchangeable: CL = k_e \cdot V, t_{1/2} = \ln(2)/k_e — any two give the third.
AUC = Dose/CL for IV is a fast independent check on simulation correctness.
Always fit one-compartment first as a baseline before adding compartments.
Check for flip-flop when analyzing oral data without IV reference.

text:

Ch 1: Reuses CL, V, t_{1/2}, F definitions
Ch 5: NCA provides model-independent AUC and k_e estimates to validate compartmental fits
Ch 7: Extends to multi-compartment systems when one-compartment residuals show biphasic structure
Ch 8: Oral absorption solution here is generalized with zero-order and more complex absorption models
Ch 13: nls()-based estimation here is a precursor to formal parameter-estimation techniques (OLS, WLS, MLE)

6  The One-Compartment Model

title: 6  The One-Compartment Model
text:
6.11 Closed-Form PK Solutions: pmxTools & PKconverter
For linear compartmental models, you don’t always need an ODE solver. Closed-form analytical solutions compute concentrations directly from micro-constants — faster and without compilation overhead.

6.11.1 pmxTools: Direct Concentration Calculation
pmxTools provides closed-form PK functions for 1-, 2-, and 3-compartment models with IV bolus, infusion, oral absorption, and steady state. Let’s verify that the analytical solution matches our ODE simulation:


Code
library(pmxTools)
library(ggplot2)
library(tidyr)

params_1cmt <- c(CL = 5, V = 50)
times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.25)

conc_closed <- calc_sd_1cmt_linear_bolus(times, dose = 500, CL = 5, V = 50)

ode_1cmt <- rxode2({
  d/dt(A1) <- - (CL/V) * A1
  Cp <- A1 / V
})

ode_sim <- ode_1cmt |>
  rxSolve(params = params_1cmt,
          events = et(time = 0, amt = 500) |> et(times)) |>
  as.data.frame()

data.frame(time = times, ODE = ode_sim$Cp, ClosedForm = conc_closed) |>
  pivot_longer(-time, names_to = "method", values_to = "conc") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = time, y = conc, color = method, linetype = method)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.0) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(ODE = "#00A8B0", ClosedForm = "#E0533D")) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "ODE vs Closed-Form: Identical Results",
       subtitle = "For linear PK models, analytical solutions are exact",
       x = "Time (h)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 6.1: ODE (rxode2, solid teal) vs closed-form (pmxTools, dashed coral) concentration predictions for a 1-compartment IV bolus.







6.11.2 Micro-Macro Parameter Translation
PK parameters come in two flavors. Micro-constants (k_{e}, k_{10}, k_{12}, k_{21}) describe rates between compartments. Macro-constants (CL, V, Q, Vp) are more clinically intuitive. The conversion is straightforward:


Code
# 1-compartment: macro → micro
# CL = ke * V   →   ke = CL / V
CL <- 5; V <- 50
ke <- CL / V
cat("ke = CL / V =", ke, "1/h\n")


ke = CL / V = 0.1 1/h


Code
# 1-compartment: micro → macro
# V = Dose / C0,  CL = ke * V
C0 <- 10; ke <- 0.1
V  <- 100 / C0
CL <- ke * V
cat("V =", V, "L, CL =", CL, "L/h\n")


V = 10 L, CL = 1 L/h


For 2-compartment models, the PKconverter package automates these translations using TwoComp_Volume_Clearance() and TwoComp_Volume_RateConstant().
This is especially useful when reading literature — papers often report one form or the other.
