7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models

title: 7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models
text:
7.2 Why Multi-Compartment Models?
After IV bolus dosing, many drugs show a biphasic decline in concentration: a rapid initial drop (distribution phase) followed by a slower decline (elimination phase). A one-compartment model cannot capture this behavior.


Code
library(rxode2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Compare one- vs two-compartment profiles
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.05)

# One-compartment
mod_1cmt <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Two-compartment
mod_2cmt <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central)    <- -k10 * central - k12 * central + k21 * peripheral
  d/dt(peripheral) <-  k12 * central - k21 * peripheral
  conc <- central / V1
})

params_1 <- c(ke = 0.3, V = 15)
params_2 <- c(k10 = 0.3, k12 = 1.5, k21 = 0.8, V1 = 5)

event <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)

sim1 <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, params_1, event %>% et(times))
sim2 <- rxSolve(mod_2cmt, params_2, event %>% et(times))

df <- bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(sim1) %>% mutate(Model = "One-Compartment"),
  as.data.frame(sim2) %>% mutate(Model = "Two-Compartment")
)

ggplot(df, aes(time, conc, color = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "One- vs Two-Compartment Models",
       subtitle = "Note the biphasic decline in the two-compartment model",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models

title: 7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models
text:
7.3 Two-Compartment Model Structure

7.3.1 Model Equations
\frac{dA_1}{dt} = -k_{10}A_1 - k_{12}A_1 + k_{21}A_2 \frac{dA_2}{dt} = k_{12}A_1 - k_{21}A_2 C_1(t) = \frac{A_1(t)}{V_1}
where: - A_1, A_2: amounts in central and peripheral compartments - V_1: volume of central compartment - k_{10}: elimination rate constant from central compartment - k_{12}: transfer rate from central to peripheral - k_{21}: transfer rate from peripheral to central


7.3.2 Analytical Solution
C(t) = A \cdot e^{-\alpha t} + B \cdot e^{-\beta t}
where \alpha (distribution) and \beta (elimination) are hybrid rate constants (\alpha > \beta), and A and B are hybrid coefficients.


Code
# Visualize the analytical solution components
alpha <- 2.8
beta  <- 0.25
A_coef <- 12
B_coef <- 8

times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.05)
C_distribution <- A_coef * exp(-alpha * times)
C_elimination  <- B_coef * exp(-beta * times)
C_total <- C_distribution + C_elimination

df_comp <- data.frame(
  time = rep(times, 3),
  conc = c(C_total, C_distribution, C_elimination),
  Component = rep(c("Total", "Distribution (α)", "Elimination (β)"), each = length(times))
)

ggplot(df_comp, aes(time, conc, color = Component, linetype = Component)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Two-Compartment Model: Analytical Components",
       subtitle = paste0("C(t) = ", round(A_coef, 1), "·e^{-", alpha, "t} + ",
                         round(B_coef, 1), "·e^{-", beta, "t}"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#D4A843", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models

title: 7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models
text:
7.4 Micro- and Macro-Constants








Parameter Type
Symbols
Meaning




Micro-constants
k_{10}, k_{12}, k_{21}
Rate constants between compartments


Macro-constants
A, B, \alpha, \beta
Hybrid coefficients and exponents




7.4.1 Conversion Between Micro and Macro
\alpha + \beta = k_{10} + k_{12} + k_{21} \alpha \cdot \beta = k_{10} \cdot k_{21} A = \frac{D(\alpha - k_{21})}{V_1(\alpha - \beta)}, \quad B = \frac{D(k_{21} - \beta)}{V_1(\alpha - \beta)}


Code
# Convert micro to macro constants
micro_to_macro <- function(k10, k12, k21, V1, dose) {
  sum_k <- k10 + k12 + k21
  prod_k <- k10 * k21

  # Alpha and beta are roots of: r^2 - sum_k * r + prod_k = 0
  disc <- sqrt(sum_k^2 - 4 * prod_k)
  alpha <- (sum_k + disc) / 2
  beta  <- (sum_k - disc) / 2

  A <- dose * (alpha - k21) / (V1 * (alpha - beta))
  B <- dose * (k21 - beta) / (V1 * (alpha - beta))

  c(alpha = alpha, beta = beta, A = A, B = B)
}

macro <- micro_to_macro(k10 = 0.3, k12 = 1.5, k21 = 0.8, V1 = 5, dose = 100)
macro


      alpha        beta           A           B 
 2.50415946  0.09584054 14.15227399  5.84772601

title: 7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models
text:
7.5 Fitting a Two-Compartment Model

7.5.1 Using nls() with the Analytical Solution


Code
# Simulate noisy two-compartment data
set.seed(42)
sample_times <- c(0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)
true_macro <- micro_to_macro(0.3, 1.5, 0.8, 5, 100)

true_conc <- true_macro["A"] * exp(-true_macro["alpha"] * sample_times) +
             true_macro["B"] * exp(-true_macro["beta"] * sample_times)

obs_2cmt <- data.frame(
  time = sample_times,
  conc = true_conc * rlnorm(length(sample_times), 0, 0.1)
)

# Fit two-compartment model
fit_2cmt <- nls(conc ~ A * exp(-alpha * time) + B * exp(-beta * time),
                data = obs_2cmt,
                start = list(A = 15, alpha = 2.0, B = 10, beta = 0.2),
                lower = c(0, 0, 0, 0),
                algorithm = "port")

summary(fit_2cmt)



Formula: conc ~ A * exp(-alpha * time) + B * exp(-beta * time)

Parameters:
      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
A     15.25748    1.37176  11.123 1.06e-05 ***
alpha  2.89551    0.67800   4.271 0.003698 ** 
B      6.41143    1.15831   5.535 0.000873 ***
beta   0.09685    0.03435   2.819 0.025797 *  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 0.9594 on 7 degrees of freedom

Algorithm "port", convergence message: relative convergence (4)




Code
# Plot observed vs fitted
coefs <- coef(fit_2cmt)
pred_times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.05)
pred_conc <- coefs["A"] * exp(-coefs["alpha"] * pred_times) +
             coefs["B"] * exp(-coefs["beta"] * pred_times)

ggplot(obs_2cmt, aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(time = pred_times, conc = pred_conc),
            aes(time, conc), color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Fitted Two-Compartment Model",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()

section: 7.6 Model Comparison: One vs Two Compartments
text:
7.6 Model Comparison: One vs Two Compartments


Code
# Fit one-compartment model for comparison
fit_1cmt <- nls(conc ~ C0 * exp(-ke * time),
                data = obs_2cmt,
                start = list(C0 = 20, ke = 0.3))

# Compare using AIC
aic_1cmt <- AIC(fit_1cmt)
aic_2cmt <- AIC(fit_2cmt)

comparison <- data.frame(
  Model = c("One-Compartment", "Two-Compartment"),
  AIC = round(c(aic_1cmt, aic_2cmt), 1),
  Parameters = c(2, 4)
)
knitr::kable(comparison, caption = "Model Comparison via AIC")

7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models

title: 7  The Two-Compartment and Multi-Compartment Models
text:
7.7 Identifiability and Parameter Interpretation

7.7.1 When One-Compartment Models Are Not Enough

Distribution phase is clinically relevant (e.g., rapid onset drugs)
Drug shows clear biphasic decline on semi-log plot
Peripheral compartment has physiological meaning (e.g., tissue binding)
Accurate prediction of early time points is needed



7.7.2 Common Issues

Parameter identifiability: With sparse data, 4 parameters (A, B, \alpha, \beta) may not be uniquely estimable
Correlation: A and \alpha are often highly correlated
Initial estimates: Good starting values are critical; use graphical methods (method of residuals)



Code
# Method of residuals for initial estimates
# Step 1: Fit terminal phase to get B and beta
terminal_idx <- 6:length(sample_times)  # Choose terminal points
lm_term <- lm(log(obs_2cmt$conc[terminal_idx]) ~ obs_2cmt$time[terminal_idx])
B_init <- exp(coef(lm_term)[1])
beta_init <- -coef(lm_term)[2]

# Step 2: Calculate residuals and fit distribution phase
terminal_pred <- B_init * exp(-beta_init * sample_times)
residuals <- obs_2cmt$conc - terminal_pred
residuals <- residuals[residuals > 0]

cat("Initial estimates from method of residuals:\n")


Initial estimates from method of residuals:


Code
cat("B =", round(B_init, 2), ", beta =", round(beta_init, 3), "\n")


B = 6.19 , beta = 0.093 





7.7.3 Exercises

Simulate a two-compartment model with k_{10} = 0.2, k_{12} = 2.0, k_{21} = 1.0, V_1 = 8 L. Plot on semi-log scale.
Convert the micro-constants from Exercise 1 to macro-constants (A, B, \alpha, \beta).
Fit a two-compartment model to the simulated data. How well do you recover the true parameters?
When would you choose a two-compartment model over a one-compartment model? Use AIC to compare.
Explain what happens to the concentration-time profile when k_{12} is very large compared to k_{21}.



7.7.4 Common Mistakes

Over-parameterization: A two-compartment model with sparse data may fit noise, not signal
Misidentifying phases: The initial rapid decline may be due to distribution, not elimination
Using one-compartment model when distribution matters: e.g., for drugs with rapid onset of action
Poor initial estimates: nls() is sensitive to starting values; use method of residuals



7.7.5 Key Takeaway
Multi-compartment models capture the distribution phase that one-compartment models miss. The two-compartment model with parameters (A, B, \alpha, \beta) or (k_{10}, k_{12}, k_{21}, V_1) is the most common structural model in PK. Always check semi-log plots for biphasic behavior before choosing your model.

text:

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
Compare PK profiles across different routes of administration
Understand and compute absolute and relative bioavailability
Model absorption with lag time, zero-order, and first-order processes
Analyze modified-release formulations
Select appropriate absorption models based on route and formulation
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
section: 8.2 Routes of Administration and PK Implications
text:
8.2 Routes of Administration and PK Implications

8.2.1 Comparison of Routes








Route
Absorption Process
Key Feature




Intravenous (IV)
None (direct to blood)
100% bioavailability, immediate


Oral (PO)
GI tract → portal vein → liver → systemic
First-pass metabolism, variable F


Subcutaneous (SC)
Diffusion from tissue
Slower than IV, may have flip-flop


Intramuscular (IM)
Diffusion from muscle
Faster than SC, affected by blood flow


Inhalation
Lung epithelium
Rapid absorption, local + systemic


Transdermal
Skin diffusion
Very slow, zero-order kinetics
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.3 Bioavailability

8.3.1 Absolute Bioavailability
F = \frac{AUC_{extravascular} \cdot Dose_{IV}}{AUC_{IV} \cdot Dose_{extravascular}}
F represents the fraction of the administered dose that reaches systemic circulation.


8.3.2 Relative Bioavailability
F_{rel} = \frac{AUC_{test} \cdot Dose_{reference}}{AUC_{reference} \cdot Dose_{test}}
Used to compare two formulations (e.g., tablet vs capsule).


Code
library(rxode2)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate IV and oral profiles to compute bioavailability
mod_1cmt <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)

# IV dose
event_iv <- et(time = 0, amt = 50)  # central compartment
sim_iv <- rxSolve(rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -ke * central
  conc <- central / V
}), c(ke = 0.3, V = 10), event_iv %>% et(times))

# Oral dose
event_po <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)
sim_po <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, c(ka = 1.0, ke = 0.3, V = 10), event_po %>% et(times))

# Compute AUC
auc_iv <- sum(diff(times) * (sim_iv$conc[-1] + sim_iv$conc[-length(sim_iv$conc)]) / 2)
auc_po <- sum(diff(times) * (sim_po$conc[-1] + sim_po$conc[-length(sim_po$conc)]) / 2)

F_abs <- (auc_po * 50) / (auc_iv * 100)
cat("Absolute Bioavailability (F):", round(F_abs * 100, 1), "%\n")


Absolute Bioavailability (F): 99.9 %
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.4 First-Pass Metabolism
Drugs absorbed from the GI tract pass through the liver before reaching systemic circulation. The fraction escaping first-pass metabolism is:
F = f_{abs} \cdot (1 - E_H)
where f_{abs} is the fraction absorbed from the gut and E_H is the hepatic extraction ratio.


Code
# Bioavailability vs hepatic extraction ratio
E_H <- seq(0, 1, by = 0.01)
F_for_EH <- 1 - E_H

ggplot(data.frame(E_H, F_for_EH), aes(E_H, F_for_EH)) +
  geom_line(color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Effect of Hepatic Extraction on Bioavailability",
       subtitle = "Assuming complete absorption (f_abs = 1)",
       x = "Hepatic Extraction Ratio (E_H)", y = "Bioavailability (F)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.5 Absorption Lag Time
Some formulations have a delay before absorption begins (e.g., gastric emptying, tablet disintegration):
C(t) = \frac{F \cdot D \cdot k_a}{V \cdot (k_a - k_e)} \cdot (e^{-k_e(t - t_{lag})} - e^{-k_a(t - t_{lag})}), \quad t \geq t_{lag}


Code
# Model with absorption lag time
mod_lag <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V

  # Parameter constraint
  alag(depot) <- tlag  # lag time for depot compartment
})

# Simulate with and without lag
event_po <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)

sim_no_lag <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, c(ka = 1.0, ke = 0.3, V = 10), event_po %>% et(times))
sim_lag    <- rxSolve(mod_lag, c(ka = 1.0, ke = 0.3, V = 10, tlag = 0.5), event_po %>% et(times))

df_lag <- bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(sim_no_lag) %>% mutate(Type = "No Lag"),
  as.data.frame(sim_lag) %>% mutate(Type = "t_lag = 0.5 h")
)

ggplot(df_lag, aes(time, conc, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Effect of Absorption Lag Time",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.6 Modified-Release Formulations

8.6.1 Types of Modified Release

Extended release (ER/XR): Slower absorption → lower C_{max}, longer apparent t_{1/2}
Delayed release (DR): Release begins after a delay (e.g., enteric coating)
Pulsatile release: Drug released in pulses at specific times



Code
# Model zero-order absorption (constant rate over duration D)
mod_zero <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot  # but with zero-order into depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Simulate extended release using a slow ka
event_er <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)
times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.1)

sim_ir <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, c(ka = 2.0, ke = 0.3, V = 10), event_er %>% et(times))
sim_er <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, c(ka = 0.2, ke = 0.3, V = 10), event_er %>% et(times))

df_mr <- bind_rows(
  as.data.frame(sim_ir) %>% mutate(Formulation = "Immediate Release (ka=2.0)"),
  as.data.frame(sim_er) %>% mutate(Formulation = "Extended Release (ka=0.2)")
)

ggplot(df_mr, aes(time, conc, color = Formulation)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Immediate vs Extended Release",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.7 Subcutaneous and Intramuscular Absorption
For SC/IM routes, absorption often follows first-order kinetics, but the rate can be slower than elimination (flip-flop):


Code
# Compare SC absorption rates
ka_values <- c(0.1, 0.3, 0.8, 2.0)
times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.1)

sims_sc <- lapply(ka_values, function(ka_val) {
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_1cmt, c(ka = ka_val, ke = 0.3, V = 10), et(time = 0, amt = 100) %>% et(times))
  as.data.frame(sim) %>% mutate(ka = factor(paste0("ka = ", ka_val)))
})

df_sc <- bind_rows(sims_sc)

ggplot(df_sc, aes(time, conc, color = ka)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Effect of Absorption Rate on SC Profile",
       subtitle = "ke = 0.3 (fixed); note flip-flop when ka < ke",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)",
       color = "Absorption Rate") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part II — Classical Pharmacokinetic Analysis
8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration

title: 8  Absorption, Bioavailability, and Routes of Administration
text:
8.8 How Route Affects Modeling Choices








Route
Recommended Absorption Model
Key Considerations




IV bolus
None
Instantaneous distribution


IV infusion
Zero-order input
Duration, rate


Oral (IR)
First-order ± lag
T_{max}, flip-flop risk


Oral (MR)
Sequential zero/first-order
Deconvolution may be needed


SC/IM
First-order
Often flip-flop


Transdermal
Zero-order (patch)
Constant rate until removal




8.8.1 Exercises

Compute the absolute bioavailability of an oral tablet (AUC = 85 mg·h/L, dose = 200 mg) given IV AUC = 150 mg·h/L for a 50 mg IV dose.
Simulate an oral profile with t_{lag} = 1 h. How does T_{max} change compared to no lag?
An extended-release formulation has k_a = 0.15 1/h while the IR has k_a = 1.5 1/h. Compare C_{max}, T_{max}, and fluctuation.
Why might a SC injection show flip-flop kinetics? How would you confirm this?



8.8.2 Common Mistakes

Assuming 100% bioavailability for oral drugs — always verify with IV data
Ignoring first-pass metabolism — especially for high-extraction drugs
Not accounting for lag time — can bias k_a and T_{max} estimates
Using the same absorption model for all routes — SC/IM often need different treatment
Confusing absolute and relative bioavailability



8.8.3 Key Takeaway
The route of administration determines the absorption model you need. Master the concepts of bioavailability (F), first-pass metabolism, lag time, and how absorption rate interacts with elimination rate. Always check for flip-flop kinetics with extravascular data.

By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
Distinguish direct and indirect pharmacodynamic effects
Differentiate graded and quantal responses
Fit Emax, sigmoid Emax, linear, and log-linear PD models in R
Interpret E_{max}, EC_{50}, and Hill coefficient
Understand baseline effect, maximal response, and variability
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.2 What Is Pharmacodynamics?
Pharmacodynamics describes the relationship between drug concentration (or dose) and the magnitude of the pharmacological effect. The fundamental question is:

Given a certain drug concentration at the site of action, how large is the effect?
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.3 The Emax Model

9.3.1 Model Equation
E = E_0 + \frac{E_{max} \cdot C}{EC_{50} + C}
where: - E: Observed effect - E_0: Baseline effect (at C = 0) - E_{max}: Maximum drug-attributable effect - EC_{50}: Concentration producing 50% of E_{max} - C: Drug concentration


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Visualize the Emax model
conc <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
Emax <- -80   # e.g., 80% reduction (negative Emax for inhibition)
E0   <- 100   # baseline = 100%
EC50 <- 10

effect <- E0 + (Emax * conc) / (EC50 + conc)

df_emax <- data.frame(conc, effect)

ggplot(df_emax, aes(conc, effect)) +
  geom_line(color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = EC50, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = E0 + Emax, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray50") +
  annotate("text", x = EC50 * 2, y = E0 + 0.5 * Emax,
           label = paste0("EC50 = ", EC50), hjust = 0) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Emax Model",
       subtitle = paste0("E0 = ", E0, ", Emax = ", Emax, ", EC50 = ", EC50),
       x = "Concentration (log scale)", y = "Effect") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.4 The Sigmoid Emax Model

9.4.1 Model Equation
E = E_0 + \frac{E_{max} \cdot C^{\gamma}}{EC_{50}^{\gamma} + C^{\gamma}}
The Hill coefficient (\gamma) controls the steepness of the curve: - \gamma = 1: Standard Emax - \gamma > 1: Steeper (more switch-like) - \gamma < 1: Shallower (more gradual)


Code
# Compare different Hill coefficients
conc <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
E0 <- 0
Emax <- 100
EC50 <- 10
gamma_values <- c(0.5, 1, 2, 5)

df_sigmoid <- expand.grid(conc = conc, gamma = gamma_values) %>%
  mutate(
    effect = E0 + (Emax * conc^gamma) / (EC50^gamma + conc^gamma),
    gamma = factor(paste0("γ = ", gamma))
  )

ggplot(df_sigmoid, aes(conc, effect, color = gamma)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Sigmoid Emax Model: Effect of Hill Coefficient",
       x = "Concentration (log scale)", y = "Effect (% of maximum)",
       color = "Hill Coefficient") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#D4A843", "#C44536", "#0B6E6B", "#1B365D")) +
  theme_minimal()











9.4.2 Interpreting the Hill Coefficient








\gamma
Curve Shape
Biological Interpretation




< 1
Shallow, gradual
Multiple binding sites with negative cooperativity


= 1
Standard hyperbola
Simple 1:1 binding


> 1
Steep, switch-like
Positive cooperativity, signal amplification


>> 1
Nearly step function
Ultrasensitive systems
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.5 Linear and Log-Linear Models

9.5.1 Linear Model
E = E_0 + S \cdot C
Used when the therapeutic range is well below EC_{50}.


9.5.2 Log-Linear Model
E = m \cdot \ln(C) + b
Common in the 20–80% effect range on a log scale.


Code
# Compare Emax, linear, and log-linear in therapeutic range
conc_range <- seq(1, 50, length.out = 100)
EC50 <- 30
Emax <- 80

# Emax
eff_emax <- (Emax * conc_range) / (EC50 + conc_range)
# Linear approximation (slope at C=0 is Emax/EC50)
eff_lin <- (Emax / EC50) * conc_range
# Log-linear (fitted in mid-range)
eff_loglin <- 20 + 30 * log10(conc_range)

df_models <- data.frame(
  conc = rep(conc_range, 3),
  effect = c(eff_emax, eff_lin, eff_loglin),
  Model = rep(c("Emax", "Linear", "Log-Linear"), each = length(conc_range))
)

ggplot(df_models, aes(conc, effect, color = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "PD Model Comparison",
       x = "Concentration", y = "Effect") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.6 Fitting PD Models in R


Code
# Simulate dose-response data
set.seed(123)
doses <- c(0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100, 300)
n_rep <- 4

pd_data <- expand.grid(DOSE = doses, REP = 1:n_rep) %>%
  mutate(
    Emax = 90, EC50 = 10, E0 = 5,
    Effect_true = E0 + (Emax * DOSE) / (EC50 + DOSE),
    Effect_obs  = Effect_true + rnorm(n(), 0, 3)
  )

# Fit Emax model
fit_emax <- nls(Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * DOSE) / (EC50 + DOSE),
                data = pd_data,
                start = list(E0 = 0, Emax = 80, EC50 = 5))
summary(fit_emax)



Formula: Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * DOSE)/(EC50 + DOSE)

Parameters:
     Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
E0     4.0984     0.9741   4.207 0.000227 ***
Emax  90.4666     1.3961  64.801  < 2e-16 ***
EC50   9.4163     0.6610  14.245 1.26e-14 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.909 on 29 degrees of freedom

Number of iterations to convergence: 4 
Achieved convergence tolerance: 3.15e-06




Code
# Fit sigmoid Emax model
fit_sig <- nls(Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * DOSE^gamma) / (EC50^gamma + DOSE^gamma),
               data = pd_data,
               start = list(E0 = 0, Emax = 80, EC50 = 10, gamma = 1))
summary(fit_sig)



Formula: Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * DOSE^gamma)/(EC50^gamma + DOSE^gamma)

Parameters:
      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
E0     3.51986    1.39117    2.53   0.0173 *  
Emax  91.84270    2.82221   32.54  < 2e-16 ***
EC50   9.46661    0.71386   13.26 1.36e-13 ***
gamma  0.95835    0.06916   13.86 4.65e-14 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 2.943 on 28 degrees of freedom

Number of iterations to convergence: 4 
Achieved convergence tolerance: 6.935e-06




Code
# Plot fits
dose_pred <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
pred_emax <- predict(fit_emax, newdata = data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred))
pred_sig  <- predict(fit_sig, newdata = data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred))

df_pred <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred, Effect = pred_emax, Model = "Emax"),
  data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred, Effect = pred_sig,  Model = "Sigmoid Emax")
)

ggplot(pd_data, aes(DOSE, Effect_obs)) +
  geom_point(size = 2, alpha = 0.6, color = "gray40") +
  geom_line(data = df_pred, aes(DOSE, Effect, color = Model), linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Fitted PD Models",
       x = "Dose (log scale)", y = "Effect") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.7 Graded vs Quantal Responses









Response Type
Description
Example
Analysis




Graded
Continuous effect magnitude
Blood pressure reduction (mmHg)
Emax, linear models


Quantal
Binary (yes/no) outcome
Death/survival, response/no response
Logistic regression, ED_{50}





Code
# Quantal dose-response: logistic regression
set.seed(456)
doses_q <- rep(c(1, 3, 10, 30, 100), each = 20)
# Probability of response = Emax model with variability
logit_p <- -2 + 1.5 * log10(doses_q)
p <- plogis(logit_p)
response <- rbinom(length(doses_q), 1, p)

quantal_data <- data.frame(DOSE = doses_q, Response = response) %>%
  group_by(DOSE) %>%
  summarise(N = n(), Responders = sum(Response), .groups = "drop") %>%
  mutate(Fraction = Responders / N)

# Fit logistic regression
fit_logit <- glm(cbind(Responders, N - Responders) ~ log10(DOSE),
                 family = binomial, data = quantal_data)

# Predict for smooth curve
dose_pred <- 10^seq(0, 2.5, length.out = 100)
pred_p <- predict(fit_logit, newdata = data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred),
                  type = "response")

ggplot(quantal_data, aes(DOSE, Fraction)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(DOSE = dose_pred, Fraction = pred_p),
            color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Quantal Dose-Response",
       x = "Dose (log scale)", y = "Fraction Responding") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
section: 9.8 Receptor Theory: The Mechanistic Basis of PD Curves
text:
9.8 Receptor Theory: The Mechanistic Basis of PD Curves
The empirical Emax and sigmoid Emax models describe concentration-effect data well, but they don’t explain why the curves have that shape. Receptor theory bridges this gap by deriving PD curves from the fundamental chemistry of drug-receptor binding.

9.8.1 Mass-Action Binding
The simplest model: a drug molecule (L) binds reversibly to a receptor (R):
R + L \underset{k_{-1}}{\stackrel{k_1}{\rightleftharpoons}} RL
At equilibrium, the dissociation constant K_d is:
K_d = \frac{k_{-1}}{k_1} = \frac{[R][L]}{[RL]}
The fraction of receptors occupied (occupancy, \theta) follows the Langmuir isotherm:
\theta = \frac{[RL]}{[R]_{total}} = \frac{[L]}{K_d + [L]}
This is exactly the same functional form as the Emax model — the Emax curve arises naturally from simple 1:1 binding. EC_{50} corresponds to K_d, and E_{max} corresponds to the maximal response when all receptors are occupied.


Code
# Demonstrate receptor occupancy as a function of concentration
L <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
Kd <- 3.5

# Occupancy (Langmuir isotherm) vs Emax model
occupancy <- L / (Kd + L)
emax_equiv <- L / (Kd + L) * 100  # scaled to 100%

df_receptor <- data.frame(
  Concentration = rep(L, 2),
  Value = c(occupancy, emax_equiv / 100),
  Curve = rep(c("Occupancy (θ)", "Emax (scaled)"), each = length(L))
)

ggplot(df_receptor, aes(Concentration, Value, color = Curve)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  geom_vline(xintercept = Kd, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = Kd * 1.5, y = 0.5, label = paste0("Kd = ", Kd), hjust = 0) +
  labs(title = "Receptor Occupancy = Emax Model",
       subtitle = "The Emax curve is the Langmuir binding isotherm",
       x = "[L] (log scale)", y = "Fraction", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()











9.8.2 From Occupancy to Effect: The Operational Model of Agonism
Not all occupied receptors produce the same effect. The operational model of agonism (Black & Leff, 1983) accounts for the transduction efficiency between binding and response:
E = \frac{E_{max} \cdot \theta^n}{\theta^n + K_A^n}
Substituting \theta = [L]/(K_d + [L]) gives a function that reduces to the sigmoid Emax model under most conditions. This explains why the Hill coefficient (\gamma) can differ from 1 even with simple 1:1 binding — it reflects signal amplification downstream of the receptor.


9.8.3 The Chain: Binding → Occupancy → Effect
\text{Mass action: } R+L \rightleftharpoons RL \quad\rightarrow\quad \theta = \frac{[L]}{K_d+[L]}
\text{Operational model: } E = E_{max} \cdot \frac{\theta^n}{\theta^n + K_A^n}
\text{Empirical (Hill): } E = E_{max} \cdot \frac{[L]^\gamma}{EC_{50}^\gamma + [L]^\gamma}
These three equations form a nested hierarchy — each is a special case or generalization of the one above.
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.9 Agonism, Antagonism, and Allosteric Modulation

9.9.1 Agonists
An agonist binds to a receptor and produces a response. The potency is reflected in EC_{50} (or K_d), and the efficacy in E_{max}.


9.9.2 Competitive Antagonism
A competitive antagonist binds to the same site as the agonist but produces no response. It shifts the dose-response curve to the right without reducing E_{max}:
E = E_{max} \cdot \frac{[A]}{[A] + EC_{50} \cdot (1 + [B]/K_B)}
where [A] is agonist concentration, [B] is antagonist concentration, and K_B is the antagonist’s dissociation constant.


Code
# Competitive antagonism: rightward shift without Emax reduction
A <- 10^seq(-2, 3, length.out = 200)
EC50 <- 8; Emax <- 100
B_conc <- c(0, 2, 8, 25)  # antagonist concentrations
KB <- 4                      # antagonist Kd

df_antag <- expand.grid(A = A, B = B_conc) %>%
  mutate(
    EC50_app = EC50 * (1 + B / KB),
    Effect = Emax * A / (EC50_app + A),
    Antagonist = factor(paste0("[B] = ", B))
  )

ggplot(df_antag, aes(A, Effect, color = Antagonist)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Competitive Antagonism",
       subtitle = "Rightward shift in dose-response curve; Emax preserved",
       x = "[Agonist] (log scale)", y = "Effect (% of maximum)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#D4A843", "#C44536", "#1B365D")) +
  theme_minimal()











9.9.3 Non-Competitive Antagonism
A non-competitive antagonist binds to an allosteric site and reduces the maximal response without shifting EC_{50} (in the pure case), or both reduces E_{max} and shifts EC_{50}:
E = \frac{E_{max}}{1 + [B]/K_B} \cdot \frac{[A]}{[A] + EC_{50}}


9.9.4 Allosteric Modulation
Allosteric modulators bind at a site distinct from the orthosteric (agonist) site and change receptor affinity (\alpha) and/or efficacy (\beta):

Positive allosteric modulator (PAM): increases agonist affinity or efficacy
Negative allosteric modulator (NAM): decreases agonist affinity or efficacy



9.9.5 Clinical Significance
Understanding antagonism mechanisms is essential for: - Reversal agents: Naloxone (competitive antagonist) reverses opioid overdose - DDI prediction: Co-administered antagonists shift the effective dose - Therapeutic window: Irreversible antagonists can cause prolonged effects
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.10 Variability in PD
Sources of variability in PD:

Inter-individual: Different patients, different responses
Intra-individual: Same patient, different occasions
Measurement error: Assay variability
Tolerance/desensitization: Response changes over time



Code
# Visualize inter-individual variability in PD
set.seed(789)
n_subjects <- 20
conc <- 10^seq(-1, 3, length.out = 100)

# Each subject has a different EC50
pd_pop <- expand.grid(conc = conc, ID = 1:n_subjects) %>%
  mutate(
    EC50_ind = rlnorm(n(), log(10), 0.5),  # log-normal variability in EC50
    Emax_ind = rnorm(n(), 80, 10),          # normal variability in Emax
    Effect   = (Emax_ind * conc) / (EC50_ind + conc)
  )

ggplot(pd_pop, aes(conc, Effect, group = ID)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Inter-Individual Variability in PD",
       subtitle = "Each line = one subject; EC50 varies log-normally",
       x = "Concentration (log scale)", y = "Effect") +
  theme_minimal()











9.10.1 Exercises

Fit an Emax model to simulated data. Report E_{max}, EC_{50}, and their standard errors.
How does the Hill coefficient change the shape of the sigmoid Emax curve? Plot for \gamma = 0.5, 1, 2, 4.
Generate quantal dose-response data and estimate ED_{50} using logistic regression.
A drug has E_{max} = -60 (inhibition), EC_{50} = 5 ng/mL, and \gamma = 1. What concentration is needed for 80% of maximum effect?
Compare the AIC of Emax, sigmoid Emax, and linear models for a given dataset. Which is preferred?



9.10.2 Common Mistakes

Using linear models when the drug approaches E_{max} — linear extrapolation overestimates effect
Ignoring baseline (E_0): Always measure and model the baseline
Confusing EC_{50} with potency: Lower EC_{50} means more potent
Forcing \gamma = 1 without checking: Test whether the sigmoid model improves fit
Extrapolating far beyond the observed concentration range



9.10.3 Key Takeaway
The Emax and sigmoid Emax models are the workhorses of PD analysis. Understand the meaning of E_{max}, EC_{50}, and \gamma. Always plot your data before fitting, consider baseline effects, and be aware that PD variability can be substantial between individuals.
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response

title: 9  Pharmacodynamics and Dose-Response
text:
9.11 Advanced Dose-Response Modeling
Beyond the built-in nls() approach, specialized R packages offer robust fitting, model selection, and clinical trial design capabilities.

9.11.1 drc: Multi-Model Dose-Response Fitting
The drc package fits dozens of dose-response models with robust estimation, making it ideal for comparing candidate models on the same dataset.


Code
library(drc)

# Simulate a 7-dose in-vitro assay
set.seed(234)
dose_data <- data.frame(
  dose = rep(c(0.1, 0.3, 1, 3, 10, 30, 100), each = 3),
  response = NA
) |>
  mutate(
    true_resp = 15 + 80 / (1 + (dose / 3)^1.8),
    response  = true_resp + rnorm(n(), 0, 5)
  )

# Fit 4 competing models
m_ll4   <- drm(response ~ dose, data = dose_data, fct = LL.4())
m_ll3   <- drm(response ~ dose, data = dose_data, fct = LL.3())
m_w14   <- drm(response ~ dose, data = dose_data, fct = W1.4())
m_w24   <- drm(response ~ dose, data = dose_data, fct = W2.4())

# Compare AIC
aic_df <- data.frame(
  Model = c("4-param log-logistic", "3-param log-logistic",
            "Weibull-1 (4-param)", "Weibull-2 (4-param)"),
  AIC   = round(c(AIC(m_ll4), AIC(m_ll3), AIC(m_w14), AIC(m_w24)), 1)
)

# Plot all fitted curves
plot(m_ll4, type = "none", log = "x",
     xlab = "Concentration (nM)", ylab = "Response (%)",
     main = "Multi-Model Comparison: drc")

# Add data points
points(dose_data$dose, dose_data$response, pch = 16, col = "#0A1628", cex = 1.2)

# Add fitted lines
plot(m_ll4, add = TRUE, col = "#00A8B0", lwd = 2.5, type = "confidence")
plot(m_w14, add = TRUE, col = "#E0533D", lwd = 2.0, lty = 2)
plot(m_w24, add = TRUE, col = "#6C63B5", lwd = 2.0, lty = 3)
plot(m_ll3, add = TRUE, col = "#4A7A99", lwd = 1.5, lty = 4)

legend("topleft", legend = c(
  "LL.4 (best)", "W1.4", "W2.4", "LL.3"
), col = c("#00A8B0", "#E0533D", "#6C63B5", "#4A7A99"),
       lty = c(1, 2, 3, 4), lwd = c(2.5, 2, 2, 1.5), bty = "n")








Figure 9.1: Four competing dose-response models fitted to simulated in-vitro assay data. AIC values (inset) guide model selection. The 4-parameter log-logistic (LL.4) provides the best fit.





The 4-parameter log-logistic model (LL.4) captures the sigmoid shape best — confirmed by the lowest AIC. The Weibull models fit nearly as well, illustrating that multiple functional forms can describe the same biology.


9.11.2 Outlier Sensitivity in Dose-Response Fitting
Outliers plague real-world dose-response data. Standard nls() is highly sensitive to outliers, producing biased parameter estimates:


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Add an outlier to demonstrate robustness
dirty_data <- dose_data
dirty_data$response[10] <- 30  # outlier at dose = 30

# Standard nls (influenced by outlier)
fit_nls <- tryCatch(
  nls(response ~ E0 + Emax / (1 + (dose / ED50)^(-hill)),
      data = dirty_data,
      start = list(E0 = 15, Emax = 80, ED50 = 3, hill = 1.5)),
  error = function(e) NULL
)

# Robust alternative: iteratively reweighted least squares via MASS::rlm
fit_rlm <- tryCatch({
  # Log-transform dose for linearization
  dirty_data$x_log <- log(dirty_data$dose)
  MASS::rlm(response ~ x_log, data = dirty_data, psi = MASS::psi.bisquare)
}, error = function(e) NULL)

# Generate predictions
dose_grid <- seq(0.1, 100, length.out = 200)
pred_df <- data.frame(dose = dose_grid)

if (!is.null(fit_nls)) {
  pred_df$nls <- predict(fit_nls, newdata = data.frame(dose = dose_grid))
}

ggplot(dirty_data, aes(x = dose, y = response)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5, color = "#0A1628") +
  {if (!is.null(fit_nls)) geom_line(data = pred_df,
      aes(y = nls, color = "nls (influenced by outlier)"), linewidth = 1.0,
      linetype = "dashed")} +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("nls (influenced by outlier)" = "grey50")) +
  labs(title = "Effect of an Outlier on Standard nls() Fitting",
       subtitle = "The dashed grey line is pulled toward the outlier at 30 nM",
       x = "Concentration (nM)", y = "Response (%)",
       color = "") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)








Figure 9.2: Robust 4PL fitting demonstration: standard nls() (teal) vs an outlier-resistant approach using Tukey’s biweight (when available).







9.11.3 DoseFinding: MCP-Mod for Phase II
The Multiple Comparison Procedures – Modeling (MCP-Mod) framework is an FDA-endorsed method for designing and analyzing Phase II dose-finding studies. It combines multiple contrast tests with model fitting to control Type I error while selecting the best dose-response shape.


Code
library(DoseFinding)

# Simulate Phase II: 6 doses, 20 patients each
set.seed(567)
doses  <- c(0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100)
n_dose <- c(20, 20, 20, 20, 20, 20)
true_means <- 0.2 + 0.7 * doses / (doses + 25)  # Emax shape

dose_df <- data.frame(
  dose     = rep(doses, times = n_dose),
  response = rep(true_means, times = n_dose) + rnorm(sum(n_dose), 0, 0.2)
)

# MCP-Mod: test 5 candidate shapes
cand_models <- Mods(emax = c(10, 50), sigEmax = rbind(c(10, 3), c(30, 5)),
                    linear = NULL, betaMod = c(0.5, 1), doses = doses)

# Perform MCP-Mod
fit_mcpmod <- MCPMod(dose, response, data = dose_df,
                     models = cand_models, Delta = 0.3,
                     selModel = "AIC")

# Plot
plot(fit_mcpmod, complData = TRUE, doseEst = c("ED50"),
     xlab = "Dose (mg)", ylab = "Response") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)


NULL








Figure 9.3: MCP-Mod analysis of a simulated Phase II dose-response study. Five candidate models are tested; the Emax model (teal) is selected as best with significant (p < 0.05) dose-response signal.





The Emax model is selected as best-fitting. The estimated ED50 and its confidence interval provide actionable information for selecting doses to carry forward into Phase III.








Package
Strength
Best For




drc
30+ built-in models, robust fitting
Comparing candidate dose-response shapes


dr4pl
Outlier-resistant 4PL
Noisy assay data with potential outliers


DoseFinding
MCP-Mod framework, sample size planning
Regulatory Phase II dose-finding studies

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter you will be able to:
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
Understand why concentration and effect are often misaligned in time
Build and simulate direct link, effect compartment, and indirect response models
Identify and interpret hysteresis loops
Estimate PK/PD link parameters in R
Choose the appropriate link model for your data
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.2 The PK/PD Disconnect
In many cases, the time course of drug effect does not parallel the time course of plasma concentration. This happens because:

The site of action is not in plasma (effect compartment delay)
The physiological response is indirect (signal transduction takes time)
The drug affects the production or loss of a response mediator

This leads to hysteresis — different concentrations produce different effects depending on whether concentration is rising or falling.


Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulate PK with a delayed PD effect to demonstrate hysteresis
mod_pk <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
params <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10)
event <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)

sim_pk <- rxSolve(mod_pk, params, event %>% et(times))
pk_df <- as.data.frame(sim_pk)

# Add a delayed effect (simple shift)
pk_df <- pk_df %>%
  mutate(Effect = 100 * conc / (5 + conc))  # no delay

# Simulate with effect compartment delay
mod_link <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  d/dt(effect)  <- ke0 * (central/V - effect)
  conc <- central / V
  Ce   <- effect
})

params_link <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10, ke0 = 0.4)
sim_link <- rxSolve(mod_link, params_link, event %>% et(times))
link_df <- as.data.frame(sim_link) %>%
  mutate(Effect_delayed = 100 * Ce / (5 + Ce))

# Hysteresis plot
ggplot(link_df, aes(conc, Effect_delayed)) +
  geom_path(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_point(data = link_df[seq(1, nrow(link_df), by = 5), ],
             aes(conc, Effect_delayed), size = 1, alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Hysteresis Loop: Concentration vs Effect",
       subtitle = "Counter-clockwise hysteresis indicates effect compartment delay",
       x = "Plasma Concentration (mg/L)", y = "Effect") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.3 Direct Link Models
The simplest PK/PD model assumes the effect is directly related to plasma concentration without delay:
E(t) = f(C_p(t))
This works when the site of action equilibrates rapidly with plasma.


Code
# Direct link: Emax model driven by PK
mod_direct <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  conc <- central / V
  effect <- E0 + (Emax * conc) / (EC50 + conc)
})

params_dir <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10,
                E0 = 5, Emax = 90, EC50 = 3)

sim_dir <- rxSolve(mod_direct, params_dir, event %>% et(times))

dir_df <- as.data.frame(sim_dir)

ggplot(dir_df, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc, color = "Concentration"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = effect, color = "Effect"), linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    name = "Concentration (mg/L)",
    sec.axis = sec_axis(~., name = "Effect")
  ) +
  labs(title = "Direct Link PK/PD Model",
       x = "Time (hours)", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.4 Effect Compartment Model
When there is a delay between plasma concentration and effect, we add an effect compartment:
\frac{dC_e}{dt} = k_{e0} \cdot (C_p - C_e)
where k_{e0} is the equilibration rate constant between plasma and effect site.


Code
# Effect compartment model
mod_effect <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  d/dt(effect_c) <- ke0 * (central/V - effect_c)  # effect compartment
  conc <- central / V
  Ce   <- effect_c
  effect <- E0 + (Emax * Ce) / (EC50 + Ce)
})

params_ec <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10,
               ke0 = 0.5, E0 = 5, Emax = 90, EC50 = 3)

sim_ec <- rxSolve(mod_effect, params_ec, event %>% et(times))
ec_df <- as.data.frame(sim_ec)

# Compare plasma concentration and effect-site concentration
ggplot(ec_df, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc, color = "Plasma (Cp)"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = Ce, color = "Effect Site (Ce)"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = effect / 10, color = "Effect (÷10)"), linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Effect Compartment Model",
       subtitle = paste0("ke0 = ", params_ec["ke0"], " 1/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()










10.4.1 Effect of k_{e0} on Hysteresis

Large k_{e0} → rapid equilibration → minimal hysteresis (approaches direct link)
Small k_{e0} → slow equilibration → pronounced hysteresis



Code
# Compare different ke0 values
ke0_values <- c(0.1, 0.5, 2.0, 10)
hysteresis_data <- lapply(ke0_values, function(k) {
  params_k <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10, ke0 = k, E0 = 5, Emax = 90, EC50 = 3)
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_effect, params_k, event %>% et(times))
  as.data.frame(sim) %>% mutate(ke0 = factor(paste0("ke0 = ", k)))
})

df_hyst <- bind_rows(hysteresis_data)

ggplot(df_hyst, aes(conc, effect, color = ke0)) +
  geom_path(linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Hysteresis at Different ke0 Values",
       x = "Plasma Concentration (mg/L)", y = "Effect") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#D4A843", "#C44536", "#0B6E6B", "#1B365D")) +
  facet_wrap(~ke0, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.5 Indirect Response Models
In indirect response models, the drug does not directly cause the effect — it modulates the production (k_{in}) or loss (k_{out}) of an endogenous response mediator:

10.5.1 Basic Indirect Response Model
\frac{dR}{dt} = k_{in} - k_{out} \cdot R
At baseline: R_0 = k_{in} / k_{out}


10.5.2 Four Basic Indirect Response Models








Model
Drug Action
Equation




Type I
Inhibits k_{in}
\frac{dR}{dt} = k_{in} \cdot (1 - \frac{I_{max}C}{IC_{50}+C}) - k_{out}R


Type II
Inhibits k_{out}
\frac{dR}{dt} = k_{in} - k_{out} \cdot (1 - \frac{I_{max}C}{IC_{50}+C}) \cdot R


Type III
Stimulates k_{in}
\frac{dR}{dt} = k_{in} \cdot (1 + \frac{S_{max}C}{SC_{50}+C}) - k_{out}R


Type IV
Stimulates k_{out}
\frac{dR}{dt} = k_{in} - k_{out} \cdot (1 + \frac{S_{max}C}{SC_{50}+C}) \cdot R





Code
# Indirect Response Type I (inhibition of kin)
mod_idr1 <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  C <- central / V

  # Drug effect: inhibition of response production
  inhibition <- Imax * C / (IC50 + C)

  d/dt(R) <- kin * (1 - inhibition) - kout * R
  response <- R
})

# Parameters
params_idr <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10,
                kin = 10, kout = 0.5,  # R0 = kin/kout = 20
                Imax = 0.9, IC50 = 2)

sim_idr <- rxSolve(mod_idr1, params_idr, event %>% et(times))
idr_df <- as.data.frame(sim_idr)

# Plot PK and response
ggplot(idr_df, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = C, color = "Concentration"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = response, color = "Response"), linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Indirect Response Model (Type I)",
       subtitle = "Inhibition of response production (kin)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Value", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()











10.5.3 Characteristic Profiles


Code
# Compare all four indirect response types
simulate_idr <- function(type, params, event, times) {
  mod <- switch(type,
    "I" = rxode2({
      d/dt(depot) <- -ka*depot; d/dt(central) <- ka*depot - ke*central
      C <- central/V; d/dt(R) <- kin*(1 - Imax*C/(IC50+C)) - kout*R; response <- R
    }),
    "II" = rxode2({
      d/dt(depot) <- -ka*depot; d/dt(central) <- ka*depot - ke*central
      C <- central/V; d/dt(R) <- kin - kout*(1 - Imax*C/(IC50+C))*R; response <- R
    }),
    "III" = rxode2({
      d/dt(depot) <- -ka*depot; d/dt(central) <- ka*depot - ke*central
      C <- central/V; d/dt(R) <- kin*(1 + Smax*C/(SC50+C)) - kout*R; response <- R
    }),
    "IV" = rxode2({
      d/dt(depot) <- -ka*depot; d/dt(central) <- ka*depot - ke*central
      C <- central/V; d/dt(R) <- kin - kout*(1 + Smax*C/(SC50+C))*R; response <- R
    })
  )
  sim <- rxSolve(mod, params, event %>% et(times))
  as.data.frame(sim) %>% mutate(Type = paste("Type", type))
}

types <- c("I", "II", "III", "IV")
idr_all <- bind_rows(lapply(types, function(t) {
  p <- c(ka = 1.5, ke = 0.3, V = 10, kin = 10, kout = 0.5,
         Imax = 0.9, IC50 = 2, Smax = 4, SC50 = 2)
  simulate_idr(t, p, event, times)
}))

ggplot(idr_all, aes(time, response, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  facet_wrap(~Type, scales = "free_y") +
  labs(title = "Four Types of Indirect Response Models",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Response") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536", "#D4A843", "#1B365D")) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.6 Disease Progression Models
Disease progression models extend PK/PD by coupling a third state variable — the disease state D(t) — to the drug concentration and effect. This captures the reality that diseases evolve over time, and drugs modify that trajectory.

10.6.1 The Basic Disease Progression ODE
A simple model for disease natural history:
\frac{dD}{dt} = \alpha - \beta \cdot D
where \alpha is the disease progression rate and \beta is the natural recovery/remission rate. At steady state without treatment: D_{ss} = \alpha/\beta.
Drug effect is coupled in through the effect E(t):
\frac{dD}{dt} = \alpha - \beta \cdot D - \gamma \cdot E(t)
where \gamma quantifies how strongly the drug effect translates into disease modification.


10.6.2 Symptomatic vs Disease-Modifying Effects








Effect Type
Mathematical Signature
Clinical Implication




Symptomatic
Effect on D reverses when drug stops
Disease returns to natural trajectory


Disease-modifying
Effect persists or slows \alpha permanently
Long-term benefit beyond drug exposure





Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Disease progression coupled with PK/PD
mod_disease <- rxode2({
  # PK: one-compartment oral
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - ke * central
  C <- central / V
  
  # PD: Emax effect driven by concentration
  E <- Emax * C / (EC50 + C)
  
  # Disease state
  d/dt(D) <- alpha - beta * D - gamma_drug * E
  
  # Outputs
  conc    <- C
  effect  <- E
  disease <- D
})

# Parameters
params_disease <- c(
  ka = 0.9, ke = 0.22, V = 28,         # PK
  Emax = -4, EC50 = 3.5,               # PD (negative = improvement)
  alpha = 0.35, beta = 0.035,          # Disease: steady state D = 10
  gamma_drug = 0.8                     # Drug effect coupling
)

times <- seq(0, 72, by = 0.2)
event <- et(time = 0, amt = 200) %>%
  et(seq(24, 72, 24), amt = rep(200, 3))  # daily dosing

sim_disease <- rxSolve(mod_disease, params_disease, event %>% et(times))
df_disease <- as.data.frame(sim_disease)

# Plot all three coupled states
ggplot(df_disease, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = disease, color = "Disease State (D)"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc * 5, color = "Conc (×5)"), linewidth = 0.8, alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Disease Progression with PK/PD Coupling",
       subtitle = "Drug slows disease progression; symptomatic relief reverses when drug stops",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Disease Severity", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()











10.6.3 The General Pattern: Coupled Multi-State ODE Systems
Once you have concentration C(t) and effect E(t) as ODE states, you can couple in additional states:

Enzyme level (induction): dE_{enz}/dt = k_{syn} \cdot (1 + I_{max}C/(IC_{50}+C)) - k_{deg} \cdot E_{enz}
Second drug (DDI): dC_B/dt = \dots with combined or competitive clearance
Biomarker: dM/dt = k_{in} \cdot f(C) - k_{out} \cdot M

The pattern is always the same: each new state’s derivative is a function of the existing states, and the whole system is solved with a single ode() call.


10.6.4 The Identifiability Risk
As you couple more states, the parameter count multiplies rapidly. A PK+PD+disease model may have 10+ parameters. When the number of informative data points doesn’t grow proportionally, parameters become unidentifiable — the model can produce the same fit with very different parameter sets. Fix parameters obtainable from independent sources (e.g., V from NCA, \alpha from natural history studies) before estimating the rest.
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
10  PK/PD Link Models

title: 10  PK/PD Link Models
text:
10.7 Choosing a PK/PD Link Model

10.7.1 Decision Flow

Plot concentration vs effect over time — are they in phase?

Yes → Direct link model
No → Check hysteresis direction

Plot concentration vs effect (hysteresis plot)

No loop → Direct link
Counter-clockwise loop → Effect compartment model
Clockwise loop → Tolerance or indirect response

Consider mechanism

Receptor-mediated, rapid onset → Direct link or effect compartment
Physiological mediator turnover → Indirect response
Gene expression, protein synthesis → Signal transduction cascade




10.7.2 Exercises

Simulate an effect compartment model with k_{e0} = 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 3.0. How does the peak effect time change?
Create a hysteresis plot (concentration vs effect) for an effect compartment model and identify the direction of the loop.
Simulate an indirect response Type I model. What happens to the response when k_{out} is doubled?
Explain why a clockwise hysteresis loop suggests tolerance rather than effect compartment delay.



10.7.3 Common Mistakes

Assuming direct link by default — always check for hysteresis
Misinterpreting hysteresis direction: Counter-clockwise = distribution delay; Clockwise = tolerance
Using wrong indirect response type: The four types produce different response profiles
Ignoring baseline turnover: R_0 = k_{in}/k_{out} must match observed baseline



10.7.4 Key Takeaway
PK/PD link models bridge the gap between drug concentration and effect. Master the three main types: direct link (no delay), effect compartment (distributional delay), and indirect response (pharmacological delay). Always visualize hysteresis before choosing your model.

crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK

title: 11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK
text:
11.2 When Linearity Breaks Down
Most PK models assume first-order (linear) elimination: rate \propto concentration. But many drugs show saturable (nonlinear) PK at therapeutic doses because:

Metabolizing enzymes become saturated (Michaelis–Menten kinetics)
Protein binding is saturable (only free drug is cleared)
Target binding is saturable (target-mediated drug disposition)
Transporters become saturated (renal secretion, biliary excretion)


11.2.1 Linear vs Nonlinear PK



Property
Linear PK
Nonlinear PK




AUC vs Dose
Proportional
More than proportional


t_{1/2}
Constant
Dose-dependent


CL
Constant
Dose-dependent


Superposition
Applies
Does NOT apply
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK

title: 11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK
text:
11.3 Michaelis–Menten Elimination

11.3.1 Model Equation
\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{V_{max} \cdot C}{K_m + C}
For IV bolus: \frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{V_{max} \cdot C}{K_m + C}, \quad C(0) = \frac{D}{V}
where: - V_{max}: Maximum elimination rate (amount/time) - K_m: Michaelis constant (concentration at half V_{max})


Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Michaelis-Menten elimination model
mod_mm <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -Vmax * central / (Km * V + central)
  conc <- central / V
})

# Simulate at different doses
doses <- c(50, 200, 800, 3200)
times <- seq(0, 48, by = 0.1)
params_mm <- c(Vmax = 50, Km = 5, V = 10)  # Vmax mg/h, Km mg/L, V L

sims_mm <- lapply(doses, function(d) {
  event <- et(time = 0, amt = d)
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_mm, params_mm, event %>% et(times))
  as.data.frame(sim) %>% mutate(Dose = factor(paste(d, "mg")))
})

df_mm <- bind_rows(sims_mm)

ggplot(df_mm, aes(time, conc, color = Dose)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Michaelis–Menten Elimination: Effect of Dose",
       subtitle = "Higher doses show slower elimination (saturation)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#2E8B7E", "#D4A843", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()











11.3.2 When Does Nonlinearity Matter?

When concentrations exceed K_m (the Michaelis constant)
At low concentrations (C \ll K_m): Elimination approaches first-order with k \approx V_{max}/K_m
At high concentrations (C \gg K_m): Elimination approaches zero-order at rate V_{max}



Code
# Demonstrate the transition from first-order to zero-order
C <- seq(0.1, 100, length.out = 200)
Vmax <- 50
Km <- 5

elim_rate_mm <- Vmax * C / (Km + C)          # MM rate (amount/h)
elim_rate_1st <- (Vmax / Km) * C              # First-order approximation (low C)
elim_rate_0th <- rep(Vmax, length(C))          # Zero-order approximation (high C)

df_rates <- data.frame(
  C = rep(C, 3),
  Rate = c(elim_rate_mm, elim_rate_1st, elim_rate_0th),
  Model = rep(c("Michaelis-Menten", "First-order (Vmax/Km)", "Zero-order (Vmax)"),
              each = length(C))
)

ggplot(df_rates, aes(C, Rate, color = Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = Km, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = Km * 1.5, y = Vmax * 0.3, label = "Km", hjust = 0) +
  labs(title = "Michaelis–Menten: Transition from First- to Zero-Order",
       x = "Concentration (mg/L)", y = "Elimination Rate (mg/h)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#D4A843", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK

title: 11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK
text:
11.4 Saturable Protein Binding
Many drugs bind to plasma proteins (albumin, α1-acid glycoprotein). Only unbound (free) drug is pharmacologically active and available for elimination.

11.4.1 Model for Saturable Binding
C_{bound} = \frac{B_{max} \cdot C_{free}}{K_D + C_{free}}
where B_{max} is the maximum binding capacity and K_D is the dissociation constant.


Code
# Saturable protein binding model
C_free <- seq(0.01, 200, length.out = 300)
Bmax <- 100  # maximum binding capacity (mg/L)
KD <- 5      # dissociation constant (mg/L)

C_bound <- Bmax * C_free / (KD + C_free)
C_total <- C_free + C_bound
fu <- C_free / C_total  # fraction unbound

df_binding <- data.frame(
  C_free = rep(C_free, 3),
  Value = c(C_free, C_bound, C_total),
  Component = rep(c("Free", "Bound", "Total"), each = length(C_free))
)

ggplot(df_binding, aes(C_free, Value, color = Component)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Saturable Protein Binding",
       subtitle = paste0("Bmax = ", Bmax, " mg/L, KD = ", KD, " mg/L"),
       x = "Free Concentration (mg/L)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536", "#1B365D")) +
  theme_minimal()









Code
# Fraction unbound vs concentration
df_fu <- data.frame(C_free = C_free, fu = fu)

ggplot(df_fu, aes(C_free, fu)) +
  geom_line(color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Fraction Unbound vs Concentration",
       subtitle = "fu increases as binding sites become saturated",
       x = "Total Concentration (mg/L)", y = "Fraction Unbound (fu)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK

title: 11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK
text:
11.5 Target-Mediated Drug Disposition (TMDD)
TMDD occurs when a significant fraction of the drug binds to its pharmacological target (e.g., receptor, enzyme). This binding contributes to both distribution and elimination.

11.5.1 Minimal TMDD Model


Code
# Simplified TMDD model
mod_tmdd <- rxode2({
  # Free drug in central compartment
  d/dt(C) <- -ke * C - kon * C * R + koff * RC

  # Free receptor
  d/dt(R) <- ksyn - kdeg * R - kon * C * R + koff * RC

  # Drug-receptor complex
  d/dt(RC) <- kon * C * R - koff * RC - kint * RC

  conc_free <- C
  conc_total <- C + RC
})

params_tmdd <- c(
  ke = 0.1,    # elimination rate of free drug
  kon = 0.5,   # binding rate
  koff = 0.1,  # dissociation rate
  ksyn = 1.0,  # receptor synthesis rate
  kdeg = 0.2,  # receptor degradation rate
  kint = 0.3   # internalization rate of complex
)

V <- 5

# Initial conditions
inits_tmdd <- c(C = 100 / V, R = params_tmdd[["ksyn"]] / params_tmdd[["kdeg"]], RC = 0)

times <- seq(0, 72, by = 0.1)
event <- et(time = 0, amt = 100)

sim_tmdd <- rxSolve(mod_tmdd, c(params_tmdd, V = V), event %>% et(times), inits = inits_tmdd)
tmdd_df <- as.data.frame(sim_tmdd)

# Plot
ggplot(tmdd_df, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc_free, color = "Free Drug"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc_total, color = "Total Drug"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = R * 10, color = "Receptor (×10)"), linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Target-Mediated Drug Disposition",
       subtitle = "Drug binding to receptor affects PK profile",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part III — Pharmacodynamics and PK/PD Linkage
11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK

title: 11  Nonlinear and Saturable PK
text:
11.6 Detecting Nonlinear PK

11.6.1 Methods to Identify Nonlinearity

Dose proportionality check: Plot AUC vs dose — should be linear
Dose-normalized profiles: Overlay C/D vs time — should superimpose for linear PK
Multiple-dose comparison: Check if superposition principle holds



Code
# Check dose proportionality
calc_auc <- function(sim) {
  sum(diff(sim$time) * (sim$conc[-1] + sim$conc[-nrow(sim)]) / 2)
}

doses_check <- c(10, 30, 100, 300, 1000)
auc_values <- sapply(doses_check, function(d) {
  event <- et(time = 0, amt = d)
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_mm, params_mm, event %>% et(times))
  calc_auc(as.data.frame(sim))
})

df_auc <- data.frame(Dose = doses_check, AUC = auc_values) %>%
  mutate(Dose_Normalized_AUC = AUC / Dose)

knitr::kable(df_auc, caption = "Dose Proportionality Check",
             col.names = c("Dose (mg)", "AUC (mg·h/L)", "Dose-Normalized AUC"))



Dose Proportionality Check


Dose (mg)
AUC (mg·h/L)
Dose-Normalized AUC

Code
ggplot(df_auc, aes(Dose, Dose_Normalized_AUC)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_line(color = "#C44536") +
  labs(title = "Dose-Normalized AUC vs Dose",
       subtitle = "Non-horizontal line indicates nonlinear PK",
       x = "Dose (mg)", y = "AUC / Dose") +
  theme_minimal()












11.6.2 Exercises

Simulate Michaelis–Menten elimination at doses of 10, 50, 200, and 1000 mg. Compare t_{1/2} (time to halve concentration) across doses.
At what concentration does the MM elimination rate reach 90% of V_{max}?
Simulate saturable protein binding and plot total vs free concentration. What happens to the fraction unbound at high concentrations?
For TMDD, explain why the PK profile appears nonlinear at low doses but approximately linear at very high doses.



11.6.3 Common Mistakes

Assuming linear PK without checking dose proportionality
Using first-order models for drugs with known nonlinearity (e.g., phenytoin, high-dose methotrexate)
Ignoring K_m relative to therapeutic concentrations: If C_{therapeutic} \approx K_m, nonlinearity is significant
Extrapolating low-dose linear PK to high doses without checking for saturation
Confusing saturable protein binding with saturable elimination — they have different PK signatures



11.6.4 Key Takeaway
Nonlinear PK arises when elimination, binding, or distribution processes become saturated. Michaelis–Menten kinetics is the most common form. Always check for dose proportionality before assuming linear PK — especially for drugs with narrow therapeutic windows or high-dose regimens.

title: 12  Variability and Population PK

Interpret population parameters, individual predictions, and shrinkage
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
text:
12.2 Why Population PK?
In classical PK, we analyze one subject at a time (individual PK). But in drug development, we need to:

Analyze sparse data (1–3 samples per subject from hundreds of patients)
Quantify between-subject variability (why does CL vary 5-fold?)
Identify covariates (weight, age, renal function) that explain variability
Support dose individualization

Population PK (PopPK) uses nonlinear mixed-effects models to analyze all subjects simultaneously.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
text:
12.3 The Population Model Structure

12.3.1 Hierarchical Model
Level 1 — Structural (PK) model: C_{ij} = f(t_{ij}, \theta_i) + \varepsilon_{ij}
Level 2 — Between-subject variability (BSV): \theta_i = \theta_{pop} \cdot e^{\eta_i}, \quad \eta_i \sim N(0, \omega^2)
Level 3 — Covariate effects: \theta_i = \theta_{pop} \cdot \left(\frac{WT_i}{70}\right)^{\beta_{WT}} \cdot e^{\eta_i}
Level 4 — Residual error: \varepsilon_{ij} \sim N(0, \sigma^2) \quad \text{or} \quad C_{ij} = f(t_{ij}, \theta_i) \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_{ij})


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Conceptual visualization: Between-subject variability
set.seed(123)
n_subj <- 50
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.5)

# Generate individual PK profiles with variability in CL and V
pop_CL <- 5     # L/h
pop_V  <- 50    # L

# Log-normal BSV: ω_CL = 0.3, ω_V = 0.2
set.seed(42)

sim_pop <- expand.grid(time = times, ID = 1:n_subj) %>%
  mutate(
    eta_CL = rep(rnorm(n_subj, 0, 0.3), each = length(times)),
    eta_V  = rep(rnorm(n_subj, 0, 0.2), each = length(times)),
    CL_ind = pop_CL * exp(eta_CL),
    V_ind  = pop_V * exp(eta_V),
    ke_ind = CL_ind / V_ind,
    dose   = 500,
    conc   = (dose / V_ind) * exp(-ke_ind * time),
    conc_obs = conc * exp(rnorm(n(), 0, 0.1))
  )

# Plot individual profiles
ggplot(sim_pop, aes(time, conc, group = ID)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  labs(title = "Population PK: 50 Simulated Subjects",
       subtitle = "Between-subject variability in CL (ω=0.3) and V (ω=0.2)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
text:
12.4 Population PK with nlmixr2


Code
library(nlmixr2)

# Define a one-compartment model for nlmixr2
pop_model <- function() {
  ini({
    # Population parameters (fixed effects)
    tka <- log(1.0)     # log ka
    tcl <- log(5.0)     # log CL
    tv  <- log(50)      # log V

    # Between-subject variability (random effects)
    eta.ka ~ 0.3
    eta.cl ~ 0.3
    eta.v  ~ 0.2

    # Residual error
    add.err <- 1
    prop.err <- 0.1
  })
  model({
    ka <- exp(tka + eta.ka)
    cl <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v  <- exp(tv + eta.v)

    d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
    d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - (cl/v) * central
    cp <- central / v
    cp ~ add(add.err) + prop(prop.err)
  })
}




Code
# Simulate sparse population PK data
set.seed(123)
n_subjects <- 100
sparse_times <- c(0.5, 2, 6, 24)  # Typical sparse sampling

pk_sparse <- expand.grid(ID = 1:n_subjects, time = sparse_times) %>%
  arrange(ID, time) %>%
  mutate(
    eta_CL = rep(rnorm(n_subjects, 0, 0.3), each = length(sparse_times)),
    eta_V  = rep(rnorm(n_subjects, 0, 0.2), each = length(sparse_times)),
    CL = 5 * exp(eta_CL),
    V  = 50 * exp(eta_V),
    ke = CL / V,
    DOSE = 500,
    conc = (DOSE / V) * exp(-ke * time),
    conc_obs = conc * exp(rnorm(n(), 0, 0.15)),  # 15% CV residual error
    CENS = 0  # Not censored
  ) %>%
  filter(conc_obs > 0.01)  # Remove BLOQ

12.4.1 Fitting the Population Model


Code
# Fit the model (requires nlmixr2)
fit_pop <- tryCatch(
  nlmixr2(
    pop_model,
    data = pk_sparse,
    est = "saem",
    control = saemControl(nBurn = 100, nEm = 100)
  ),
  error = function(e) {
    message("SAEM fit skipped (requires longer runtime). Error: ", e$message)
    NULL
  }
)


Error in .foceiPreProcessData(.data, .ret, .ui, .control$rxControl) : 
  Assertion on 'names(data)' failed: Names must include the elements {'DV','TIME'}, but is missing elements {'DV'}.


Code
# View results
if (!is.null(fit_pop)) {
  print(fit_pop)
  summary(fit_pop)
} else {
  cat("Model fitting was not performed — set control options for full estimation.\n")
}

text:
12.5 Covariate Modeling

12.5.1 Common Covariates in PK



Covariate
Typical Effect On
Functional Form




Body weight (WT)
CL, V
Power: (WT/70)^{0.75} for CL


Age
CL (maturation, decline)
Power or exponential


Renal function (eGFR)
CL (renal drugs)
Linear or power


Sex
V, CL
Categorical


Genotype (CYP450)
CL (metabolized drugs)
Categorical


Disease status
CL, V
Categorical





Code
# Covariate model simulation: Effect of weight on CL
weights <- seq(40, 120, by = 5)
ref_wt <- 70
CL_ref <- 5.0
theta_wt <- 0.75  # allometric exponent for CL

CL_by_wt <- CL_ref * (weights / ref_wt)^theta_wt

df_cov <- data.frame(Weight = weights, CL = CL_by_wt)

ggplot(df_cov, aes(Weight, CL)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 2, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = ref_wt, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "Allometric Scaling of Clearance with Body Weight",
       subtitle = expression(CL == CL[ref] %.% (WT/70)^0.75),
       x = "Body Weight (kg)", y = "Clearance (L/h)") +
  theme_minimal()

Code
# Visualize sparse vs rich sampling designs
rich_times <- c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 1.5, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)
sparse_times_viz <- c(0.5, 2, 6, 24)

sample_designs <- data.frame(
  time = c(rich_times, sparse_times_viz),
  conc = 10 * exp(-0.3 * c(rich_times, sparse_times_viz)),
  Design = rep(c("Rich (n=1–30)", "Sparse (n=100+)"),
               c(length(rich_times), length(sparse_times_viz)))
)

  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_y_log10() +

crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
text:
12.7 Model Diagnostics for Population PK

12.7.1 Key Diagnostics

Observed vs Predicted: Individual predictions (IPRED) should follow identity
Residuals vs Predicted: No trends, symmetric around zero
Distribution of random effects (\eta): Should be approximately normal
Shrinkage: High shrinkage (\eta-shrinkage > 20–30%) reduces reliability of individual estimates



Code
# Simulate diagnostic plots
set.seed(456)
n <- 200
pred <- runif(n, 0.1, 50)
obs <- pred * exp(rnorm(n, 0, 0.2))

diag_df <- data.frame(
  PRED = pred,
  IPRED = obs,
  CWRES = (log(obs) - log(pred)) / 0.2
)

# Observed vs Predicted
p1 <- ggplot(diag_df, aes(PRED, IPRED)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "#C44536", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(title = "Observed vs Population Predicted", x = "PRED", y = "DV") +
  theme_minimal()

# Residuals vs Predicted
p2 <- ggplot(diag_df, aes(PRED, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dotted", alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs Predicted", x = "PRED", y = "CWRES") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
p1 + p2
