12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
text:
12.8 Shrinkage
Shrinkage occurs when individual data are sparse and individual estimates “shrink” toward the population mean:
\eta\text{-shrinkage} = 1 - \frac{var(\hat{\eta}_i)}{\omega^2}

Low shrinkage (< 10%): Individual estimates are reliable
Moderate shrinkage (10–30%): Caution needed for covariate selection using EBEs
High shrinkage (> 30%): Individual estimates unreliable; use population predictions



12.8.1 Exercises

Simulate a population PK dataset with 200 subjects, 3 samples each. Fit using nlmixr2 and report \omega_{CL} and \omega_V.
Add body weight as a covariate on CL. Does it reduce \omega_{CL}? By how much?
Generate diagnostic plots for a fitted model. What patterns would indicate model misspecification?
Explain why shrinkage matters for covariate model building.
Compare rich vs sparse sampling designs: which gives better estimates of \omega^2?



12.8.2 Common Mistakes

Using individual PK methods on sparse data: Leads to biased, imprecise estimates
Ignoring shrinkage: EBEs with high shrinkage should not be used for covariate screening
Overfitting covariates: Each covariate consumes degrees of freedom; use scientific rationale
Assuming log-normal BSV for all parameters: check ETA distributions
Not checking parameter correlations: High correlations can destabilize the model



12.8.3 Key Takeaway
Population PK uses mixed-effects models to analyze all subjects simultaneously, quantifying both population-typical parameters and between-subject variability. Covariates like weight and renal function explain variability and enable dose individualization. Always check diagnostics and shrinkage before interpreting individual estimates.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
12  Variability and Population PK

title: 12  Variability and Population PK
section: 12.9 Alternative Estimation Methods: SAEM and nlme
text:
12.9 Alternative Estimation Methods: SAEM and nlme
nlmixr2’s FOCE is excellent, but other algorithms have strengths for certain problems. Let’s compare SAEM (Stochastic Approximation Expectation-Maximization, via saemix) and the classic nlme package on a simulated PopPK dataset.

12.9.1 Simulated PopPK Dataset


Code
library(nlmixr2)
library(saemix)
library(nlme)

# Simulate 1-cmt oral PopPK: 50 subjects, sparse sampling
set.seed(1234)
n_subj <- 50
pop_pk <- data.frame()
for (i in 1:n_subj) {
  CL_i  <- 5  * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.3))   # BSV 30%
  V_i   <- 80 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.25))  # BSV 25%
  ka_i  <- 1.2
  t_i   <- sort(c(0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24))
  conc_i <- (100 / V_i) * (ka_i / (ka_i - CL_i/V_i)) *
    (exp(-(CL_i/V_i) * t_i) - exp(-ka_i * t_i)) +
    rnorm(length(t_i), 0, 0.5)
  pop_pk <- rbind(pop_pk,
    data.frame(ID = i, TIME = t_i, DV = pmax(conc_i, 0.01),
               AMT = 0, EVID = 0))
  pop_pk <- rbind(pop_pk,
    data.frame(ID = i, TIME = 0, DV = 0, AMT = 100, EVID = 1))
}
pop_pk <- pop_pk[order(pop_pk$ID, pop_pk$TIME), ]

# ---- nlmixr2 (FOCE) ----
fit_nlmixr <- tryCatch({
  mod_nlmixr <- nlmixr2({
    ka     <- exp(tka + eta.ka)
    cl     <- exp(tcl + eta.cl)
    v      <- exp(tv  + eta.v)
    d/dt(A1) <- -ka * A1
    d/dt(A2) <-  ka * A1 - (cl/v) * A2
    cp <- A2 / v
    cp ~ add(add.sd)
  })
  nlmixr(mod_nlmixr, pop_pk, est = "focei",
         control = foceiControl(maxOuterIterations = 0))
}, error = function(e) {
  message("nlmixr2 FOCE fitting failed: ", e$message)
  NULL
})

# ---- saemix ----
fit_saem <- tryCatch({
  saemix_data <- saemixData(
    name.data = pop_pk[pop_pk$EVID == 0, c("ID", "TIME", "DV")],
    name.group = "ID", name.predictors = "TIME",
    name.response = "DV"
  )
  
  model_saemix <- function(psi, id, xidep) {
    CL <- psi[id, 1]; V <- psi[id, 2]; ka <- psi[id, 3]
    (100 / V) * (ka / (ka - CL/V)) * (exp(-(CL/V) * xidep) - exp(-ka * xidep))
  }
  
  fit_saemix <- saemixModel(
    model = model_saemix,
    psi0  = c(CL = 5, V = 80, ka = 1.2),
    omega.init = diag(c(0.09, 0.06, 0.01)),
    error.model = "constant"
  )
  
  saemix(fit_saemix, saemix_data,
         saemix.options(seed = 123, displayProgress = FALSE))
}, error = function(e) NULL)




The following SaemixData object was successfully created:

Object of class SaemixData
    longitudinal data for use with the SAEM algorithm
Dataset pop_pk[pop_pk$EVID == 0, c("ID", "TIME", "DV")] 
    Structured data: DV ~ TIME | ID 
    Predictor: TIME () 
Warning: psi0 given as a vector, reshaping it to a matrix.


The following SaemixModel object was successfully created:

Nonlinear mixed-effects model
  Model function
  Model type:  structural
function (psi, id, xidep) 
{
    CL <- psi[id, 1]
    V <- psi[id, 2]
    ka <- psi[id, 3]
    (100/V) * (ka/(ka - CL/V)) * (exp(-(CL/V) * xidep) - exp(-ka * 
        xidep))
}
  Nb of parameters: 3 
      parameter names:  CL V ka 
      distribution:
     Parameter Distribution Estimated
[1,] CL        normal       Estimated
[2,] V         normal       Estimated
[3,] ka        normal       Estimated
  Variance-covariance matrix:
   CL V ka
CL  1 0  0
V   0 1  0
ka  0 0  1
  Error model: constant , initial values: a.1=1 
    No covariate in the model.
    Initial values
             CL  V  ka
Pop.CondInit  5 80 1.2


Code
# ---- nlme ----
fit_nlme <- tryCatch({
  lme_model <- function(CL, V, ka, TIME) {
    (100 / V) * (ka / (ka - CL/V)) * (exp(-(CL/V) * TIME) - exp(-ka * TIME))
  }
  
  nlme(DV ~ lme_model(CL, V, ka, TIME),
       data   = pop_pk[pop_pk$EVID == 0, ],
       fixed  = CL + V + ka ~ 1,
       random = CL + V ~ 1 | ID,
       start  = c(CL = 5, V = 80, ka = 1.2),
       method = "ML")
}, error = function(e) NULL)

# ---- Parameter comparison table ----
est_table <- data.frame(
  Parameter = c("CL (L/h)", "V (L)", "ka (1/h)"),
  Truth     = c(5.0, 80, 1.2),
  FOCE      = if (!is.null(fit_nlmixr)) round(fixef(fit_nlmixr)["tcl"] |> exp(), 1) else NA,
  SAEM      = if (!is.null(fit_saem)) round(fit_saem@results@fixed.effects[1:3], 1) else NA,
  nlme      = if (!is.null(fit_nlme)) round(fixef(fit_nlme), 1) else NA
)
knitr::kable(est_table, caption = "Parameter estimates from 3 PopPK methods")

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.3 Choosing the Structural Model

13.3.1 Decision Criteria







Criterion
How to Assess




Scientific plausibility
Does the model reflect known physiology?


Data support
Can the data distinguish between models?


Objective function (OFV)
Lower OFV = better fit


AIC/BIC
Penalizes for extra parameters


Goodness-of-fit plots
Visual assessment of fit quality


Parameter precision
RSE% should be reasonable (< 30–50%)





Code
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate data from a two-compartment model
set.seed(123)
sample_times <- c(0.05, 0.1, 0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24)
true_A <- 15; true_alpha <- 2.5
true_B <- 8;  true_beta  <- 0.25

obs <- data.frame(
  time = sample_times,
  conc = true_A * exp(-true_alpha * sample_times) +
         true_B * exp(-true_beta * sample_times)
)
obs$conc <- obs$conc * exp(rnorm(nrow(obs), 0, 0.1))

# Fit one-compartment
fit1 <- nls(conc ~ C0 * exp(-ke * time), data = obs,
            start = list(C0 = 20, ke = 0.3))

# Fit two-compartment
fit2 <- nls(conc ~ A * exp(-alpha * time) + B * exp(-beta * time),
            data = obs,
            start = list(A = 15, alpha = 2, B = 8, beta = 0.2),
            lower = c(0, 0, 0, 0), algorithm = "port")

# Model comparison
model_comp <- data.frame(
  Model = c("One-Compartment", "Two-Compartment"),
  AIC = round(c(AIC(fit1), AIC(fit2)), 1),
  BIC = round(c(AIC(fit1) + 2*log(nrow(obs)), AIC(fit2) + 4*log(nrow(obs))), 1),
  Parameters = c(2, 4)
)
knitr::kable(model_comp, caption = "Model Comparison")



Model Comparison


Model
AIC
BIC

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.4 Residual Error Models

13.4.1 Common Error Models








Model
Equation
When to Use




Additive
Y = F + \varepsilon
Constant error across concentrations


Proportional
Y = F \cdot (1 + \varepsilon)
Error scales with concentration


Combined
Y = F \cdot (1 + \varepsilon_1) + \varepsilon_2
Both components present


Log-additive
\log(Y) = \log(F) + \varepsilon
Lognormal error, PK default





Code
# Visualize different error models
conc_true <- 10^seq(-1, 2, length.out = 100)
set.seed(123)

add_err  <- 2
prop_err <- 0.2

df_errors <- data.frame(
  conc = rep(conc_true, 3),
  Error_Model = rep(c("Additive (σ=2)", "Proportional (σ=20%)", "Combined"), each = 100),
  SD = c(rep(add_err, 100),
         prop_err * conc_true,
         sqrt(add_err^2 + (prop_err * conc_true)^2))
)

ggplot(df_errors, aes(conc, SD, color = Error_Model)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(title = "Residual Error: Standard Deviation vs Concentration",
       x = "True Concentration (log scale)", y = "Residual SD") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.5 Goodness-of-Fit Plots

13.5.1 The Four Essential Plots


Code
# Generate example diagnostic data
set.seed(42)
n <- 100
pred <- runif(n, 0.5, 50)
obs <- pred * exp(rnorm(n, 0, 0.2))
ipred <- obs  # simplified
time <- 1:n

diag_data <- data.frame(
  DV = obs, PRED = pred, IPRED = ipred,
  CWRES = rnorm(n, 0, 1), TIME = time,
  IWRES = (log(obs) - log(pred)) / 0.2
)

# 1. DV vs PRED
p1 <- ggplot(diag_data, aes(PRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "#C44536", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#D4A843", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "DV vs PRED", x = "Population Predicted", y = "Observed") +
  theme_minimal()

# 2. DV vs IPRED
p2 <- ggplot(diag_data, aes(IPRED, DV)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, color = "#C44536", linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#D4A843", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "DV vs IPRED", x = "Individual Predicted", y = "Observed") +
  theme_minimal()

# 3. CWRES vs PRED
p3 <- ggplot(diag_data, aes(PRED, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = c(-2, 2), linetype = "dotted", alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs PRED", x = "Population Predicted", y = "CWRES") +
  theme_minimal()

# 4. CWRES vs TIME
p4 <- ggplot(diag_data, aes(TIME, CWRES)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6, color = "#C44536") +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_smooth(method = "loess", se = FALSE, color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "CWRES vs TIME", x = "Time", y = "CWRES") +
  theme_minimal()

library(patchwork)
(p1 + p2) / (p3 + p4)

13  Model Building and Diagnostics

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.6 Visual Predictive Check (VPC)
The VPC simulates data from the final model and compares the observed data distribution to the simulated distribution:


Code
# Simulate a VPC
library(rxode2)

set.seed(42)
n_sim <- 500
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.5)

# Simulate from a reference model
mod_ref <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - (CL/V) * central
  conc <- central / V
})

# Population parameters with variability
pop_params <- c(ka = 1.0, CL = 5, V = 50)

vpc_data <- lapply(1:n_sim, function(i) {
  params <- c(
    ka = 1.0 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)),
    CL = 5.0 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.3)),
    V  = 50 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.2))
  )
  event <- et(time = 0, amt = 500)
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_ref, params, event %>% et(times))
  as.data.frame(sim) %>% mutate(sim_id = i)
})

vpc_df <- bind_rows(vpc_data)

# Compute percentiles of simulated data
vpc_summary <- vpc_df %>%
  group_by(time) %>%
  summarise(
    p05 = quantile(conc, 0.05),
    p50 = quantile(conc, 0.50),
    p95 = quantile(conc, 0.95),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

# Generate "observed" data (a single trial)
set.seed(99)
obs_data <- data.frame(
  time = rep(c(0.5, 1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24), each = 5),
  ID = rep(1:5, 8)
) %>%
  mutate(
    conc = 500/50 * exp(-5/50 * time) * exp(rnorm(n(), 0, 0.2))
  )

# VPC plot
ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = vpc_summary, aes(time, ymin = p05, ymax = p95),
              fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.15) +
  geom_line(data = vpc_summary, aes(time, p50),
            color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_point(data = obs_data, aes(time, conc, group = ID),
             color = "#C44536", size = 1.5, alpha = 0.6) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Visual Predictive Check (VPC)",
       subtitle = "Shaded area: 90% prediction interval; Dashed: median; Points: observed",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.7 Bootstrap for Uncertainty
Bootstrap resampling provides non-parametric confidence intervals for parameter estimates:


Code
# Simplified bootstrap example
n_boot <- 200
bootstrap_params <- matrix(NA, nrow = n_boot, ncol = 3)

for (i in 1:n_boot) {
  # Resample subjects with replacement
  boot_ids <- sample(unique(pk_data$ID), replace = TRUE)

  boot_data <- do.call(rbind, lapply(seq_along(boot_ids), function(j) {
    orig_data <- pk_data[pk_data$ID == boot_ids[j], ]
    orig_data$ID <- j  # Rename to avoid duplicates
    orig_data
  }))

  # Fit model to bootstrap sample and store parameters
  # bootstrap_params[i, ] <- coef(fit_boot)
}

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.8 Model Selection Criteria



Criterion
Formula
Notes




OFV
-2 \times \log(Likelihood)
Lower = better


AIC
OFV + 2p
Penalizes for p parameters


BIC
OFV + p \cdot \ln(n)
Stronger penalty for large n


ΔOFV
OFV_1 - OFV_2
\sim \chi^2 with df = p_2 - p_1



For nested models, a drop in OFV > 3.84 (p < 0.05, 1 df) is considered significant.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
13  Model Building and Diagnostics

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.9 Model Selection Decision Tree



Model Selection Decision Tree: Four Key Branching Points







Step
Yes
No




Is clearance constant (linear PK)?
→ Analytical 1-/2-compartment (Ch 6-7)
→ ODE with MM/induction/inhibition (Ch 11, 16)


Does effect lag concentration (hysteresis)?
→ Add effect compartment (Ch 10)
→ Direct Cp→E Emax/Hill model (Ch 9)


Multiple subjects with variability to explain?
→ NLME / Covariate model (Ch 12, 16)
→ Standard NLS/MLE (Ch 9, 13)


Need uncertainty quantification / priors?
→ Bayesian MCMC/Stan (Ch 18)
→ Frequentist NLS/MLE/NLME suffices
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
13  Model Building and Diagnostics

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
section: 13.10 Identifiability: Can Your Data Support Your Model?
text:
13.10 Identifiability: Can Your Data Support Your Model?
Identifiability asks whether unique parameter values can be recovered from available data. An unidentifiable model can produce the same fit with very different parameter combinations — making estimates meaningless.

13.10.1 Identifiability Red Flags

Too many parameters for the data: Estimating > 4-5 parameters simultaneously from one dataset without fixing any externally-derivable values (e.g., V from NCA, k_a from literature)
Coupled multi-state systems: PK+PD+disease or two-drug DDI models where the parameter count grows faster than informative data points. Each new state typically adds 2-4 parameters.
Flat or ridge-shaped likelihood/posterior: Check via profile likelihood (fix one parameter at a range of values, re-optimize the rest, watch OFV) or posterior pairs plots — if the OFV barely changes as a parameter varies over a wide range, that parameter is unidentifiable.
High parameter correlations: |\rho| > 0.95 between estimates (e.g., CL and V in oral dosing without IV data). The correlation matrix from nls/nlme output is your first diagnostic.
k_a and k_e in oral data: Without early sampling (< 30 min post-dose), these two rate constants are notoriously confounded — the “flip-flop” phenomenon.



13.10.2 The Sensitivity Triage Principle
Run sensitivity analysis before estimation to triage which parameters to fit vs fix. Parameters with first-order Sobol index S_i < 0.05 (explain < 5% of output variance) are strong candidates for fixing at literature values. This reduces the estimation burden and improves identifiability of the remaining parameters. See Chapter 20 for full methodology.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
13  Model Building and Diagnostics

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
text:
13.11 The Estimation Ladder
PK/PD parameter estimation forms a ladder of increasing complexity and information:









Rung
Method
R Function
Use When




1
OLS (linearized)
lm()
Quick initial estimates; log-transform C vs t for k_e


2
WLS
lm(weights = ...)
Heteroscedastic residuals (variance not constant)


3
NLS
nls() / nlsLM()
Nonlinear model with additive error on natural scale


4
MLE
stats4::mle()
Need \sigma, asymptotic CIs, non-normal errors


5
Bayesian
Stan / brms
Priors needed, full posterior uncertainty, sparse data



Each rung costs more computation but reduces bias (OLS linearization bias) or adds information (uncertainty quantification in Bayesian). Start low on the ladder for exploration, then climb as needed.

13.11.1 Exercises

Compare a one- vs two-compartment model using AIC and BIC. Which is preferred?
Create the four essential goodness-of-fit plots for a fitted model. What patterns indicate a good fit?
Generate a VPC for a PK model. Interpret what it means if >10% of observed points fall outside the 90% PI.
What does a funnel shape in CWRES vs PRED indicate about your residual error model?
Use bootstrap to compute 95% CI for CL from a population PK model.



13.11.2 Common Mistakes

Relying only on OFV: Always check GOF plots, parameter precision, and scientific plausibility
Ignoring correlated residuals: Time series structure in residuals suggests model misspecification
VPC with too few simulations: Use at least 500–1000 simulations
Over-interpreting small OFV drops: ΔOFV < 3.84 may be noise, not signal
Not checking parameter identifiability: High RSE (> 50%) or correlations > 0.95 are red flags



13.11.3 Key Takeaway
Model evaluation is as important as model fitting. Use a combination of OFV/AIC, goodness-of-fit plots, VPC, and bootstrap to build confidence in your model. A good model is not just statistically adequate — it must also be scientifically plausible and practically useful.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
13  Model Building and Diagnostics

title: 13  Model Building and Diagnostics
section: 13.12 Visual Predictive Checks: vpc, tidyvpc & xpose
text:
13.12 Visual Predictive Checks: vpc, tidyvpc & xpose
A VPC compares observed data percentiles to simulated percentiles from the model — the gold standard for evaluating predictive performance.

13.12.1 Classic Binned VPC with vpc


Code
library(vpc)
library(tidyvpc)
library(ggplot2)

# Simulate data
if (!exists("n_subj")) n_subj <- 24

# Simulate observed + simulated data for VPC
set.seed(4567)
n_sim <- 200
sim_data <- data.frame()
obs_data <- data.frame()

for (s in 1:n_subj) {
  # Simulate model predictions
  CL_s <- 5 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.3))
  V_s  <- 80 * exp(rnorm(1, 0, 0.25))
  ka_s <- 1.2
  t_s  <- sort(c(0.5, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24))

  conc_pred <- (100 / V_s) * (ka_s / (ka_s - CL_s/V_s)) *
    (exp(-(CL_s/V_s) * t_s) - exp(-ka_s * t_s))

  obs_data <- rbind(obs_data,
    data.frame(ID = s, TIME = t_s,
               DV = pmax(conc_pred + rnorm(length(t_s), 0, 0.5), 0.01),
               PRED = conc_pred))

  # Simulated replicates
  for (r in 1:n_sim) {
    sim_data <- rbind(sim_data,
      data.frame(ID = s, SIM = r, TIME = t_s,
                 DV = pmax(conc_pred + rnorm(length(t_s), 0, 0.5), 0.01)))
  }
}

# Binned VPC
vpc_bin <- tryCatch(
  vpc(sim = sim_data, obs = obs_data,
      stratify = ~ TIME,
      bins = c(0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, 24),
      vpc_theme = create_vpc_theme(list(
        obs_color  = "#0A1628",
        sim_pi_fill = "#00A8B0",
        sim_pi_alpha = 0.2
      ))),
  error = function(e) { message("vpc package failed: ", e$message); NULL }
)

# Binless VPC (AQR) with tidyvpc
vpc_binless <- tryCatch({
  observed(obs_data, x = TIME, y = DV) |>
  simulated(sim_data, y = DV) |>
  censoring(blq = 0.05, lloq = 0.05) |>
  binless() |>
  vpcstats(vpc_type = "continuous")
}, error = function(e) { message("tidyvpc failed: ", e$message); NULL })

# Plot
if (!is.null(vpc_bin)) plot(vpc_bin) + ggtitle("Binned VPC")
if (!is.null(vpc_binless)) plot(vpc_binless) + ggtitle("Binless VPC (AQR)")




13.12.2 xpose Diagnostic Grid
The xpose package provides a comprehensive suite of model diagnostics for NONMEM and nlmixr2 models:


Code
library(xpose)
library(xpose.nlmixr2)

if (!exists("fit_nlmixr") || is.null(fit_nlmixr)) {
  message("No nlmixr2 fit available — skipping xpose diagnostic grid.")
} else {
  xpdb <- xpose_data(fit_nlmixr)

  grid_plots <- list(
    res_vs_time(xpdb),
    res_vs_pred(xpdb),
    ind_plots(xpdb, nrow = 3, ncol = 3),
    dv_vs_pred(xpdb)
  )

  patchwork::wrap_plots(grid_plots[1:2], ncol = 2) |>
    print()
  patchwork::wrap_plots(grid_plots[3:4], ncol = 1) |>
    print()
}










Diagnostic
What to Look For
Red Flags




CWRES vs TIME
No trend, centered at 0
U-shape, time trend


CWRES vs PRED
Random scatter
Funnel shape (heteroscedasticity)


DV vs PRED
Points along identity line
Systematic bias at extremes


VPC
Observed within simulation bands
>10% outside 95% CI

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
Simulate dosing regimens for multiple-dose scenarios
Predict accumulation and steady-state concentrations
Select optimal dosing intervals based on PK/PD targets
Compute probability of target attainment (PTA)
Apply therapeutic drug monitoring concepts

crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.2 Why Simulate?
Clinical trial simulation allows you to explore “what-if” scenarios without exposing patients to risk:

Dose selection: What dose achieves the desired exposure?
Dosing interval: How often should the drug be given?
Special populations: How should we dose in renal impairment?
Drug interactions: What happens when co-administered with an inhibitor?
Missed doses: What if a patient misses a dose?
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.3 Multiple Dosing and Accumulation

14.3.1 The Accumulation Principle
With repeated dosing at interval \tau, drug accumulates until the amount eliminated during each interval equals the dose:
R_{acc} = \frac{1}{1 - e^{-k_e \tau}}


Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulate multiple-dose IV bolus
mod_iv <- rxode2({
  d/dt(central) <- -ke * central
  conc <- central / V
})

ke <- 0.3; V <- 10; tau <- 6  # q6h dosing
n_doses <- 10
dose_times <- seq(0, (n_doses - 1) * tau, by = tau)
event <- et(time = dose_times, amt = rep(100, n_doses))
times <- seq(0, n_doses * tau, by = 0.05)

sim_multi <- rxSolve(mod_iv, c(ke = ke, V = V), event %>% et(times))

# Compute accumulation factor
R_acc <- 1 / (1 - exp(-ke * tau))

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_multi), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(title = "Multiple IV Bolus Dosing (q6h)",
       subtitle = paste0("Accumulation factor = ", round(R_acc, 2),
                         "; 97% steady state by ~", round(5 * log(2)/ke, 1), " h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()











14.3.2 Peak and Trough at Steady State
For IV bolus at steady state:
C_{ss,max} = \frac{D}{V} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - e^{-k_e \tau}} C_{ss,min} = \frac{D}{V} \cdot \frac{e^{-k_e \tau}}{1 - e^{-k_e \tau}}


Code
# Steady-state peak and trough vs dosing interval
tau_seq <- seq(2, 24, by = 0.5)
D <- 100; V <- 10; ke <- 0.3

Css_max <- (D/V) / (1 - exp(-ke * tau_seq))
Css_min <- (D/V) * exp(-ke * tau_seq) / (1 - exp(-ke * tau_seq))

df_ss <- data.frame(
  tau = rep(tau_seq, 2),
  conc = c(Css_max, Css_min),
  Type = rep(c("Css,max", "Css,min"), each = length(tau_seq))
)

ggplot(df_ss, aes(tau, conc, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Steady-State Peak and Trough vs Dosing Interval",
       x = "Dosing Interval (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.4 Oral Dosing at Steady State

14.4.1 Average Steady-State Concentration
\bar{C}_{ss} = \frac{F \cdot D}{CL \cdot \tau}
This is dose proportional and independent of V and k_a.


Code
# Simulate multiple oral doses
mod_oral <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - (CL/V) * central
  conc <- central / V
})

params <- c(ka = 1.0, CL = 5, V = 50)
tau_oral <- 12
dose_times <- seq(0, 5 * tau_oral, by = tau_oral)
event_oral <- et(time = dose_times, amt = rep(250, length(dose_times)))
times_oral <- seq(0, 6 * tau_oral, by = 0.1)

sim_ss <- rxSolve(mod_oral, params, event_oral %>% et(times_oral))

# Compute average Css
F_bio <- 1.0
Css_avg <- F_bio * 250 / (params["CL"] * tau_oral)

ggplot(as.data.frame(sim_ss), aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = Css_avg, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C44536") +
  annotate("text", x = max(times_oral) * 0.7, y = Css_avg * 0.85,
           label = paste0("Avg Css = ", round(Css_avg, 1), " mg/L"),
           color = "#C44536") +
  labs(title = "Oral Dosing to Steady State (q12h)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.5 Probability of Target Attainment (PTA)
PTA answers: Given variability, what is the probability that a dosing regimen achieves the target?

14.5.1 Target Examples








Target Type
Example
Definition




C_{min} > MIC
Antibiotics
Trough above minimum inhibitory concentration


C_{max} < Toxicity
Safety
Peak below toxicity threshold


AUC/MIC > 125
Fluoroquinolones
Exposure ratio


T > MIC (40–60%)
β-lactams
Time above MIC





Code
# PTA simulation: Probability of Cmin > MIC
set.seed(123)
n_sim <- 5000

# Population PK parameters
pop_CL <- 5.0; omega_CL <- 0.3  # L/h
pop_V  <- 50;  omega_V  <- 0.2  # L

# Regimen
dose <- 500  # mg
tau  <- 8    # hours

# Target
MIC <- 1.0   # mg/L

# Simulate
sim_CL <- pop_CL * exp(rnorm(n_sim, 0, omega_CL))
sim_V  <- pop_V  * exp(rnorm(n_sim, 0, omega_V))
sim_ke <- sim_CL / sim_V

# Cmin at steady state
Cmin_ss <- (dose / sim_V) * exp(-sim_ke * tau) / (1 - exp(-sim_ke * tau))

# PTA
pta <- mean(Cmin_ss > MIC)
cat("Probability of Target Attainment (Cmin > MIC):", round(pta * 100, 1), "%\n")


Probability of Target Attainment (Cmin > MIC): 100 %




Code
# Visualize PTA distribution
ggplot(data.frame(Cmin = Cmin_ss), aes(Cmin)) +
  geom_histogram(fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.7, bins = 50) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = MIC, color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = MIC * 1.5, y = 200,
           label = paste0("PTA = ", round(pta * 100, 1), "%\n",
                          "(Cmin > MIC = ", MIC, " mg/L)"),
           color = "#C44536", hjust = 0) +
  labs(title = "Probability of Target Attainment",
       subtitle = "Distribution of steady-state Cmin across 5000 simulated subjects",
       x = "Cmin,ss (mg/L)", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.6 Dose Optimization by Target

14.6.1 PTA vs Dose


Code
# PTA across dose range
dose_range <- seq(100, 2000, by = 50)
pta_by_dose <- sapply(dose_range, function(d) {
  Cmin_ss <- (d / sim_V) * exp(-sim_ke * tau) / (1 - exp(-sim_ke * tau))
  mean(Cmin_ss > MIC)
})

df_pta <- data.frame(Dose = dose_range, PTA = pta_by_dose)

ggplot(df_pta, aes(Dose, PTA)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.9, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C44536", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = dose_range[which.min(abs(pta_by_dose - 0.9))],
             linetype = "dashed", color = "#D4A843", alpha = 0.7) +
  labs(title = "PTA vs Dose",
       subtitle = "Dose needed for 90% PTA",
       x = "Dose (mg)", y = "Probability of Target Attainment") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.7 Therapeutic Drug Monitoring (TDM)

14.7.1 Bayesian Dose Adjustment
After measuring a drug concentration in a patient, Bayesian methods combine the population prior with the individual observation to compute a posterior:
P(\theta_i \mid C_{obs}) \propto P(C_{obs} \mid \theta_i) \cdot P(\theta_i)


Code
# Simplified Bayesian TDM example
# Prior: CL ~ lognormal(log(5), 0.3)
# Observation: single concentration = 8 mg/L at t = 2h after 500 mg IV

# Prior density
CL_seq <- seq(1, 20, length.out = 200)
prior_CL <- dlnorm(CL_seq, log(5), 0.3)

# Likelihood (simplified: assuming V=50 fixed)
V_fixed <- 50
conc_obs <- 8; t_obs <- 2; dose <- 500
likelihood <- dnorm(conc_obs,
                    mean = (dose / V_fixed) * exp(-(CL_seq / V_fixed) * t_obs),
                    sd = 1.0)

# Posterior (unnormalized)
posterior_CL <- prior_CL * likelihood
posterior_CL <- posterior_CL / sum(posterior_CL) / diff(CL_seq[1:2])

df_tdm <- data.frame(
  CL = rep(CL_seq, 2),
  Density = c(prior_CL, posterior_CL),
  Distribution = rep(c("Prior", "Posterior"), each = length(CL_seq))
)

ggplot(df_tdm, aes(CL, Density, color = Distribution)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Bayesian TDM: Prior to Posterior Update",
       subtitle = paste0("Observed conc = ", conc_obs, " mg/L at t = ", t_obs, " h"),
       x = "Clearance (L/h)", y = "Density") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.8 Dose Individualization

14.8.1 Loading Dose
When rapid attainment of therapeutic concentrations is needed:
D_{loading} = \frac{C_{target} \cdot V}{F}


14.8.2 Maintenance Dose
D_{maintenance} = \frac{C_{target} \cdot CL \cdot \tau}{F}


Code
# Dose individualization example
calculate_dose <- function(target_conc, CL, V, tau, F = 1) {
  loading  <- target_conc * V / F
  maintenance <- target_conc * CL * tau / F

  data.frame(
    Parameter = c("Loading Dose", "Maintenance Dose", "Dosing Interval", "Target Css"),
    Value = c(paste0(round(loading, 0), " mg"),
              paste0(round(maintenance, 0), " mg"),
              paste0(tau, " h"),
              paste0(target_conc, " mg/L"))
  )
}

# Example: 70 kg patient with CL=5 L/h, V=50 L
calculate_dose(target_conc = 15, CL = 5, V = 50, tau = 8)


         Parameter   Value
1     Loading Dose  750 mg
2 Maintenance Dose  600 mg
3  Dosing Interval     8 h
4       Target Css 15 mg/L
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
14  Simulation and Dose Optimization

title: 14  Simulation and Dose Optimization
text:
14.9 Monte Carlo Population Simulation
When you need to explore how physiological variability affects drug exposure — but don’t yet have data to fit a full NLME model — Monte Carlo population simulation provides a lightweight alternative.

14.9.1 The Pattern

Sample PK parameters from log-normal distributions for N virtual subjects
Simulate each subject’s concentration-time profile
Aggregate results (e.g., compute percentiles of AUC, C_{max})

This is distinct from full PopPK: distribution parameters are specified from literature rather than estimated from data.


Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# One-compartment oral model
mod_oral <- rxode2({
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - (CL/V) * central
  conc <- central / V
})

set.seed(42)
n_virtual <- 200

# Sample parameters from literature-based distributions
# CL ~ LogNormal(8, 0.35), V ~ LogNormal(65, 0.25), ka ~ LogNormal(1.2, 0.35)
pop_sim <- data.frame(
  ID = 1:n_virtual,
  CL = rlnorm(n_virtual, log(8), 0.35),
  V  = rlnorm(n_virtual, log(65), 0.25),
  ka = rlnorm(n_virtual, log(1.2), 0.35)
)

# Simulate each subject
times <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.5)

sim_monte_carlo <- lapply(1:n_virtual, function(i) {
  params <- c(ka = pop_sim$ka[i], CL = pop_sim$CL[i], V = pop_sim$V[i])
  event <- et(time = 0, amt = 750)
  sim <- rxSolve(mod_oral, params, event %>% et(times))
  df <- as.data.frame(sim)
  df$ID <- i
  df
})

df_mc <- bind_rows(sim_monte_carlo)

# Compute population percentiles
mc_summary <- df_mc %>%
  group_by(time) %>%
  summarise(
    p05 = quantile(conc, 0.05),
    p25 = quantile(conc, 0.25),
    p50 = quantile(conc, 0.50),
    p75 = quantile(conc, 0.75),
    p95 = quantile(conc, 0.95),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

ggplot() +
  geom_ribbon(data = mc_summary, aes(time, ymin = p05, ymax = p95),
              fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.1) +
  geom_ribbon(data = mc_summary, aes(time, ymin = p25, ymax = p75),
              fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.2) +
  geom_line(data = mc_summary, aes(time, p50),
            color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Monte Carlo Population Simulation",
       subtitle = paste0(n_virtual, " virtual subjects; dark band: IQR, light band: 90% PI"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  theme_minimal()











14.9.2 When to Use This vs Full NLME








Scenario
Monte Carlo
Full NLME




No observed data yet (trial planning)
✓ Fast, literature-based
✗ Nothing to fit


Rich sampling, single subject
✗ Unnecessary
✗ Overkill — use NLS


Sparse data, many subjects
✗ Distributions are guesswork
✓ Estimate \omega, \sigma from data


Exploring “what-if” sensitivity to BSV magnitude
✓ Vary \omega and observe impact
✗ Requires re-fitting





14.9.3 Ground Your Distributions
Monte Carlo population simulation is fast and useful for trial/sampling-design decisions, but the distribution parameters (“arbitrary for demonstration”) should be grounded in literature or existing PopPK estimates before use in real design decisions.



14.9.4 Exercises

Compute the accumulation factor for a drug with t_{1/2} = 12 h dosed q12h, q24h, and q48h.
Simulate oral dosing to steady state and compute C_{ss,avg}, C_{ss,max}, and C_{ss,min}.
Perform a PTA analysis: target AUC/MIC > 125, MIC = 2 mg/L, dose = 500 mg q12h. Vary CL from 3 to 10 L/h.
A patient has CL = 3 L/h (low clearance). Adjust the maintenance dose to achieve the same C_{ss,avg} as a typical patient with CL = 5 L/h.
Simulate a loading dose + maintenance regimen and compare to maintenance-only in time to reach therapeutic range.



14.9.5 Common Mistakes

Forgetting that time to steady state depends on t_{1/2}, not dosing interval
Using C_{avg} when C_{min} or C_{max} is the actual target
Assuming PTA from limited simulations without checking convergence
Not accounting for between-occasion variability in TDM
Using loading dose without checking for toxicity at peak



14.9.6 Key Takeaway
Simulation is the bridge from PK/PD models to clinical decisions. Master accumulation, steady state, PTA, and TDM concepts. The ultimate goal is to select the right dose for the right patient at the right time — model-informed precision dosing.

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.2 What Is Exposure-Response Analysis?
Exposure-response (E-R) analysis relates a measure of drug exposure (e.g., AUC, C_{avg}, C_{trough}) to a clinical outcome (efficacy or safety). It answers:

Is higher exposure associated with better efficacy?
Is higher exposure associated with more toxicity?
What exposure range provides the optimal benefit-risk balance?
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.3 Types of Clinical Endpoints








Endpoint Type
Example
Statistical Model




Continuous
Blood pressure change (mmHg)
Linear/Emax regression


Binary
Response (yes/no)
Logistic regression


Categorical
AE grade (0–4)
Proportional odds


Time-to-event
Time to progression/death
Cox PH, parametric survival


Count
Number of seizures
Poisson/negative binomial


Longitudinal
Repeated biomarker measurements
Mixed-effects models
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.4 Continuous Efficacy Endpoints


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulate exposure-response data for a continuous endpoint
set.seed(123)
n_patients <- 150

er_data <- data.frame(
  AUC = rlnorm(n_patients, log(500), 0.4),  # AUC in mg·h/L
  Emax = -15, EC50 = 800, E0 = 0
) %>%
  mutate(
    Effect_true = E0 + (Emax * AUC) / (EC50 + AUC),
    Effect_obs  = Effect_true + rnorm(n(), 0, 3)
  )

# Fit Emax model
fit_er <- nls(Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * AUC) / (EC50 + AUC),
              data = er_data,
              start = list(E0 = 0, Emax = -10, EC50 = 500))
summary(fit_er)



Formula: Effect_obs ~ E0 + (Emax * AUC)/(EC50 + AUC)

Parameters:
      Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)
E0      0.1997     2.1600   0.092    0.926
Emax  -32.7691    36.1808  -0.906    0.367
EC50 2457.5256  4410.6849   0.557    0.578

Residual standard error: 2.796 on 147 degrees of freedom

Number of iterations to convergence: 5 
Achieved convergence tolerance: 9.142e-07




Code
# Plot E-R relationship
auc_pred <- seq(min(er_data$AUC), max(er_data$AUC), length.out = 200)
pred_effect <- predict(fit_er, newdata = data.frame(AUC = auc_pred))

ggplot(er_data, aes(AUC, Effect_obs)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.5, color = "gray50") +
  geom_line(data = data.frame(AUC = auc_pred, Effect = pred_effect),
            aes(AUC, Effect), color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = -5, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 1500, y = -5, label = "Target effect", hjust = 0, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Exposure-Response: Continuous Efficacy Endpoint",
       x = "AUC (mg·h/L)", y = "Change from Baseline (mmHg)") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.5 Binary Endpoints: Response Rate


Code
# Simulate binary response data
er_binary <- er_data %>%
  mutate(
    # Probability of response increases with AUC
    prob_response = plogis(-2 + 0.004 * AUC),
    Response = rbinom(n(), 1, prob_response)
  )

# Logistic regression
fit_logistic <- glm(Response ~ AUC, data = er_binary, family = binomial)
summary(fit_logistic)



Call:
glm(formula = Response ~ AUC, family = binomial, data = er_binary)

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)    
(Intercept) -1.936855   0.562982  -3.440 0.000581 ***
AUC          0.004372   0.001094   3.996 6.46e-05 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 204.71  on 149  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance: 183.24  on 148  degrees of freedom
AIC: 187.24

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4


Code
# Predict response rate vs AUC
auc_seq <- seq(100, 3000, length.out = 200)
pred_prob <- predict(fit_logistic, newdata = data.frame(AUC = auc_seq),
                     type = "response")

ggplot() +
  # Observed (binned)
  geom_point(data = er_binary %>%
    mutate(AUC_bin = cut(AUC, breaks = 5)) %>%
    group_by(AUC_bin) %>%
    summarise(
      AUC_mid = mean(AUC),
      Resp_Rate = mean(Response),
      N = n(),
      .groups = "drop"
    ),
    aes(AUC_mid, Resp_Rate, size = N), color = "#0B6E6B") +
  # Model fit
  geom_line(data = data.frame(AUC = auc_seq, Prob = pred_prob),
            aes(AUC, Prob), color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1.2) +
  labs(title = "Exposure-Response: Binary Response Endpoint",
       x = "AUC (mg·h/L)", y = "Probability of Response", size = "N") +
  theme_minimal()

15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.6 Time-to-Event Analysis


Code
library(survival)

# Simulate time-to-event data (progression-free survival)
set.seed(456)
n_pfs <- 200

pfs_data <- data.frame(
  ID = 1:n_pfs,
  AUC = rlnorm(n_pfs, log(500), 0.4),
  # Higher AUC = longer PFS
  log_HR = -0.002,  # log hazard ratio per unit AUC
  baseline_hazard = 0.02
) %>%
  mutate(
    # Simulate event times using exponential distribution
    lambda = baseline_hazard * exp(log_HR * AUC),
    Time = rexp(n(), lambda),
    Censor_Time = runif(n(), 3, 24),  # random censoring
    Status = ifelse(Time <= Censor_Time, 1, 0),
    Obs_Time = pmin(Time, Censor_Time)
  )

# Cox proportional hazards model
fit_cox <- coxph(Surv(Obs_Time, Status) ~ AUC, data = pfs_data)
summary(fit_cox)


Call:
coxph(formula = Surv(Obs_Time, Status) ~ AUC, data = pfs_data)

  n= 200, number of events= 25 

         coef exp(coef)  se(coef)      z Pr(>|z|)   
AUC -0.003520  0.996486  0.001234 -2.853  0.00433 **
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

    exp(coef) exp(-coef) lower .95 upper .95
AUC    0.9965      1.004    0.9941    0.9989

Concordance= 0.646  (se = 0.052 )
Likelihood ratio test= 10.38  on 1 df,   p=0.001
Wald test            = 8.14  on 1 df,   p=0.004
Score (logrank) test = 8.68  on 1 df,   p=0.003




Code
# Kaplan-Meier by AUC quartiles
pfs_data <- pfs_data %>%
  mutate(AUC_quartile = factor(ntile(AUC, 4),
                                labels = c("Q1 (Low)", "Q2", "Q3", "Q4 (High)")))

fit_km <- survfit(Surv(Obs_Time, Status) ~ AUC_quartile, data = pfs_data)

library(survminer)
ggsurvplot(fit_km, data = pfs_data,
           palette = c("#0B6E6B", "#2E8B7E", "#D4A843", "#C44536"),
           risk.table = TRUE,
           xlab = "Time (months)", ylab = "Progression-Free Survival",
           title = "PFS by AUC Quartile",
           legend.title = "Exposure")

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.7 Biomarkers and Surrogate Endpoints
A biomarker is a measurable indicator of a biological process. A surrogate endpoint is a biomarker intended to substitute for a clinical endpoint.

15.7.1 Modeling a Biomarker Trajectory


Code
# Simulate longitudinal biomarker data
set.seed(789)
n_subj <- 30
times <- c(0, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24)  # weeks

biomarker_data <- expand.grid(ID = 1:n_subj, Time = times) %>%
  mutate(
    AUC = rep(rlnorm(n_subj, log(500), 0.3), each = length(times)),
    # Biomarker decreases with time, rate depends on AUC
    Biomarker = 100 * exp(-0.1 * exp(log(0.002) + 0.003 * AUC) * Time) +
                rnorm(n(), 0, 5)
  )

ggplot(biomarker_data, aes(Time, Biomarker, group = ID)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.3, color = "#0B6E6B") +
  labs(title = "Biomarker Trajectories by Subject",
       x = "Time (weeks)", y = "Biomarker Level") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.8 Covariate Effects on Exposure-Response


Code
# Forest plot: covariate effects on E-R parameters
covariate_effects <- data.frame(
  Covariate = c("Age > 65", "Weight > 90 kg", "Male sex", "eGFR < 60", "CYP2D6 PM"),
  Effect_on_EC50 = c(1.35, 0.85, 1.10, 1.50, 2.20),
  Lower_CI = c(1.05, 0.65, 0.90, 1.20, 1.60),
  Upper_CI = c(1.75, 1.10, 1.35, 1.90, 3.00)
)

ggplot(covariate_effects, aes(x = Effect_on_EC50, y = reorder(Covariate, Effect_on_EC50))) +
  geom_point(color = "#0B6E6B", size = 3) +
  geom_errorbarh(aes(xmin = Lower_CI, xmax = Upper_CI),
                 height = 0.2, color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "#C44536") +
  labs(title = "Covariate Effects on EC50",
       subtitle = "Values > 1 indicate reduced potency (higher EC50)",
       x = "Fold Change in EC50 (95% CI)", y = "") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.9 Benefit-Risk Assessment
The ultimate goal is to balance efficacy and safety:


Code
# Simulate efficacy and safety vs exposure
exposure_seq <- seq(100, 3000, length.out = 200)

# Efficacy: Emax model (higher = better)
efficacy <- 0 + (80 * exposure_seq) / (800 + exposure_seq)

# Safety (adverse event rate): logistic model
safety <- plogis(-3 + 0.002 * exposure_seq)

# Therapeutic window
df_benefit_risk <- data.frame(
  Exposure = rep(exposure_seq, 2),
  Probability = c(efficacy / 100, safety),
  Outcome = rep(c("Efficacy (Response Rate)", "Safety (AE Rate)"), each = length(exposure_seq))
)

ggplot(df_benefit_risk, aes(Exposure, Probability, color = Outcome)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(500, 1500), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("rect", xmin = 500, xmax = 1500, ymin = 0, ymax = 1,
           fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.05) +
  annotate("text", x = 1000, y = 0.5, label = "Therapeutic\nWindow", size = 4) +
  labs(title = "Benefit-Risk Assessment",
       x = "Drug Exposure (AUC)", y = "Probability") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()











15.9.1 Exercises

Fit an Emax model to relate AUC to a continuous efficacy endpoint. What is the estimated EC_{50}?
Use logistic regression to model response rate vs AUC. Report the odds ratio per 100-unit increase in AUC.
Perform a Cox regression for time-to-event vs AUC. Interpret the hazard ratio.
Plot a forest plot showing covariate effects on EC_{50} from a population E-R analysis.
Determine the therapeutic window where response rate > 60% and AE rate < 10%.



15.9.2 Common Mistakes

Confusing correlation with causation: E-R relationships from observational data may be confounded
Extrapolating beyond observed exposure range: Never predict outside the data
Ignoring time-dependency: Exposure at week 1 may not predict outcome at week 24
Using a single exposure metric: C_{trough}, C_{avg}, and AUC may give different E-R relationships
Not adjusting for baseline: Always include baseline severity as a covariate



15.9.3 Key Takeaway
Exposure-response analysis is the critical step that translates PK/PD modeling into clinical decision-making. Master the relationship between exposure metrics (AUC, C_{trough}) and clinical endpoints (continuous, binary, time-to-event). The ultimate goal is to identify the exposure range that maximizes benefit while minimizing risk.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes

title: 15  Exposure–Response and Clinical Outcomes
text:
15.10 Bayesian Emax Modeling with clinDR
The clinDR package fits Bayesian Emax models using meta-analytic priors from published dose-response studies — ideal when historical information can strengthen a Phase II analysis with limited data.


Code
library(clinDR)

# Simulate Phase II dose-response (6 doses, continuous endpoint)
set.seed(890)
doses_er <- rep(c(0, 10, 25, 50, 100, 200), each = 12)
n_er <- length(doses_er)

true_e0   <- 5
true_emax <- 12
true_ed50 <- 40

response_er <- true_e0 +
  true_emax * doses_er / (doses_er + true_ed50) +
  rnorm(n_er, 0, 3)

er_data <- data.frame(dose = doses_er, response = response_er)

# Fit Bayesian Emax with meta-analytic priors
fit_cdr <- tryCatch(
  fitEmax(response ~ dose, data = er_data, modType = "emax", nSim = 2000),
  error = function(e) {
    message("clinDR fitting failed: ", e$message)
    NULL
  }
)

if (!is.null(fit_cdr)) {
  plot(fit_cdr, xlab = "Dose (mg)", ylab = "Response",
       col = "#00A8B0", pch = 16)
  post_summary <- coef(fit_cdr)
  print(post_summary)
  if ("ed50" %in% names(post_summary)) {
    cat("P(ED50 < 50 mg) =",
        round(mean(fit_cdr$mcmc[, "ed50"] < 50), 3), "\n")
  }
} else {
  message("Bayesian Emax model skipped — install clinDR and JAGS to run.")
}


Key advantage over frequentist Emax: the posterior probability P(ED_{50} < 50 \text{ mg}) directly answers the clinical question “Is the effective dose within a safe range?” — no p-value translation needed.

text:
16.1 Learning Objectives
By the end of this chapter you will be able to:

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
Identify PK/PD changes in special populations (pediatrics, geriatrics, pregnancy, renal/hepatic impairment)
Model the impact of disease states on drug disposition and effect
Analyze drug-drug interaction (DDI) effects on PK parameters
Incorporate pharmacogenomic covariates into PK/PD models
Handle messy, real-world clinical data with missing doses and uncertain sampling times
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.2 Why Special Populations Matter
Standard PK/PD studies are conducted in healthy volunteers or relatively homogeneous patient groups. But real patients are diverse:

Age extremes: Neonates and elderly have altered physiology
Organ impairment: Liver and kidney disease affect clearance
Pregnancy: Physiological changes alter V and CL
Obesity: Dosing by total vs ideal vs adjusted body weight
Genetics: Polymorphisms in drug-metabolizing enzymes and transporters


16.2.1 The Goal
Understand how patient characteristics modify PK/PD so we can individualize dosing — the right dose for the right patient.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.3 Renal Impairment
Renal function is commonly assessed by estimated glomerular filtration rate (eGFR) using the CKD-EPI or MDRD equation. For renally cleared drugs:
CL = CL_{typical} \cdot \left(\frac{eGFR}{90}\right)^{\theta_{renal}}


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulate effect of renal function on clearance
egfr_seq <- seq(10, 120, by = 1)
CL_typical <- 5.0  # L/h at eGFR = 90

# Different drugs have different dependence on renal function
theta_renal_values <- c(0.2, 0.5, 0.8, 1.0)
names(theta_renal_values) <- c("Low dependence", "Moderate",
                                "High dependence", "Fully renal")

df_renal <- expand.grid(eGFR = egfr_seq, Category = names(theta_renal_values)) %>%
  mutate(
    theta = theta_renal_values[Category],
    CL = CL_typical * (eGFR / 90)^theta,
    CL_fraction = CL / CL_typical * 100
  )

ggplot(df_renal, aes(eGFR, CL_fraction, color = Category)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = c(15, 30, 60, 90), linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.3) +
  annotate("text", x = c(15, 30, 60, 90), y = 5,
           label = c("ESRD", "Severe", "Moderate", "Normal"),
           angle = 90, size = 3, vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Effect of Renal Function on Drug Clearance",
       x = "eGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)", y = "Clearance (% of typical)",
       color = "Renal Dependence") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#D4A843", "#C44536", "#0B6E6B", "#1B365D")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.4 Hepatic Impairment
The Child-Pugh score classifies liver disease severity (A, B, C). Hepatic impairment affects:

Metabolic clearance (CYP450 enzyme activity)
Protein binding (albumin synthesis)
First-pass metabolism (portal-systemic shunting)
Biliary excretion



Code
# Dose adjustment by Child-Pugh class
child_pugh <- data.frame(
  Class = c("A (Mild)", "B (Moderate)", "C (Severe)"),
  Albumin = c(">3.5 g/dL", "2.8–3.5 g/dL", "<2.8 g/dL"),
  Bilirubin = c("<2 mg/dL", "2–3 mg/dL", ">3 mg/dL"),
  Typical_CL_Reduction = c("10–30%", "30–60%", "60–90%"),
  Dose_Adjustment = c("Monitor, possible reduction", "Reduce dose",
                       "Contraindicated or major reduction")
)
knitr::kable(child_pugh, caption = "Child-Pugh Classification and Dosing Implications")



Child-Pugh Classification and Dosing Implications









Class
Albumin
Bilirubin
Typical_CL_Reduction
Dose_Adjustment

>3.5 g/dL

crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.5 Pediatric PK
Children are NOT small adults. PK parameters change dramatically with age due to:

Maturation of metabolizing enzymes (CYP450 ontogeny)
Changing body composition (water:fat ratio)
Developing renal function (GFR matures by ~1 year)
Differences in protein binding


16.5.1 Allometric Scaling with Maturation
CL = CL_{adult} \cdot \left(\frac{WT}{70}\right)^{0.75} \cdot \frac{PMA^{\gamma}}{PMA^{\gamma} + PMA_{50}^{\gamma}}
where PMA is post-menstrual age and PMA_{50} is the PMA at 50% mature CL.


Code
# Pediatric CL maturation
pma_seq <- seq(30, 100, by = 1)  # post-menstrual age in weeks
PMA50 <- 48
gamma_mat <- 3.5

maturation <- pma_seq^gamma_mat / (PMA50^gamma_mat + pma_seq^gamma_mat)

df_maturation <- data.frame(PMA = pma_seq, Maturation = maturation)

ggplot(df_maturation, aes(PMA, Maturation)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0B6E6B", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 40, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  annotate("text", x = 40, y = 0.9, label = "Term birth\n(40 weeks)",
           hjust = -0.1, size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Maturation of Drug Clearance with Age",
       subtitle = paste0("PMA50 = ", PMA50, " weeks"),
       x = "Post-Menstrual Age (weeks)", y = "Fraction of Adult Clearance") +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.6 Geriatric PK
Aging affects PK through:

Reduced renal function (GFR declines ~1% per year after 40)
Decreased hepatic mass and blood flow
Increased body fat → increased V_d for lipophilic drugs
Decreased albumin → increased free fraction
Polypharmacy → drug-drug interactions
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.7 Obesity and Body Size Descriptors








Descriptor
Formula
Use




Total body weight (TBW)
Measured
Standard


Ideal body weight (IBW)
45.5 + 2.3 \times (Ht - 60) (kg)
Loading doses of hydrophilic drugs


Adjusted body weight (ABW)
IBW + 0.4 \times (TBW - IBW)
Aminoglycosides


Lean body weight (LBW)
More complex formulas
Most accurate for CL scaling


Body surface area (BSA)
\sqrt{(Ht \times WT)/3600}
Chemotherapy





Code
# Compare body size descriptors
height_in <- 65  # inches
weight_kg <- seq(50, 200, by = 5)

IBW <- 45.5 + 2.3 * (height_in - 60)
ABW <- IBW + 0.4 * (weight_kg - IBW)

df_body <- data.frame(
  TBW = weight_kg,
  IBW_ABW = c(rep(IBW, length(weight_kg)),
              ABW),
  Type = rep(c("IBW (constant)", "ABW (40% adjustment)"),
             each = length(weight_kg))
)

ggplot(df_body, aes(TBW, IBW_ABW, color = Type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.3) +
  labs(title = "Body Size Descriptors vs Total Body Weight",
       x = "Total Body Weight (kg)", y = "Descriptor Value (kg)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.8 Drug-Drug Interactions (DDI)
DDIs can be classified as:








Mechanism
Effect on Victim Drug
Example




CYP inhibition
↓ CL → ↑ AUC
Ketoconazole + midazolam


CYP induction
↑ CL → ↓ AUC
Rifampicin + warfarin


Transporter inhibition
↓ renal/biliary CL
Probenecid + penicillin


Protein binding displacement
↑ free fraction
(Rarely clinically significant)


pH alteration
± absorption
Antacids + ketoconazole





Code
# Simulate DDI: Competitive CYP inhibition
dose <- 100  # mg
V <- 50      # L

# Without inhibitor
CL_base <- 5.0
ke_base <- CL_base / V

# With inhibitor (competitive, increases Km_apparent)
I <- 2.0       # inhibitor concentration (mg/L)
Ki <- 0.5      # inhibition constant (mg/L)
fm <- 0.8      # fraction metabolized by inhibited pathway

CL_inhibited <- CL_base * (1 - fm + fm / (1 + I/Ki))

time <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)
conc_base <- (dose/V) * exp(-ke_base * time)
conc_inh   <- (dose/V) * exp(-(CL_inhibited/V) * time)

df_ddi <- data.frame(
  time = rep(time, 2),
  conc = c(conc_base, conc_inh),
  Scenario = rep(c("Without Inhibitor", "With Inhibitor"), each = length(time))
)

ggplot(df_ddi, aes(time, conc, color = Scenario)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Drug-Drug Interaction: CYP Inhibition",
       subtitle = paste0("CL reduced from ", CL_base, " to ", round(CL_inhibited, 1), " L/h"),
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L)") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536")) +
  theme_minimal()










16.8.1 Enzyme Induction
While inhibition reduces CL, enzyme induction increases it over time by upregulating metabolic enzymes (via PXR/CAR nuclear receptors). The enzyme level E(t) evolves slowly:
\frac{dE}{dt} = k_{syn} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{I_{max} \cdot C(t)}{IC_{50} + C(t)}\right) - k_{deg} \cdot E
At steady state: E_{ss} = \frac{k_{syn}}{k_{deg}} \cdot \left(1 + \frac{I_{max} \cdot C_{ss}}{IC_{50} + C_{ss}}\right)
Autoinduction occurs when a drug induces its own metabolism — CL rises and steady-state concentrations decline over days to weeks. This is the classic pattern for rifampicin, carbamazepine, and St. John’s Wort.


Code
# Simulate autoinduction
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

mod_autoind <- rxode2({
  # Clearance depends on enzyme level
  CL <- CL_base * enzyme
  d/dt(depot)   <- -ka * depot
  d/dt(central) <-  ka * depot - (CL/V) * central
  # Enzyme level: induction by drug concentration
  d/dt(enzyme)  <- ksyn * (1 + Imax * central/V / (IC50 + central/V)) - kdeg * enzyme
  conc <- central / V
})

params_ai <- c(ka = 1.2, V = 62, CL_base = 7.5,
               ksyn = 0.04, kdeg = 0.04, Imax = 3, IC50 = 3)

# Initial conditions: enzyme(0) = 1 (baseline = ksyn/kdeg)
inits_ai <- c(depot = 0, central = 0, enzyme = 1)

times_ai <- seq(0, 336, by = 1)  # 14 days
event_ai <- et(time = seq(0, 336, 24), amt = rep(400, 15))

sim_ai <- rxSolve(mod_autoind, params_ai, events = event_ai %>% et(times_ai), 
                   inits = inits_ai)
df_ai <- as.data.frame(sim_ai)

ggplot(df_ai, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = conc, color = "Concentration"), linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_line(aes(y = enzyme * 5, color = "Enzyme Level (×5)"), linewidth = 1) +
  labs(title = "Autoinduction: CL Increases Over Time",
       subtitle = "Steady-state concentrations decline as enzyme is induced",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Value", color = "") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#C44536", "#0B6E6B")) +
  theme_minimal()











16.8.2 DDI PD Interactions: Synergy, Antagonism, and Bliss Independence
When two drugs interact pharmacodynamically (not just PK), we need models for their combined effect.
Bliss Independence (the no-interaction reference):
E_{AB} = E_A + E_B - E_A \cdot E_B
where effects are expressed as fractions (0–1).
Synergy (combined effect exceeds expectation):
E_{AB} = E_{Bliss} + \gamma \cdot E_{Bliss} \cdot (1 - E_{Bliss}), \quad \gamma > 0
Antagonism (combined effect below expectation):
E_{AB} = E_A \cdot E_B \quad \text{(multiplicative dampening)}



Observation vs Bliss
Classification




E_{AB} > Bliss prediction
Synergy (\gamma > 0)


E_{AB} < Bliss prediction
Antagonism


E_{AB} \approx Bliss prediction
No interaction





16.8.3 Combined Clearance: Mechanistic vs Empirical
When DDI data includes enzyme-kinetic parameters (K_i):
CL_A = \frac{V_{max,A}}{K_{m,A} \cdot (1 + C_B/K_i) + C_A}
Without mechanistic data, fall back to additive (CL_A + CL_B) or multiplicative forms — but treat these as approximations, not mechanism.
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.9 Pharmacogenomics
Genetic polymorphisms in drug-metabolizing enzymes and transporters can profoundly affect PK:



Gene
Phenotype
PK Effect




CYP2D6
Poor metabolizer (PM)
↓ CL, ↑ AUC (5–10×)


CYP2C19
PM / IM / EM / UM
Variable CL


TPMT
Low activity
Risk of myelosuppression with thiopurines


UGT1A1
Reduced activity
↑ irinotecan toxicity


SLCO1B1
Reduced transport
↑ statin exposure
crumbs: Part IV — Population Modeling and Clinical Application
16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity

title: 16  Special Populations and Real-World Complexity
text:
16.10 Handling Messy Clinical Data
Real-world PK data is rarely clean. Common issues and solutions:


Code
# Handling common data quality issues
library(dplyr)

# Example: messy PK data
messy_data <- data.frame(
  ID = c(1, 1, 1, "2", "2", "2", 3, 3, NA_character_),
  TIME = c(0, 2, "MISSING", 0, 1, 4, "PRE", "2h", 6),
  CONC = c("BLQ", 5.2, 2.1, 0, ">100", 8.5, NA, 3.7, 1.2),
  DOSE = c("100mg", "", "", "200mg", "", "", "50 mg", "", ""),
  stringsAsFactors = FALSE
)

cat("Raw messy data:\n")


Raw messy data:


Code
print(messy_data)


    ID    TIME CONC  DOSE
1    1       0  BLQ 100mg
2    1       2  5.2      
3    1 MISSING  2.1      
4    2       0    0 200mg
5    2       1 >100      
6    2       4  8.5      
7    3     PRE <NA> 50 mg
8    3      2h  3.7      
9 <NA>       6  1.2      


Code
# Clean it up
clean_data <- messy_data %>%
  # Convert ID to numeric (NA for non-numeric)
  mutate(ID = as.numeric(ID)) %>%
  # Filter out rows with no ID
  filter(!is.na(ID)) %>%
  # Clean time: remove non-numeric characters
  mutate(TIME = as.numeric(gsub("[^0-9.]", "", TIME))) %>%
  # Handle BLOQ (set to LLOQ/2 = 0.05)
  mutate(CONC = case_when(
    CONC == "BLQ" ~ 0.05,
    grepl(">", CONC) ~ as.numeric(gsub(">", "", CONC)),
    TRUE ~ as.numeric(CONC)
  )) %>%
  # Forward-fill dose within subject
  mutate(DOSE = as.numeric(gsub("mg", "", DOSE))) %>%
  group_by(ID) %>%
  tidyr::fill(DOSE, .direction = "down") %>%
  ungroup()

cat("\nCleaned data:\n")



Cleaned data:


Code
print(clean_data)


# A tibble: 8 × 4
     ID  TIME   CONC  DOSE
  <dbl> <dbl>  <dbl> <dbl>
1     1     0   0.05   100
2     1     2   5.2    100
3     1    NA   2.1    100
4     2     0   0      200
5     2     1 100      200
6     2     4   8.5    200
7     3    NA  NA       50
8     3     2   3.7     50




16.10.1 Exercises

Adjust a maintenance dose for a patient with eGFR = 30 mL/min. The drug is 80% renally cleared with \theta_{renal} = 0.7, typical CL = 10 L/h.
Compute the dose for an obese patient (TBW = 150 kg, height = 170 cm) using IBW, ABW, and LBW. Compare the results.
Simulate the PK profile of a CYP2D6 substrate in a poor metabolizer (CL reduced by 80%) vs an extensive metabolizer.
Write R code to detect and flag implausible concentration values (e.g., increasing when they should be decreasing).



16.10.2 Common Mistakes

Using adult dosing in children without allometric scaling
Ignoring the free drug hypothesis — protein binding changes in disease states
Using Cockcroft-Gault for eGFR in unstable renal function — it assumes steady state
Assuming DDI magnitude from in vitro data — always verify with clinical data
Using TBW for dosing hydrophilic drugs in obesity — may overdose



16.10.3 Key Takeaway
Patient factors — age, organ function, body size, genetics, and concomitant medications — profoundly affect PK/PD. Dose individualization requires quantifying these effects through covariate modeling. Always consider the patient before the population average.

crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
text:
17.2 What Is PBPK?
Physiologically based pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models describe drug disposition using anatomical and physiological compartments connected by blood flow. Unlike classical compartmental models (which are empirical), PBPK models are mechanistic — they incorporate:

Organ volumes and blood flows
Tissue:plasma partition coefficients
Protein binding in plasma and tissues
Metabolic clearance in specific organs (liver, kidney)

PBPK is widely used for: - First-in-human dose prediction - Pediatric extrapolation - Drug-drug interaction predictions - Formulation development - Special population extrapolation
crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
text: 17.3 PBPK Model Structure
crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
text:
17.4 Mathematical Structure
For each non-eliminating tissue:
V_T \cdot \frac{dC_T}{dt} = Q_T \cdot \left(C_{art} - \frac{C_T}{Kp_T}\right)
For eliminating tissues (liver, kidney):
V_T \cdot \frac{dC_T}{dt} = Q_T \cdot \left(C_{art} - \frac{C_T}{Kp_T}\right) - CL_{int,T} \cdot C_{u,T}
where: - V_T: tissue volume (L) - Q_T: blood flow to tissue (L/h) - C_{art}: arterial concentration - C_T: tissue concentration - Kp_T: tissue:plasma partition coefficient - CL_{int,T}: intrinsic clearance in tissue
crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
section: 17.5 Building a Minimal PBPK Model in R
text:
17.5 Building a Minimal PBPK Model in R


Code
library(rxode2)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simplified PBPK model (lumped tissues: richly perfused + poorly perfused)
mod_pbpk <- rxode2({
  # Concentrations (computed first from compartment amounts)
  C_lu <- A_lu / V_lu
  C_rp <- A_rp / V_rp
  C_pp <- A_pp / V_pp
  C_li <- A_li / V_li

  # Arterial blood (lung output)
  C_art <- C_lu / Kp_lu

  # Venous blood (mixed return from tissues)
  C_ven <- (Q_rp * C_rp/Kp_rp + Q_pp * C_pp/Kp_pp + Q_li * C_li/Kp_li) / QCO

  # Lung
  d/dt(A_lu) <- QCO * (C_ven - C_lu / Kp_lu)

  # Richly perfused tissues (lumped)
  d/dt(A_rp) <- Q_rp * (C_art - C_rp / Kp_rp)

  # Poorly perfused tissues (lumped)
  d/dt(A_pp) <- Q_pp * (C_art - C_pp / Kp_pp)

  # Liver (eliminating organ)
  d/dt(A_li) <- Q_li * (C_art - C_li / Kp_li) - CL_int * (C_li / Kp_li)

  # Output: plasma concentration (approximated as arterial)
  C_plasma <- C_art
})

# Physiological parameters (70 kg human)
QCO <- 5.4 * 60     # Cardiac output: L/h
params_pbpk <- c(
  # Blood flows (L/h)
  QCO = QCO, Q_rp = 0.51 * QCO, Q_pp = 0.19 * QCO, Q_li = 0.25 * QCO,

  # Volumes (L)
  V_lu = 1.0, V_rp = 3.0, V_pp = 40.0, V_li = 1.8,

  # Partition coefficients
  Kp_lu = 1.5, Kp_rp = 2.0, Kp_pp = 0.8, Kp_li = 3.0,

  # Intrinsic clearance (L/h)
  CL_int = 20
)

times_pbpk <- seq(0, 24, by = 0.1)

# IV bolus dose (µg) into the richly perfused/blood pool compartment (A_rp)
event_pbpk <- et(time = 0, amt = 10000, cmt = "A_rp") %>% et(times_pbpk)

sim_pbpk <- rxSolve(mod_pbpk, params_pbpk, event_pbpk)
pbpk_df <- as.data.frame(sim_pbpk)

# Plot plasma and tissue concentrations
ggplot(pbpk_df, aes(time)) +
  geom_line(aes(y = C_plasma, color = "Plasma"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = C_rp / params_pbpk[["Kp_rp"]], color = "Richly Perfused"), linewidth = 1) +
  geom_line(aes(y = C_pp / params_pbpk[["Kp_pp"]], color = "Poorly Perfused"), linewidth = 1) +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Minimal PBPK Model: Tissue Concentration Profiles",
       subtitle = "IV bolus, 70 kg human",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (log scale)",
       color = "Compartment") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#C44536", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
text:
17.6 Tissue Partition Coefficients
Kp values can be predicted from drug physicochemical properties:

17.6.1 Tissue Composition Models
Tissues are composed of water, neutral lipids, and phospholipids. Drug partitioning depends on:

Lipophilicity (logP, logD)
pKa (ionization)
Protein binding in plasma and tissues



Code
# Example: predicted Kp values for a hypothetical drug
Kp_predictions <- data.frame(
  Tissue = c("Lung", "Brain", "Heart", "Liver", "Kidney",
             "Muscle", "Adipose", "Skin", "Bone", "Gut"),
  Kp = c(1.5, 2.8, 1.2, 3.5, 2.0, 0.8, 10.0, 1.5, 0.5, 2.0)
)

ggplot(Kp_predictions, aes(x = reorder(Tissue, Kp), y = Kp)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#0B6E6B", alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Predicted Tissue:Plasma Partition Coefficients (Kp)",
       x = "", y = "Kp (Tissue:Plasma)") +
  theme_minimal()

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
section: 17.7 Scaling from In Vitro to In Vivo
text:
17.7 Scaling from In Vitro to In Vivo

17.7.1 IVIVE (In Vitro to In Vivo Extrapolation)
CL_{int,in\ vivo} = CL_{int,in\ vitro} \cdot \text{Scaling Factor}
Scaling factors depend on: - Microsomal protein per gram of liver - Liver weight - Hepatocyte density


Code
# IVIVE example: Hepatocyte CL_int → In vivo CL
CL_int_hepatocytes <- 5  # µL/min/million cells
hepatocytes_per_g_liver <- 120e6  # cells/g liver
liver_weight_g <- 1500  # g

# Scale to whole liver
CL_int_liver <- CL_int_hepatocytes * hepatocytes_per_g_liver * liver_weight_g
# Convert µL/min to L/h
CL_int_liver_Lh <- CL_int_liver * 60 / 1e6

# Well-stirred liver model
Q_h <- 1500 / 60  # L/h, hepatic blood flow
fu <- 0.3  # fraction unbound in plasma

CL_hepatic <- (Q_h * fu * CL_int_liver_Lh) / (Q_h + fu * CL_int_liver_Lh)

cat("In vitro CL_int (hepatocytes):", CL_int_hepatocytes, "µL/min/10^6 cells\n")


In vitro CL_int (hepatocytes): 5 µL/min/10^6 cells


Code
cat("Scaled CL_int (whole liver):", round(CL_int_liver_Lh, 1), "L/h\n")


Scaled CL_int (whole liver): 5.4e+07 L/h


Code
cat("Predicted in vivo CL (well-stirred):", round(CL_hepatic, 1), "L/h\n")


Predicted in vivo CL (well-stirred): 25 L/h

Criterion

17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling

title: 17  Physiologically Based Pharmacokinetic Modeling
section: 17.9 IVIVE: From In Vitro to In Vivo
text:
17.9 IVIVE: From In Vitro to In Vivo
PBPK models need clearance parameters for each organ. In Vitro-In Vivo Extrapolation (IVIVE) provides these from laboratory measurements — translating microsome or hepatocyte assays into whole-body predictions.

17.9.1 The IVIVE Scaling Chain
The standard 4-step pipeline:
Step 1 — Intrinsic clearance from in vitro kinetics:
CL_{int} = \frac{V_{max}}{K_m}
Measured in microsomes or hepatocytes at sub-saturating concentrations (C \ll K_m).
Step 2 — Scale to whole-organ via the well-stirred model:
CL_{hepatic} = \frac{Q_{hep} \cdot f_u \cdot CL_{int}}{Q_{hep} + f_u \cdot CL_{int}}
where Q_{hep} is hepatic blood flow (~90 L/h in a 70 kg adult) and f_u is the unbound fraction in plasma.
Step 3 — Adjust for protein binding:
CL_{effective} = CL_{hepatic} \cdot f_u
Only unbound drug is available for metabolism.
Step 4 — Feed into PBPK mass-balance ODEs:
V_T \cdot \frac{dC_T}{dt} = Q_T \cdot \left(C_{art} - \frac{C_T}{Kp_T}\right) - CL_{met,T} \cdot C_{u,T}


17.9.2 Flow-Limited vs Capacity-Limited Clearance








Condition
CL_{hepatic} \approx
Implication




f_u \cdot CL_{int} \gg Q_{hep}
Q_{hep} (flow-limited)
CL_{int} errors barely matter — clearance is determined by blood delivery


f_u \cdot CL_{int} \ll Q_{hep}
f_u \cdot CL_{int} (capacity-limited)
CL_{int} errors propagate linearly — accurate in vitro data is critical





17.9.3 Know Your Regime
Before performing IVIVE, check which regime your drug is in. For high-extraction drugs (flow-limited, E \approx 1), CL_{hep} \approx Q_{hep} regardless of CL_{int} precision. For low-extraction drugs (capacity-limited), every error in V_{max} or K_m propagates to the in vivo prediction.


17.9.4 Allometric Scaling for Interspecies Extrapolation
Y = a \cdot \left(\frac{W}{W_{ref}}\right)^b
Default exponents: \beta \approx 0.75 for clearance, \beta \approx 1.0 for volume of distribution. These emerge from allometric principles (Kleiber’s law for metabolic rate) and are the starting point for first-in-human dose prediction.



17.9.5 Exercises

Explain why PBPK models are preferred for first-in-human dose prediction from preclinical data.
Build a minimal PBPK model with 4 tissue compartments. Simulate and compare tissue concentrations.
How does increasing the adipose Kp affect the terminal half-life? Why?
Use IVIVE to predict hepatic clearance from microsomal stability data: CL_{int} = 50 µL/min/mg protein, MPPGL = 40 mg/g, liver weight = 1500 g.



17.9.6 Common Mistakes

Using PBPK when a simple compartmental model suffices: PBPK requires many parameters — if you don’t have them, don’t guess
Ignoring blood:plasma ratio: PBPK models track blood concentrations, not plasma
Forgetting permeability-limited distribution: Not all tissues are perfusion-limited
Over-reliance on predicted Kp values: Validate with observed data whenever possible



17.9.7 Key Takeaway
PBPK models are the most mechanistically detailed PK models, incorporating anatomy, physiology, and drug-specific properties. They excel at extrapolation — predicting PK in untested populations, routes, and DDI scenarios. Use them when mechanistic understanding and extrapolation are needed; use simpler models when they suffice.

18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling

title: 18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling
text:
18.2 Why Bayesian PK/PD?
Bayesian methods are increasingly used in pharmacometrics because they:

Incorporate prior knowledge: Leverage previous studies, preclinical data, or physiological constraints
Handle sparse data naturally: Individual Bayesian posteriors can be stable with 1–2 samples
Quantify uncertainty fully: Posterior distributions show all parameter uncertainty
Enable TDM: Bayesian forecasting for individualized dosing
Avoid asymptotic approximations: Exact finite-sample inference
crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling

title: 18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling
text:
18.3 Bayes’ Theorem in PK/PD
p(\theta \mid y) = \frac{p(y \mid \theta) \cdot p(\theta)}{p(y)} \propto p(y \mid \theta) \cdot p(\theta)
where: - p(\theta): Prior distribution (what we believe about PK parameters before seeing data) - p(y \mid \theta): Likelihood (probability of observed data given parameters) - p(\theta \mid y): Posterior distribution (updated beliefs after seeing data)
crumbs: Part V — Advanced Topics and Applied Practice
18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling

title: 18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling
section: 18.4 A Bayesian PK Example: One-Compartment IV
text:
18.4 A Bayesian PK Example: One-Compartment IV


Code
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# Simulate PK data for one subject
set.seed(42)
dose <- 500  # mg
true_CL <- 5.0  # L/h
true_V  <- 45   # L
true_ke <- true_CL / true_V

sample_times <- c(0.25, 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, 12, 24)
obs_data <- data.frame(
  time = sample_times,
  conc = (dose / true_V) * exp(-true_ke * sample_times)
)
obs_data$conc <- obs_data$conc * exp(rnorm(nrow(obs_data), 0, 0.15))

# Visualize the data
ggplot(obs_data, aes(time, conc)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C44536") +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(title = "Observed PK Data (Single Subject)",
       x = "Time (hours)", y = "Concentration (mg/L, log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()










18.4.1 Prior Distributions
For PK parameters, we typically use log-normal priors because parameters are positive:
CL \sim \text{LogNormal}(\log(4), 0.5) V \sim \text{LogNormal}(\log(40), 0.3) \sigma \sim \text{HalfNormal}(0, 2)


Code
# Visualize priors
CL_seq <- seq(0.5, 20, length.out = 200)
V_seq  <- seq(10, 100, length.out = 200)

prior_CL <- dlnorm(CL_seq, log(4), 0.5)
prior_V  <- dlnorm(V_seq, log(40), 0.3)

df_priors <- bind_rows(
  data.frame(value = CL_seq, density = prior_CL, Parameter = "CL (L/h)"),
  data.frame(value = V_seq,  density = prior_V,  Parameter = "V (L)")
)

ggplot(df_priors, aes(value, density, fill = Parameter)) +
  geom_area(alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_vline(data = data.frame(Parameter = c("CL (L/h)", "V (L)"),
                                true = c(true_CL, true_V)),
             aes(xintercept = true), color = "#C44536", linewidth = 1, linetype = "dashed") +
  annotate("text", x = c(true_CL, true_V), y = max(c(prior_CL, prior_V)) * 0.9,
           label = "True value", color = "#C44536", hjust = -0.2, size = 3) +
  facet_wrap(~Parameter, scales = "free") +
  labs(title = "Prior Distributions for PK Parameters",
       x = "Parameter Value", y = "Density") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("#0B6E6B", "#D4A843")) +
  theme_minimal()

title: 18  Bayesian PK/PD and Advanced Modeling
text:
18.6 Simulating MCMC Results


Code
# Simulate posterior samples (normally we get these from MCMC)
set.seed(123)
n_post <- 4000

posterior_samples <- data.frame(
  CL = rlnorm(n_post, log(true_CL), 0.15),
  V  = rlnorm(n_post, log(true_V), 0.10),
  sigma = abs(rnorm(n_post, 0.2, 0.05))
) %>%
  mutate(ke = CL / V)

# Posterior summary
post_summary <- posterior_samples %>%
  summarise(
    across(everything(),
           list(mean = ~mean(.x), sd = ~sd(.x),
                q025 = ~quantile(.x, 0.025), q975 = ~quantile(.x, 0.975)))
  )

knitr::kable(
  data.frame(
    Parameter = c("CL (L/h)", "V (L)", "ke (1/h)", "sigma"),
    Mean = round(c(mean(posterior_samples$CL), mean(posterior_samples$V),
                   mean(posterior_samples$ke), mean(posterior_samples$sigma)), 2),
    SD = round(c(sd(posterior_samples$CL), sd(posterior_samples$V),
                  sd(posterior_samples$ke), sd(posterior_samples$sigma)), 2),
    `2.5%` = round(c(quantile(posterior_samples$CL, 0.025),
                      quantile(posterior_samples$V, 0.025),
                      quantile(posterior_samples$ke, 0.025),
                      quantile(posterior_samples$sigma, 0.025)), 2),
    `97.5%` = round(c(quantile(posterior_samples$CL, 0.975),
                       quantile(posterior_samples$V, 0.975),
                       quantile(posterior_samples$ke, 0.975),
                       quantile(posterior_samples$sigma, 0.975)), 2)
  ),
  caption = "Posterior Summary (Simulated MCMC)"
)
